The various papers on dataset documentation (Datasheets for Datasets, Model Cards, the various others) cover the data-engineering methodology.

*Specific application domains.* Machine learning has substantial application-specific literature. Computer vision has its own literature (Section 5.3 will address contemporary deep learning for vision). Natural language processing has its own literature (Section 5.3 will address contemporary work). The various other application domains (computational biology, recommendation systems, the various others) have their own substantial literatures.

*Information theory for machine learning.* Cover and Thomas's *Elements of Information Theory* (second edition, 2006) is the foundational text for information theory. MacKay's *Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms* (2003, freely available) provides an integrated treatment of information theory and machine learning that is substantial and unique in the literature.

#### Reference works

Bishop's *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* and Murphy's *Probabilistic Machine Learning* books serve as reference works that practitioners consult throughout their careers.

The various scikit-learn documentation (free) is the authoritative reference for the dominant classical machine learning library. The documentation is unusually well-developed for software documentation and serves as both reference and educational material.

The various deep learning framework documentation (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) constitutes reference for the contemporary infrastructure. PyTorch documentation in particular has become substantial.

Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman's *The Elements of Statistical Learning* serves as reference for classical machine learning.

Boyd and Vandenberghe's *Convex Optimization* serves as reference for the optimization theory.

The various survey papers in machine learning serve as references for specific subfields. The annual reviews and the various subject-specific surveys constitute substantial reference material.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the machine learning mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The frequentist statistical perspective.* The substantial Bayesian orientation of much contemporary machine learning literature obscures the frequentist statistical tradition that has substantial alternative resources. Casella and Berger's *Statistical Inference* and Wasserman's *All of Statistics* provide foundational coverage from a more frequentist perspective. Engaging with this tradition gives perspective on what alternatives to the dominant Bayesian framing might look like.

*The classical pattern recognition tradition.* The substantial literature on pattern recognition that predates the deep learning era — Fukunaga's *Introduction to Statistical Pattern Recognition*, Duda, Hart, and Stork's *Pattern Classification* — covers material that contemporary literature sometimes treats too briefly. Engaging with this tradition provides perspective on what the contemporary work has retained, modified, and replaced.

*The cognitive science and computational neuroscience perspective.* The substantial literature connecting machine learning to cognitive science and to computational neuroscience represents a perspective different from the engineering-dominated mainstream. Marr's *Vision* and the various contemporary works on the relationship between machine learning and cognition (Rumelhart and McClelland's classical PDP work, the various contemporary papers on neural network models of cognition) provide alternative framing. The argument is that machine learning should engage substantially with how natural intelligence works, not only with engineering-driven approaches to producing intelligent behavior.

*The probabilistic programming perspective.* The substantial work on probabilistic programming languages — Pyro, Stan, the various others — represents an alternative direction in probabilistic machine learning. The argument is that probabilistic models should be expressed in dedicated programming languages with appropriate inference machinery, rather than embedded in general-purpose machine learning frameworks. The position has substantial proponents and substantial industrial deployment in specific contexts.

*The skeptical perspective on contemporary ML.* Substantial heterodox work questions the contemporary machine learning mainstream — Marcus and Davis's *Rebooting AI* argues that contemporary deep learning approaches have substantial limitations that the mainstream understates, the various papers from researchers like Bender and others raise substantial concerns about contemporary scaling-driven approaches. Engaging with these critical perspectives gives perspective on the limitations of the dominant paradigm.

*The data-centric perspective.* The data-centric AI movement (substantially associated with Andrew Ng's recent work) argues that the substantial focus on algorithms and models in mainstream ML is misplaced, and that the more consequential engineering attention is to data quality, data labeling, and the various other data-engineering concerns. The position has substantial industrial support and substantial argument behind it.

#### What to skip and why

Most older books on neural networks specifically (the various 1990s and early 2000s neural network books that predated the deep learning era) should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The field has moved substantially, and the appropriate primary references address contemporary deep learning rather than the earlier neural network work.

Most marketing-driven books on machine learning for specific industries should be skipped except as practical references. The genre is uneven, and the canonical academic references are more reliable for foundational learning.

Most books on machine learning that focus exclusively on programming with specific libraries (without substantive engagement with the underlying foundations) should be skipped as primary references. They produce technicians rather than practitioners with the conceptual depth that machine learning actually requires.

Books on machine learning that promise to teach the field "without math" should be approached with substantial skepticism. The mathematical content of machine learning is integral, and treatments that abstract over it produce practitioners who cannot do the things the discipline actually requires.

Most online tutorials on machine learning topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained course materials from major universities, the substantial talks from practitioners, the well-developed framework documentation — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without the depth that serious practice requires.

Books on specific machine learning applications without strong foundational treatment should be supplemented with foundational study rather than relied on as primary references. Application-focused study without foundational preparation produces practitioners limited to the specific applications studied.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary machine learning research are conferences. NeurIPS (the Neural Information Processing Systems conference) is the largest and most prestigious, with substantial proceedings available freely. ICML (International Conference on Machine Learning) and ICLR (International Conference on Learning Representations) are similarly significant, with proceedings freely available. AAAI, IJCAI, and the various more specialized conferences (UAI for uncertainty in AI, AISTATS for AI and statistics, the various others) cover specific subareas. The Journal of Machine Learning Research (JMLR) is the leading journal venue.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue for machine learning research. Most significant machine learning research appears on arXiv before or instead of conference publication, and substantial portions of the contemporary literature exist primarily on arXiv. The appropriate engagement with arXiv requires substantial filtering — the volume is substantial and quality varies — but for practitioners working at the contemporary frontier, arXiv engagement is essentially required.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The Distill publication (now in archived state but with substantial historical content) provided unusually clear contemporary writing on machine learning topics. The Gradient publication continues this tradition. Various practitioner blogs (Sebastian Ruder's blog on NLP, the various blogs from researchers at major industrial labs, the various blogs from prominent academic researchers) provide ongoing perspective.

The various practitioner-focused podcasts (Lex Fridman's podcast where it engages technical content, the Twiml podcast, the various others) provide ongdistreamingoing audio content.

The various machine learning newsletters (The Batch, Import AI, Last Week in AI, the various others) provide curated coverage of contemporary developments.

The various industrial AI lab publications (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, FAIR, Google Research, Microsoft Research) constitute substantial contemporary research literature, often containing work that would have been published in academic conferences in earlier eras.

Public datasets and benchmark documentation constitute substantial contemporary reference. The Papers With Code site catalogs contemporary results across major benchmarks.

| Bishop, *Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts* | Bishop's deep learning treatment | Current canon, entry, depth |

| "ML without math" books | Incomplete coverage | Skip (in favor of mathematically grounded treatments) |
| Application-only ML books without foundations | Limited transferability | Skip (as primary references) |

### A note on contemporary relevance

Machine learning foundations is among the more contemporarily active subjects on the intelligence axis, with several developments substantially reshaping the discipline.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial reorientation of the field around large-scale deep learning, treated in detail in Section 5.2 and Section 5.3. The classical machine learning techniques that dominated the field through the 2000s have substantially been displaced for the most contemporarily prominent problems by deep learning approaches, particularly for problems involving images, text, and other unstructured data. The classical techniques retain importance for specific contexts (tabular data, small-data regimes, problems requiring substantial interpretability), but the substantial contemporary attention has shifted to deep learning. The trajectory suggests continued deep learning dominance, with the foundational material in this section providing the conceptual context for engaging with the contemporary work.

The second major development is the increasing engagement with foundational theory. The substantial empirical successes of contemporary machine learning have substantially outrun the available foundational theory, and the contemporary research includes substantial work on developing theory adequate to the empirical phenomena — work on neural tangent kernels, work on the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent, work on generalization beyond the classical bias-variance framework, work on the mathematics of attention and transformer architectures. The trajectory suggests continued theoretical development, though whether the theory will catch up to the empirical successes remains genuinely uncertain.

The third development is the maturation of methodological discipline. The substantial reproducibility problems that machine learning has experienced over the past decade have produced substantial response: better methodological standards, better dataset documentation, better attention to evaluation pitfalls, the various other forms of methodological maturation. The trajectory suggests continued methodological development, with potential for substantial improvement in the reliability of machine learning research and practice.

The fourth development is the increasing engagement with safety and interpretability concerns. The substantial deployment of machine learning systems in consequential contexts has produced substantial attention to whether and how the systems behave correctly, what they have learned, and how their behavior can be controlled. The trajectory suggests continued growth in safety and interpretability work, treated in Sections 5.6 and 5.7.

The fifth development is the increasing engagement with causal reasoning. The substantial limitations of purely correlational machine learning — particularly when models are deployed in contexts that differ from the contexts in which they were trained — have produced substantial interest in causal approaches to machine learning. The trajectory suggests continued causal-inference development, with potential for substantial influence on how machine learning is approached in consequential applications.

The sixth development is the substantial industrial concentration of machine learning research. Substantial portions of the most influential contemporary machine learning research come from a small number of large industrial AI labs, with substantial implications for the field's character. The trajectory in this area is genuinely uncertain — the role of academic research relative to industrial research, the implications of compute-driven research for the kinds of work that can be done, the substantial ethical questions about industrial AI development — but the contemporary distribution of research activity is unusual in CS history.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of machine learning is robust. The core concerns — generalization, optimization under stochasticity, representation and inductive bias, the finite-data regime — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to machine learning foundations: among the intelligence-axis subjects, this is the one most heavily dependent on substantial mathematical preparation, and the depth of machine learning competence is bounded substantially by the depth of mathematical background. Learners pursuing this specialization should expect to draw substantially on the material in Chapter 7, particularly probability and statistics, linear algebra, and optimization theory. The discipline is unusual among CS subjects in this requirement, and the integration of mathematical and engineering preparation is one of the substantial sources of the discipline's intellectual character. Practitioners who attempt machine learning without the mathematical preparation produce work substantially limited by this gap, and the cost of this limitation has grown as the field has matured.
## 5.2 — Deep Learning

### What it is, properly

Deep learning is the discipline concerned with neural networks of substantial depth — multilayer networks of differentiable functions whose parameters are trained by gradient-based optimization on substantial quantities of data. The subject treats deep neural networks as a substantial computational paradigm with characteristic architectural patterns, characteristic training procedures, characteristic theoretical concerns, and characteristic application domains. The discipline has substantial historical depth — the foundational ideas date from the 1940s and 1950s, with substantial development in the 1980s and 1990s — but its contemporary prominence dates from approximately 2012, when deep learning approaches began producing dramatic empirical improvements over previous methods on substantial benchmark problems, and substantial subsequent development has reshaped the entire intelligence-axis region of computer science.

The conventional account treats deep learning as the practice of training neural networks — choosing an architecture, defining a loss function, running gradient descent on training data, evaluating on test data, deploying trained models. This account captures the surface activity but understates what deep learning has become as an intellectual subject. The conceptual core is the recognition that *substantial computational behavior can be obtained by composing many layers of simple differentiable transformations and training the composition end-to-end on substantial data*, that *the architectures matter substantially — different architectural choices encode different inductive biases that determine what can be learned*, and that *the empirical phenomena of deep learning frequently outrun available theoretical understanding, requiring substantial empirical methodology and substantial humility about what is known versus what is assumed*. These recognitions, individually unremarkable, combine to produce a discipline that has substantial empirical successes, substantial conceptual challenges, and substantial practical implications across virtually every domain of computing.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Architecture design* is the layer concerned with how the computational graphs of deep learning models are structured — convolutional networks for spatial data, recurrent networks for sequential data, transformers for attention-based processing, the various more contemporary architectures (state-space models, mixture-of-experts, the various others). The architectural choices matter substantially: different architectures perform very differently on different problems, and the development of effective architectures is itself a substantial subdiscipline. *Training procedures* is the layer concerned with how networks are actually trained — initialization schemes, optimizers (SGD with momentum, Adam, AdamW, the various others), learning rate schedules, regularization techniques, the various practical concerns. The training procedures matter substantially in practice; networks that fail to train under one procedure may train successfully under another, and the practical knowledge of effective training is substantial. *Loss functions and objectives* is the layer concerned with what networks are actually trained to do — the substantial variety of loss functions for classification, regression, ranking, generation, the various self-supervised objectives, the various contrastive and reinforcement-style objectives. *Theoretical foundations* is the substantial subdiscipline concerned with understanding why deep learning works — the various analyses of optimization in deep networks, the various analyses of generalization, the substantial work on the implicit bias of gradient-based training, the various analyses of expressivity. *Practical infrastructure* is the substantial layer concerned with the engineering of deep learning at scale — frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX), training infrastructure (the various distributed training approaches, the various gradient accumulation and mixed-precision techniques), inference infrastructure (the various optimization techniques for deployment).

The intellectual content of deep learning is distinctive in several ways. The empirical character is unusually strong: substantial portions of what is known about deep learning come from empirical results that lack adequate theoretical explanation. The architectural variety is substantial: deep learning is not a single algorithm but a substantial space of architectures with substantial variation in capability and applicability. The scale dependence is substantial: many phenomena observed at small scale do not generalize to large scale, and many phenomena observed at large scale are not visible at small scale, with substantial implications for how learning and research proceed. The compute requirements are substantial: serious deep learning work requires substantial computational resources, and access to compute has become a substantial determinant of what work can be done.

The reason this account matters is that deep learning has become substantially central to contemporary computing, and the gap between informed deep learning practice and uninformed practice is substantial and increasingly consequential. As deep learning has moved from research curiosity to substantial industrial deployment, the cost of practitioners without conceptual foundation has grown: models deployed without appropriate evaluation, training pipelines that produce nominal results without substantive learning, the various forms of failure that proper foundations would have prevented. The discipline of deep learning as engineering practice differs substantially from deep learning as casual application, and the difference is increasingly visible in the production characteristics of systems built with and without the disciplined approach.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Four problems organize deep learning as a discipline.

**The problem of representation learning.** A central question deep learning addresses is how computational systems can develop useful representations of complex data — images, text, audio, the various other forms of unstructured data — through training rather than through explicit feature engineering. The classical machine learning approach required substantial feature engineering: human experts designed features that captured task-relevant aspects of data, and the machine learning algorithms operated on the engineered features. This approach worked but was limited by the human expert's understanding of which features mattered. Deep learning approaches the problem differently: networks learn representations through training, with intermediate layers developing representations that capture aspects of the data useful for the task at hand. The intellectual content includes the various analyses of what representations emerge in trained networks, the substantial work on how architectural choices shape representations, the substantial work on transfer of representations across tasks (representations learned on one task often prove useful for related tasks), and the substantial work on self-supervised learning where representations are learned without explicit task supervision. The achievement is that representation learning has produced systems that handle complex data without the substantial feature-engineering work that earlier approaches required.

**The problem of optimization at scale.** Training deep networks involves optimizing high-dimensional non-convex objective functions through gradient-based methods, and the success of this procedure is mathematically remarkable. Classical optimization theory predicts that gradient descent on non-convex functions can become trapped in poor local optima, can take exponentially long to find good solutions, can fail in various ways that have been formally characterized. Deep network training routinely succeeds despite these theoretical concerns, finding parameter configurations that perform well on training data and that often generalize well to test data. The discipline of deep learning theory has substantial work attempting to explain this success — analyses of the loss landscape (suggesting that the landscape may be more benign than worst-case analyses predict), analyses of the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent (suggesting that the optimization procedure favors certain solutions over others in ways that aid generalization), analyses of the role of overparameterization (suggesting that having more parameters than training examples may aid optimization rather than hindering it). The intellectual content here is substantial and represents one of the most active areas of contemporary theoretical work in machine learning.

**The problem of generalization in over-parameterized regimes.** Deep networks are typically substantially over-parameterized — they have far more parameters than training examples — and classical statistical learning theory predicts that such models should overfit catastrophically. They do not. Deep networks trained on substantial data routinely generalize well despite having capacity that classical theory says should produce poor generalization. This empirical observation has produced substantial theoretical work attempting to understand it: analyses of double descent (where test error first increases with model size and then decreases, contradicting classical bias-variance trade-off), analyses of the implicit regularization provided by gradient-based optimization, the various refinements of generalization theory that account for the specific structure of deep networks. The intellectual content is substantial, and the gap between classical generalization theory and what is observed empirically remains a substantial open question. The contemporary work on this question has produced substantial new theoretical framework but has not produced theory adequate to the empirical phenomena.

**The problem of scaling.** A substantial empirical phenomenon of deep learning is that performance often improves predictably with scale — with more data, more parameters, more compute — and the various scaling relationships have been characterized empirically (the substantial work on scaling laws, beginning with Hestness et al. and continuing through Kaplan et al. and the various subsequent work). The substantial implication is that deep learning has been substantially driven by scale: the contemporary impressive systems are not fundamentally different from earlier systems in algorithmic terms but operate at substantially larger scale. The intellectual content includes the empirical work characterizing scaling relationships, the theoretical work attempting to explain them, and the substantial engineering work required to actually train models at scale. The contemporary period has been substantially shaped by this scaling phenomenon, and the substantial industrial concentration of deep learning research reflects in part the substantial compute requirements of frontier work.

### Where it sits in the map

Deep learning depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational machine learning material (Section 5.1): substantially everything in machine learning foundations is prerequisite for serious deep learning work. The probability theory, the optimization theory, the statistical learning theory, the methodological discipline — all are required. Practitioners who attempt deep learning without machine learning foundations work at a substantial disadvantage that no amount of practical experience can fully overcome.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): substantial mathematical preparation is foundational. Linear algebra is essential and is used substantially more in deep learning than in classical machine learning. Calculus, particularly multivariable calculus, is foundational for understanding gradient-based optimization. Probability and statistics are foundational throughout. Optimization theory is foundational for understanding training procedures. The mathematical preparation required for serious deep learning work is substantial.

From the systems axis: the engagement is substantial and has grown enormously. Deep learning at scale is substantially a systems engineering problem, and substantial portions of contemporary deep learning practice intersect with HPC (Section 4.8), with cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.5), and with the various other systems-axis subjects. The engineering of training infrastructure for large models is substantial work in its own right, and substantial portions of the contemporary deep learning literature address engineering rather than algorithmic concerns.

From the data axis: the engagement is foundational. Deep learning is fundamentally a data discipline, with the data quality and quantity substantially determining what models can achieve. The intersection with database systems (Section 4.2), with stream processing (Section 4.3), and with the various forms of data engineering is substantial.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: deep learning provides foundation for the contemporary large language model material (Section 5.3) and substantially intersects with reinforcement learning (Section 5.4), with interpretability (Section 5.6), and with AI safety (Section 5.7). Classical AI (Section 5.5) historically preceded deep learning, with substantial conceptual content that contemporary deep learning has substantially absorbed.

The relationship to compilers (Section 4.6) is substantial and active. The compilation of deep learning workloads — for efficient execution on heterogeneous hardware, for inference at scale, for training at the largest scales — has produced substantial new compiler infrastructure (TVM, XLA, IREE, the various others, mentioned in Section 4.6). The intersection is one of the most active research areas at the boundary of compilers and machine learning.

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is squarely on the intelligence axis (definitionally), with substantial engagement on the data axis (deep learning is data-shape-determined to an unusual degree), substantial engagement on the system axis (training and inference at scale are substantial systems engineering problems), and moderate engagement on the computation axis (substantial theoretical content from optimization theory and from generalization theory).

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized deep learning acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat deep learning as a body of techniques to apply.

The first is *fluency with the standard architectures and their applications*. The trained practitioner knows what convolutional networks are, what recurrent networks are, what transformers are, what the various other architectures are, and what each is appropriate for. This architectural fluency includes understanding what inductive biases each architecture encodes, what kinds of data and tasks each is suited to, and what the substantial trade-offs among architectures are. The fluency is acquired through engagement with the substantial architectural literature and through extended practice with implementing and using the various architectures.

The second capacity is *the ability to train deep networks effectively*. The trained practitioner can take a problem and a dataset and train a deep network that performs well, with the substantial practical knowledge required for this — choice of architecture, choice of optimizer, choice of learning rate schedule, choice of regularization, the various other choices that determine whether training succeeds. This capacity is substantially practical and is acquired through substantial practice rather than through theoretical study alone. The substantial gap between practitioners who can train networks effectively and practitioners who cannot is one of the most consequential variations in deep learning competence.

The third capacity is *fluency with the deep learning frameworks*. The trained practitioner is fluent with at least one major deep learning framework (typically PyTorch, sometimes TensorFlow or JAX), can implement architectures from research papers, can debug training problems, can extend frameworks with custom components. This fluency is essential for serious deep learning work and is acquired through extended practice. The frameworks are substantial software systems with substantial complexity, and competence with them requires substantial investment.

The fourth capacity is *the ability to read and implement contemporary deep learning research*. The trained practitioner can read a contemporary deep learning paper, understand what it is doing, evaluate the contribution, and reproduce the work. This skill is essential because the contemporary deep learning literature is substantial and substantial portions of practical work requires implementing or adapting recent research. The skill is acquired through substantial practice and is one of the more durable capacities the discipline produces.

The fifth capacity is *the disposition for empirical methodology*. Deep learning is substantially empirical, with substantial portions of what is known coming from empirical observation rather than theoretical derivation, and competent deep learning practice requires substantial methodological discipline: appropriate experimental design, appropriate baselines, appropriate statistical analysis of results, appropriate skepticism about results that are too good to be true. The methodological discipline is acquired through substantial practice and through engagement with the substantial methodological literature.

The sixth capacity is *the disposition to engage with scale*. Substantial portions of contemporary deep learning are scale-dependent — phenomena visible at scale are not visible at smaller scales, and substantial portions of contemporary practice operate at scales that require substantial engineering investment. The trained practitioner engages with scale as integral to deep learning rather than as incidental, and develops the engineering disposition required to work at scale productively. The capacity transfers across specific applications and remains relevant as scales continue to grow.

The seventh capacity is *appreciation for the empirical limits of theoretical understanding*. Contemporary deep learning works substantially better than the available theory predicts, and competent practice involves recognizing this gap honestly. The trained practitioner does not pretend to understand what is not understood, does not over-extrapolate from theoretical claims to empirical predictions, and engages with the empirical character of the discipline as substantively rather than as transient. This appreciation produces engineering substantially more reliable than the alternative that overstates theoretical confidence.

### How the field sees the world

Deep learning embeds a worldview that is unusually distinctive and that has substantially shaped contemporary intelligence-axis thinking.

The first commitment is *to representation learning over feature engineering*. The deep learning worldview holds that representations should be learned from data rather than engineered by humans, when sufficient data is available to support the learning. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer explicit specification, that treat learned representations with skepticism because their structure is not directly traceable to specifications. The deep learning practitioner takes a different view: for problems where substantial data is available, learned representations consistently outperform engineered ones, and the engineering effort is better directed at architecture design and training procedures than at feature engineering. The disposition has substantial consequences for how problems are approached: rather than asking what features to extract, the deep learning practitioner asks what data and what architecture would support effective representation learning.

The second commitment is *to end-to-end training*. The deep learning worldview holds that the various components of a system should be trained jointly to optimize the overall objective rather than trained separately and combined. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer modular composition, that treat end-to-end training with skepticism because it produces systems whose behavior is not directly traceable to individual components. The deep learning practitioner takes a different view: end-to-end training routinely produces systems that perform substantially better than systems composed of separately trained modules, because the joint training optimizes for the actual objective rather than for proxy objectives that the components were trained on. The disposition has substantial consequences for system design and produces engineering substantially different from the modular alternative.

The third commitment is *to scale as substantive engineering concern*. The deep learning worldview takes scale seriously — recognizing that many phenomena are scale-dependent, that frontier work requires substantial computational resources, that the relationship between scale and capability is empirically substantial. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat scale as configuration parameter rather than as substantive concern. The deep learning practitioner treats scale as integral to the discipline: as something that must be engineered for, that produces qualitatively different behavior at different scales, that determines what work is possible. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the substantial engineering of scale as primary work rather than as auxiliary to algorithmic concerns.

The fourth commitment is *to empirical methodology as primary epistemology*. The deep learning worldview holds that the appropriate way to understand deep learning phenomena is through empirical investigation rather than through theoretical derivation alone. This commitment is in some tension with traditional mathematical disciplines that prefer theoretical understanding, but it reflects the empirical character of contemporary deep learning: substantial portions of what is known are known empirically, and the theoretical work attempts to explain empirical observations rather than predicting them in advance. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat empirical investigation as integral to deep learning work, design experiments carefully, evaluate results rigorously, and update beliefs in response to evidence.

The fifth commitment is *to the recognition that deep learning is not fully understood*. The deep learning worldview takes seriously the substantial gap between empirical successes and theoretical understanding, and the substantial open questions about why deep learning works as well as it does. This commitment runs against marketing-influenced presentations that suggest deep learning is well-understood. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the field's substantial open questions honestly, do not claim more understanding than they have, and contribute to the development of theory rather than treating the existing theory as adequate.

The sixth commitment is *to the integration of architectural design with optimization design*. The deep learning worldview treats the architecture and the training procedure as deeply integrated concerns, with the choice of architecture substantially affecting what training procedures work and the choice of training procedure substantially affecting what architectures train successfully. This commitment runs against the disciplinary separation that some adjacent fields practice. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat architecture and training as continuous concerns, with architectural choices made with awareness of training implications and training choices made with awareness of architectural constraints.

The seventh commitment is *to the recognition that data is substantively determinative*. The deep learning worldview takes data seriously as integral to model behavior, recognizing that what models learn is substantially determined by what data they are trained on, that data quality and composition matter enormously, that the various forms of data bias and contamination produce model behaviors that no algorithmic sophistication can correct. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat data engineering as primary work rather than as preparatory to the more interesting modeling work.

These seven commitments — to representation learning, to end-to-end training, to scale as substantive concern, to empirical methodology, to honest recognition of incomplete understanding, to integrated architecture and optimization, and to data as substantively determinative — distinguish the deep learning worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in deep learning as serious engineering and intellectual practice rather than as application of pre-built techniques.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning deep learning are characteristic, well-documented, and unusually consequential — the discipline's substantial empirical character and substantial scale dependence create many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the framework-as-discipline misconception*. Deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) are substantial pieces of software with substantial APIs, and learners can come to treat learning the framework as the substance of learning deep learning. The result is practitioners who can use frameworks fluently but cannot reason about what they are doing, cannot adapt techniques to non-standard problems, cannot diagnose training failures, cannot evaluate when deep learning is or is not appropriate. The countermeasure is to engage with the underlying concepts seriously: to study the theoretical foundations, to understand what training is actually doing, to read research papers carefully, to recognize that framework fluency is necessary but not sufficient for deep learning competence. Framework knowledge is portable across frameworks, but conceptual depth requires the harder work of engaging with the foundations.

A second pitfall is *the architecture-as-magic trap*. Substantial portions of deep learning practice involve choosing among architectures and configuring them — choosing whether to use a transformer or a CNN, choosing the number of layers and their dimensions, choosing the various other architectural choices. Learners can come to treat architectural choice as a kind of magic, with no reasoning about why specific architectures work for specific problems and no ability to evaluate whether a given architecture is appropriate. The result is practitioners who can replicate standard architectures but cannot adapt them to non-standard problems and cannot evaluate when architectural choices are appropriate. The countermeasure is to engage with architectural design as substantive engineering: to understand what inductive biases each architecture encodes, what problems each is suited to, what the trade-offs among architectures are. The substantial literature on architecture design (the various analyses of why specific architectures work, the various design papers and the various subsequent analyses) provides the conceptual content this engagement requires.

A third pitfall is *the small-scale-only practice*. Deep learning has substantial scale dependence: phenomena visible at scale are not visible at smaller scales, and practical experience at small scale often does not extend to larger scales. Learners working only at small scales (the scales practical for individual practitioners with limited compute) can develop substantial misunderstandings: techniques that appear effective at small scale fail at larger scale, techniques that appear ineffective at small scale prove essential at larger scale, the various phenomena of scale (emergent capabilities, scaling laws, the various others) are not visible. The countermeasure is to engage with the scale-dependent literature seriously even when one cannot replicate it directly, to recognize that small-scale experience is partial, and to maintain humility about what small-scale practice teaches about larger-scale behavior.

A fourth pitfall is *the optimization-as-solved misconception*. The standard deep learning training procedure (typically Adam or AdamW with appropriate learning rate schedule, applied to the transformer or other contemporary architecture) works substantially well for substantial portions of standard problems, and learners can come to treat optimization as a solved problem to be configured rather than understood. In practice, optimization in deep learning has substantial subtleties: the choice of optimizer affects what the network learns, the learning rate schedule substantially affects training dynamics, the various forms of regularization (weight decay, dropout, batch normalization) interact with optimization in non-obvious ways, the various failure modes (vanishing/exploding gradients, mode collapse, the various others) require substantial diagnostic skill to address. The countermeasure is to engage with optimization as substantive engineering: to study the theoretical foundations, to understand what each component of the standard training procedure does, to develop the diagnostic skill required to address training failures.

A fifth pitfall is *the metric-tunnel-vision problem*. Deep learning evaluation typically involves metrics — accuracy, loss, the various task-specific metrics — and learners can come to optimize for metrics without engaging with what the metrics actually measure. The pattern is particularly common in research where benchmark performance is the standard measure of progress: methods that improve benchmark performance are taken as improvements, but the benchmark may not measure what users actually care about, and the apparent improvement may not transfer to real applications. The countermeasure is to treat metrics with substantial skepticism: to ask what each metric actually measures, to consider what failure modes the metric does not detect, to evaluate methods qualitatively as well as quantitatively, to recognize that metric improvements are evidence about methods but not proof of methods' value.

A sixth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied specifically to deep learning. Modern AI tools can produce substantial deep learning code, can answer many deep learning questions, and can guide learners through standard exercises. The cost is unusually high in deep learning because the discipline depends substantially on internalized intuition about what works and what does not, on extensive practical experience with training networks, on diagnostic skill that develops through hands-on engagement with failure modes. Learners who use LLMs to bypass the practical engagement do not develop these capacities, and the gap is substantially visible when problems arise that the tools cannot address. The countermeasure is to do early deep learning work with deliberate engagement, even when LLM assistance could produce working code, and to use the tools as supplements to direct engagement rather than as replacements for it.

A seventh pitfall is *the reproducibility-neglect problem*. Deep learning has substantial documented reproducibility issues, with substantial portions of published results proving difficult or impossible to reproduce reliably. Learners who do not engage with reproducibility seriously can produce work that they believe is correct but that may not be — that depends on specific random seeds, on specific software versions, on specific data preprocessing details that are not adequately documented. The countermeasure is explicit attention to reproducibility: documenting training procedures completely, running experiments multiple times to characterize variation, releasing code and configuration when possible, recognizing that reproducibility is integral to honest engineering rather than optional.

An eighth pitfall is *the underestimation of data work*. Substantial deep learning success depends on data quality, data composition, and the various data engineering concerns, and learners often underestimate this dimension of the work. The pattern is reinforced by the substantial focus on architectures and training procedures in the deep learning literature, with data treated as relatively passive input rather than as substantive engineering concern. The countermeasure is to engage with data work as primary engineering: to understand where data comes from, what biases it might contain, what preprocessing is appropriate, what evaluation data should look like, how to detect data leakage and contamination. The substantial literature on data-centric AI and on dataset documentation addresses this concern, and serious engagement with it produces practitioners substantially more capable than those who treat data as preparation rather than as substance.

A ninth pitfall is *the overfitting-to-the-current-paradigm problem*. The contemporary deep learning paradigm — transformers, large-scale pretraining, gradient descent at scale — is substantially dominant, and learners can come to treat this paradigm as the entire field rather than as one moment in a developing discipline. The result is practitioners who cannot engage with earlier ideas (which may return, as ideas in AI have done before) and cannot evaluate alternatives (which become available as the field continues to evolve). The countermeasure is to engage with the broader history of neural networks and machine learning: to study the earlier neural network work (the 1980s connectionist tradition, the 1990s neural network work, the various other earlier efforts), to engage with the substantial alternatives to deep learning (probabilistic methods, classical machine learning, the various symbolic approaches), and to recognize that the contemporary paradigm is one phase in a longer-developing field rather than its terminus.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on deep learning has shifted substantially over the past decade, reflecting both the field's substantial empirical development and its substantial reorientation around new architectural paradigms. The contemporary literature is unusually rich but also unusually unstable: textbooks become outdated quickly, and substantial portions of the contemporary engagement requires direct contact with research papers rather than through textbook mediation.

#### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text has been Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville's *Deep Learning* (2016, freely available). The book covered the field as it stood at its publication, with substantial mathematical depth and substantial coverage of the major topics through 2016. The book remains valuable for its mathematical foundations and its coverage of pre-transformer deep learning, but it is increasingly dated for contemporary work — substantial developments since 2016 (transformers, large-scale self-supervised learning, the various contemporary architectures) are not adequately covered. The book is appropriate as a foundational reference but should be supplemented with more recent material for contemporary practice.

The contemporary alternative is Bishop and Bishop's *Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts* (2024). The book is a substantial revision of Bishop's earlier work, covering deep learning with substantial mathematical depth and including substantial contemporary material. Its publication date makes it the most current of the major foundational deep learning texts, and for learners beginning serious deep learning study now, it is the appropriate primary text.

For more accessible introduction with substantial practical orientation, Howard and Gugger's *Deep Learning for Coders with fastai and PyTorch* (2020) and the associated fast.ai courses (freely available) provide pedagogically substantial introduction. The approach is substantially practical and bottom-up, in contrast to the more theoretical foundations of Goodfellow et al. and Bishop. The fast.ai materials are particularly appropriate for learners with substantial programming background but limited mathematical preparation; they enable productive engagement with deep learning while building mathematical understanding alongside practice.

For PyTorch specifically (the dominant contemporary framework), the PyTorch documentation and tutorials are unusually well-developed and constitute substantial reference material. Stevens, Antiga, and Viehmann's *Deep Learning with PyTorch* provides framework-focused introduction.

For learners interested in the historical and conceptual development of deep learning, the various survey papers from major figures in the field (LeCun, Bengio, and Hinton's "Deep Learning" survey in Nature, the various other historical surveys) provide substantial context. Schmidhuber's various surveys, while controversial in some specifics, provide unusually thorough historical engagement with the development of neural network ideas.

The reading-order recommendation: Bishop (2024) as the primary foundational text, supplemented with fast.ai materials for accessible practical entry, with Goodfellow et al. consulted as historical reference and for material it covers well. The PyTorch documentation as practical infrastructure reference throughout.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in deep learning branches according to specialization.

*Convolutional networks and computer vision.* For learners pursuing computer vision applications of deep learning, the foundational papers are substantial. LeCun et al.'s LeNet papers founded the field. Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton's AlexNet paper launched the contemporary era. The various subsequent architecture papers (VGG, ResNet, the various others) extended the picture. He et al.'s ResNet paper is particularly important. Szeliski's *Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications* (second edition, 2022, freely available) provides comprehensive coverage of computer vision including the deep learning era. Forsyth and Ponce's *Computer Vision: A Modern Approach* covers similar territory.

*Recurrent networks and the pre-transformer NLP era.* For historical context on recurrent networks, Schmidhuber's various papers and the substantial LSTM literature (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber's foundational paper, the various subsequent work) provide foundation. Karpathy's "The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks" essay (free) provides accessible introduction to the era. The substantial pre-transformer NLP literature has been substantially superseded but remains relevant for understanding what transformers replaced.

*Transformers and contemporary architectures.* Vaswani et al.'s "Attention is All You Need" paper is the foundational transformer paper. The substantial subsequent literature on transformer variants and refinements (the various efficient transformer papers, the various papers on transformer scaling, the various papers on transformer interpretability) constitutes the contemporary research foundation. Phuong and Hutter's "Formal Algorithms for Transformers" paper provides unusually clear technical treatment. The various papers on state-space models (Mamba, the various others) and on mixture-of-experts architectures extend the picture into very contemporary directions. The contemporary large language model literature is treated more substantially in Section 5.3.

*Generative models.* The substantial literature on generative models has multiple traditions. The variational autoencoder literature (Kingma and Welling's foundational paper, the various subsequent work) covers one tradition. The generative adversarial network literature (Goodfellow et al.'s foundational paper, the various subsequent work) covers another. The diffusion model literature (Ho, Jain, and Abbeel's DDPM paper, Song et al.'s score-based papers, the various subsequent work) covers the contemporary dominant approach for image generation. The various papers on the contemporary unification of generative modeling approaches extend the picture.

*Self-supervised learning.* The substantial literature on self-supervised learning has reshaped substantial portions of deep learning. The various papers on contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo, the various others), on masked reconstruction approaches (BERT for NLP, MAE for vision, the various others), and on the various other self-supervised approaches constitute the contemporary literature. Chen et al.'s SimCLR paper and the various BERT-style papers are foundational.

*Optimization and training procedures.* For depth on optimization, beyond the references in Section 5.1, the various papers on Adam (Kingma and Ba), AdamW (Loshchilov and Hutter), the various other contemporary optimizers, the various papers on learning rate schedules (cosine annealing, warmup, the various others), the various papers on initialization (He initialization, Glorot initialization, the various others), and the various papers on normalization (batch normalization, layer normalization, the various others) constitute substantial contemporary literature on training procedures.

*Theoretical foundations of deep learning.* The substantial contemporary theoretical literature on deep learning is rapidly developing. The various papers on neural tangent kernels (Jacot, Gabriel, Hongler's foundational paper, the various subsequent work) provide one theoretical framework. The various papers on the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent provide another. Roberts, Yaida, and Hanin's *The Principles of Deep Learning Theory* (2022, freely available) provides substantial recent treatment of theoretical foundations. The various papers on double descent (Belkin, Hsu, Ma, Mandal's foundational paper, the various subsequent work) cover the generalization-related phenomena.

*Engineering of deep learning at scale.* The substantial contemporary literature on engineering deep learning at scale is essential for serious frontier work. The various papers on distributed training (data parallelism, model parallelism, pipeline parallelism, the various combinations), the various papers on mixed-precision training, the various papers on memory-efficient training, the various papers on gradient checkpointing — all constitute substantial contemporary literature. The various engineering blog posts from major industrial AI labs (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, FAIR, DeepMind) provide substantial practical engineering content.

*Deep learning compilers.* The substantial work on compilers for deep learning (TVM, XLA, IREE, the various others, mentioned in Section 4.6) constitutes substantial contemporary literature. The intersection with compilers and with HPC is increasingly substantial.

*Domain-specific deep learning.* Deep learning has substantial domain-specific literature. The various papers on graph neural networks (Hamilton, Ying, and Leskovec, the various others) cover graph-structured data. The various papers on protein structure prediction (AlphaFold, the various successors) cover the substantial bioinformatics applications. The various papers on deep learning for the sciences (deep learning for physics, for chemistry, for the various scientific domains) cover the broader scientific applications.

#### Reference works

The PyTorch documentation is the authoritative reference for PyTorch and is unusually well-developed.

The Hugging Face documentation and the Transformers library are substantial reference for working with transformer models, particularly large language models.

The various JAX documentation covers the alternative framework that has substantial use in research contexts.

The Papers With Code site provides substantial reference for benchmark results and for paper-to-code mapping.

The various major industrial AI lab publication archives (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, FAIR, Google Research) constitute substantial contemporary research reference.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the deep learning mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective.* Substantial critical engagement with deep learning provides perspective on the dominant paradigm's limitations. Marcus and Davis's *Rebooting AI* makes substantial arguments about the limitations of contemporary deep learning. The various papers from researchers who emphasize symbolic and hybrid approaches (the neuro-symbolic AI literature, the various others) extend the picture. Pearl's various critical writings on deep learning's relationship to causal reasoning are similarly substantial. The arguments are sometimes overstated but contain genuine insight that engagement with only the mainstream literature does not provide.

*The cognitive science perspective.* The connection between deep learning and cognitive science has substantial heterodox literature. Rumelhart and McClelland's *Parallel Distributed Processing* is the foundational text for the connectionist tradition that produced contemporary deep learning, and engaging with it provides substantial conceptual context. The contemporary work connecting deep learning to cognitive science (the various papers on neural network models of cognition, the various papers on the relationship between deep learning representations and cognitive representations) provides ongoing alternative framing.

*The neuroscience perspective.* The relationship between deep learning and biological neural networks is genuinely contested. The substantial literature on this question — the various papers on cortical computation, the various papers on biological plausibility of backpropagation, the various papers on the relationship between deep network representations and cortical representations — provides perspective on what deep learning is and is not. Hassabis et al.'s "Neuroscience-Inspired Artificial Intelligence" provides one accessible synthesis.

*The classical machine learning persistence.* Substantial machine learning practice continues to use classical methods (decision trees, ensemble methods, the various others) rather than deep learning, particularly for tabular data and small-data regimes. The substantial argument behind continuing classical methods is that deep learning's apparent universal applicability is overstated and that classical methods substantially outperform deep learning on substantial problem categories. The various papers comparing deep learning to classical methods on tabular data (the various Kaggle competition analyses, the various academic comparisons) make this argument with substantial empirical support.

*The brittle generalization perspective.* Substantial work has documented the brittleness of deep learning generalization — the various forms of distribution shift, the various adversarial examples, the various out-of-distribution failures. The substantial literature on this question (Goodfellow et al.'s adversarial examples papers, the various distribution shift papers, the various papers on deep learning failures) provides substantial reason for skepticism about deep learning generalization claims.

*The interpretability perspective.* Substantial heterodox engagement with deep learning argues for substantial attention to what models actually learn, rather than only to their outputs. The mechanistic interpretability literature (treated more substantially in Section 5.6) constitutes substantial heterodox correction to mainstream practice that treats models as black boxes evaluated on outputs.

#### What to skip and why

Books on neural networks that predate the contemporary deep learning era (the various 1990s and early 2000s neural network books) should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The field has changed substantially, and the appropriate primary references address the contemporary period.

Marketing-driven books on "AI" that conflate deep learning with broader claims about artificial intelligence should generally be skipped. The genre is uneven, and the canonical academic references are more reliable.

Most "deep learning for X" books, where X is some specific industry or application, should be approached with caution. Some are valuable, but many oversimplify deep learning by abstracting away concerns that serious practice requires.

Books on deep learning that focus exclusively on a single framework without engaging with the underlying concepts should be skipped as primary references. Framework knowledge is necessary but not sufficient; the underlying concepts are foundational.

Books on deep learning that promise quick mastery or that abstract over the mathematical content should be approached with substantial skepticism. Deep learning has genuine mathematical content that serious practice requires engagement with, and treatments that abstract over it produce practitioners who cannot do what the discipline actually requires.

Most online tutorials on deep learning topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — well-maintained framework documentation, the substantial fast.ai materials, the well-structured course materials from major universities, the substantial talks from prominent researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth.

Pre-2020 books on transformer-based models are now substantially dated and should be supplemented with current material. The transformer landscape has changed substantially since 2020.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary deep learning research are conferences. NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR (mentioned in Section 5.1) are the primary venues, with proceedings freely available. The various more specialized venues (CVPR for computer vision, ACL and EMNLP for NLP, the various others) cover specific domain applications.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue for deep learning research, with substantial portions of research appearing on arXiv before or instead of conference publication. The cs.LG, cs.CV, cs.CL, and stat.ML categories cover the major subareas.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The Distill publication (now archived) provided unusually clear contemporary writing on deep learning topics; its archive remains valuable. The Gradient continues this tradition. Andrej Karpathy's various writings and tutorials are consistently valuable. Sebastian Raschka's writings provide ongoing technical content. Lilian Weng's blog posts provide substantial deep technical writing on various topics. Chris Olah's writings (especially the historical Distill articles and the contemporary Anthropic interpretability work) are exceptionally valuable.

The various industrial AI lab blog posts and publications constitute substantial contemporary literature. The Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, FAIR, and Google Research publication archives include substantial research that would have appeared in academic conferences in earlier eras.

The various practitioner-focused YouTube channels provide ongoing video content. Yannic Kilcher's paper reviews provide ongoing coverage of new papers. Andrej Karpathy's various tutorial videos are consistently valuable. The various conference talk recordings (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR talks freely available) provide substantial video content.

The various deep learning newsletters (The Batch from DeepLearning.AI, Import AI from Jack Clark, Last Week in AI, The Algorithmic Bridge, the various others) provide curated coverage of contemporary developments.

The various practitioner-focused podcasts (Lex Fridman's podcast where it engages technical content, Machine Learning Street Talk, Twiml, the various others) provide ongoing audio content.
