| Conference talk recordings (free YouTube) | Video content from primary sources | Current, sampling |
| Yannic Kilcher and ML YouTube channels | Paper review content | Current, sampling |
| ML newsletters (The Batch, Import AI, etc.) | Curated contemporary developments | Current, ongoing |
| Marcus, Davis, *Rebooting AI* | Skeptical perspective | Current, heterodox |
| Rumelhart, McClelland, *Parallel Distributed Processing* | Connectionist foundation | Permanent canon, heterodox, conceptual |
| Hassabis et al., "Neuroscience-Inspired AI" | Neuroscience perspective | Current canon, heterodox |
| Pre-2010 neural network books | Significantly dated | Skip (with exceptions) |
| Pre-2020 transformer books | Substantially dated | Skip (in favor of current sources) |
| "AI" books conflating deep learning with broader claims | Mixed quality | Skip (in favor of foundational sources) |
| Industry-specific deep learning books | Mixed quality | Skip (with caution) |
| Framework-only books without conceptual depth | Incomplete coverage | Skip (in favor of integrated treatments) |
| Math-avoiding deep learning books | Incomplete coverage | Skip (in favor of mathematically grounded treatments) |

### A note on contemporary relevance

Deep learning is the most contemporarily active subject in computer science, with substantial developments substantially reshaping the field on timescales of months rather than years.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial rise of large language models, treated in detail in Section 5.3. The substantial empirical successes of large language models — their substantial general-purpose capabilities, their substantial economic and social implications, the various technical questions they raise — have substantially shaped contemporary deep learning. The trajectory continues to develop, with substantial uncertainty about where it will lead.

The second major development is the substantial diversification of model types beyond pure language models. Multimodal models that handle text, images, audio, and video together; vision-language models with substantial reasoning capability; speech and audio models with substantial generative capability; the various other multimodal directions all substantially extend deep learning beyond the language-only frame. The trajectory suggests continued multimodal development, with substantial implications for what deep learning systems can do.

The third development is the substantial emergence of state-space models and other transformer alternatives. The transformer architecture has been dominant for several years, but substantial work on alternatives (state-space models like Mamba, the various structured state-space variants, the various other architectures) suggests possible architectural diversification. The trajectory in this area is genuinely uncertain — transformers may remain dominant, or substantial transitions may occur — but the contemporary work is substantial.

The fourth development is the substantial maturation of training and inference engineering. The substantial engineering required for training large models has produced substantial new infrastructure, and the substantial engineering required for inference at scale has similarly matured. The trajectory suggests continued engineering development, with substantial implications for who can do frontier work and at what cost.

The fifth development is the substantial growth of mechanistic interpretability research, treated in detail in Section 5.6. The substantial work on understanding what trained models actually do internally — at the level of individual neurons, circuits, and representations — has produced substantial new approaches to deep learning that complement the empirical engineering tradition. The trajectory suggests continued interpretability development, with substantial implications for how deep learning is understood and deployed.

The sixth development is the substantial growth of AI safety research, treated in detail in Section 5.7. The substantial concerns about the safe deployment of increasingly capable AI systems have produced substantial research community focused on safety questions. The trajectory in this area is substantial and ongoing.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of deep learning is robust. The core concerns — representation learning, optimization at scale, generalization in over-parameterized regimes, the relationship between scale and capability — remain as articulated in earlier work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to deep learning: the discipline is in a substantial moment, with substantial empirical successes, substantial economic and social implications, and substantial uncertainty about its future trajectory. Practitioners pursuing this specialization should expect substantial change to continue, should maintain currency through ongoing engagement with contemporary sources, and should recognize that substantial portions of contemporary practice may look substantially different in a decade. The substantial investment in deep learning competence is rewarded by substantial capability and substantial career opportunity, but the discipline is one where the foundational mathematical and conceptual preparation transfers more reliably than the substantial specifics of the contemporary moment. Learners building durable competence should invest in the foundations alongside the contemporary specifics, recognizing that the foundations will remain valuable while the specifics will need ongoing refresh.

## 5.3 — Large Language Models and Foundation Models

### What it is, properly

Large language models and foundation models is the discipline concerned with the contemporary class of substantial deep neural networks that are pretrained on broad data at substantial scale and then adapted — through fine-tuning, prompting, or various other techniques — to a wide range of downstream tasks. The subject treats these models as a substantial new computational paradigm with characteristic engineering challenges, characteristic emergent behaviors, characteristic deployment patterns, and characteristic open questions. The discipline has emerged primarily over the past decade, with the substantial transformer architecture providing the foundational technical substrate (introduced in 2017), the substantial pretraining-then-adapting paradigm establishing itself through models like BERT and GPT (2018-2020), and the substantial contemporary period beginning approximately with GPT-3 (2020) and continuing through the substantial subsequent development of GPT-4, Claude, the various Llama models, the various Gemini models, and the substantial others.

The conventional account treats large language models as "AI chatbots" — systems that produce text in response to prompts, useful for various information and generation tasks. This account captures part of what these systems are but understates substantially what makes them a substantial intellectual subject. The conceptual core is the recognition that *substantial pretraining at scale produces models with substantial general-purpose capability that emerges from the training process rather than being explicitly programmed*, that *the relationship between training procedure and resulting capability is empirically substantial but theoretically incomplete*, that *the engineering of these systems involves substantial technical challenges that smaller-scale machine learning does not surface*, and that *the deployment of these systems raises substantial questions — about capability, about reliability, about safety, about the various societal implications — that the technical community is engaging with actively*. These recognitions reshape how the subject is approached: as a substantial paradigm with substantial intellectual content rather than as a particular kind of product.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Pretraining* is the layer concerned with the substantial training of foundation models on broad data at scale — the data engineering, the training procedures, the architectural choices, the substantial engineering of distributed training. *Adaptation and fine-tuning* is the layer concerned with how pretrained models are adapted to specific tasks — supervised fine-tuning, instruction tuning, the various forms of preference-based fine-tuning (RLHF, DPO, the various others), parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (LoRA, the various others). *Inference and deployment* is the layer concerned with running these models efficiently at scale — quantization, pruning, distillation, the various inference optimization techniques, the substantial systems engineering of serving large models. *Prompting and context engineering* is the layer concerned with how these models are used at inference time — prompt design, in-context learning, the various forms of prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, the various agentic patterns. *Evaluation and characterization* is the substantial layer concerned with measuring what these models can and cannot do — the various benchmarks, the substantial work on model evaluation methodology, the substantial work on characterizing model capabilities and limitations. *Alignment and safety* is the substantial layer concerned with making these models behave usefully and safely — fine-tuning approaches that aim at desired behaviors, the various interpretability approaches, the substantial work on identifying and addressing failure modes. (This last concern is treated more substantially in Section 5.7, but it intersects with the present section throughout.)

The intellectual content of the subject is distinctive in several ways. The empirical character is unusually strong — perhaps the strongest of any contemporary CS subject. Substantial portions of what is known about these models come from empirical investigation that lacks adequate theoretical explanation, and the field operates substantially through empirical methodology rather than theoretical derivation. The scale dependence is substantial in ways that earlier deep learning was not: capabilities that emerge at one scale are not visible at smaller scales, and substantial portions of contemporary practice operate at scales that require substantial industrial resources. The pace of development is unusually rapid, with substantial new results appearing on monthly timescales and the dominant systems changing substantially within timeframes of months to years. The disciplinary breadth is substantial: serious engagement with contemporary foundation models requires fluency with deep learning, with the various forms of NLP and multimodal processing, with substantial systems engineering, with substantial evaluation methodology, with substantial portions of the AI safety literature.

The reason this account matters is that foundation models have become substantially central to contemporary computing and to substantial portions of broader social and economic activity, and the gap between informed engagement with these systems and uninformed engagement is substantial and increasingly consequential. The substantial deployment of foundation models in consequential contexts — medical, legal, educational, financial, the various others — makes the cost of practitioners without conceptual foundation increasingly serious. The discipline of foundation model engineering as serious practice differs substantially from foundation model usage as casual application, and competent work in this area requires substantial preparation that the marketing-influenced presentations often understate.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize large language models and foundation models as a discipline. The fifth problem reflects the field's substantial engagement with concerns that other deep learning subfields engage with less directly.

**The problem of capability emergence from pretraining.** A substantial empirical phenomenon of foundation models is that capabilities emerge from large-scale pretraining that the training procedure was not specifically designed to produce. Models trained on next-token prediction develop substantial general capabilities — reasoning, instruction following, code generation, the various others — that are not directly the training objective. The discipline includes substantial work on understanding this phenomenon: the various analyses of what scaling produces, the substantial work on the relationship between pretraining data and model capabilities, the various theoretical frameworks attempting to explain capability emergence (the various scaling law analyses, the various papers on in-context learning, the various analyses of what mechanisms underlie observed capabilities). The intellectual content is substantial and represents one of the central open questions of contemporary deep learning. The various papers on emergent abilities (Wei et al.'s "Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models" and the various subsequent work) document the phenomenon empirically; the various theoretical analyses attempt to explain it. The current state is that the phenomenon is substantially documented but inadequately understood.

**The problem of adaptation from pretraining to deployment.** A pretrained foundation model is rarely deployed directly; it is typically adapted through fine-tuning, instruction tuning, preference-based fine-tuning, or some combination of these to produce a model suitable for specific deployment. The discipline of adaptation has substantial intellectual content: the various supervised fine-tuning techniques and their implications, the substantial work on instruction tuning (the various papers on Self-Instruct, on instruction-following datasets, on the various adaptations), the substantial work on preference-based fine-tuning (the original RLHF papers from OpenAI and Anthropic, the various subsequent work on Direct Preference Optimization and its variants, the various theoretical analyses), and the substantial work on parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA, the various other adapter approaches). The intellectual content includes both substantial engineering questions (what techniques work, when, with what trade-offs) and substantial conceptual questions (what fine-tuning actually changes about model behavior, what the relationship between pretraining and fine-tuning is, what the various forms of fine-tuning preserve and modify).

**The problem of inference at scale.** Foundation models are typically substantially large — billions to trillions of parameters — and serving them at scale presents substantial engineering challenges. The discipline includes substantial work on inference optimization: quantization (substantially developed over the past several years, with INT8 and INT4 inference now standard), pruning, distillation (the various techniques for training smaller models to mimic larger ones), the various caching strategies (KV cache, prefix caching, the various others), the various decoding optimizations (speculative decoding, the various others). The intersection with systems engineering is substantial: serving large models at scale is substantially a systems engineering problem, with substantial intersection with the cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.5), the HPC engineering (Section 4.8), and the compiler engineering (Section 4.6) covered elsewhere in this document. The various contemporary inference frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, the various others) constitute substantial engineering infrastructure that practitioners working in this area engage with regularly.

**The problem of evaluation and characterization.** Evaluating what foundation models can and cannot do is genuinely difficult. Models exhibit substantial capabilities on standard benchmarks but exhibit substantial failures in real applications; they appear to perform well on tasks but fail on apparently similar tasks for non-obvious reasons; they exhibit capabilities that vary substantially across prompting approaches in ways that complicate evaluation. The discipline includes substantial work on evaluation methodology: the various standard benchmarks (MMLU, BigBench, the various others), the various critiques of benchmark validity, the substantial work on evaluation that better captures real-world deployment performance, the various efforts at adversarial evaluation, the substantial work on evaluating specific capabilities (reasoning, coding, the various others). The intellectual content is substantial because evaluation determines what work is taken seriously, what improvements are recognized, what failures are detected, and inadequate evaluation produces substantial misallocation of attention and effort. The contemporary work on evaluation (including the work from various AI safety organizations on evaluating dangerous capabilities specifically) is among the more important contemporary research directions.

**The problem of alignment and safe behavior.** Foundation models are deployed in substantial contexts, and ensuring they behave usefully and safely in those contexts is substantial engineering work. The discipline includes substantial work on what is sometimes called "alignment": fine-tuning approaches that aim at desired behaviors (helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty), the various techniques for reducing undesired behaviors (refusal of harmful requests, avoidance of various failure modes), the substantial work on adversarial robustness (jailbreaks and the responses to them), the substantial interpretability work (treated more substantially in Section 5.6), the substantial broader safety work (treated more substantially in Section 5.7). The intellectual content is genuine and substantial: the question of how to make models behave reliably and safely is substantially open, and the various contemporary techniques (RLHF, Constitutional AI, the various other approaches) represent partial solutions with known limitations rather than complete solutions to a solved problem. The contemporary engagement with these questions is substantial, with substantial industrial investment and substantial academic attention.

### Where it sits in the map

Large language models and foundation models depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the deep learning material (Section 5.2): substantially everything in deep learning is prerequisite for serious foundation model work. The architectural fluency with transformers, the training procedure expertise, the optimization knowledge, the methodological discipline — all are required. Practitioners who attempt foundation model work without substantial deep learning preparation operate at substantial disadvantage.

From the machine learning foundations material (Section 5.1): the foundational material is similarly required, particularly the probabilistic and statistical foundations, the optimization theory, and the methodological discipline.

From the systems axis: the engagement is substantial and constitutive. Foundation model work is substantially a systems engineering problem at scale, with the engineering of training infrastructure, of inference infrastructure, of the substantial data infrastructure required, all constituting substantial work. The intersection with HPC (Section 4.8) is particularly substantial — training frontier foundation models requires HPC-scale engineering — but the intersection with cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.5), with stream processing (Section 4.3), and with the various other systems-axis subjects is also substantial.

From the data axis: the engagement is foundational. Foundation models are substantially shaped by the data they are trained on, with the substantial pretraining datasets (Common Crawl-derived, the various code datasets, the various other sources) substantially determining what models learn. The substantial work on data filtering, on data composition, on the various forms of data engineering for pretraining constitutes substantial discipline in its own right.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: the relationship to interpretability (Section 5.6) and AI safety (Section 5.7) is substantial and active. Substantial portions of contemporary interpretability work are directed at understanding foundation models specifically; substantial portions of contemporary AI safety work are directed at foundation models specifically. The relationship to reinforcement learning (Section 5.4) is substantial through the RLHF and related work — substantial portions of contemporary alignment work uses reinforcement learning techniques applied to foundation models.

From the broader CS context: the engagement with software engineering is increasingly substantial, as foundation models are increasingly used as substantial components of software systems. The engagement with HCI is substantial, as foundation models substantially reshape what user interfaces can do. The engagement with the broader societal questions — economic, ethical, regulatory — is substantial, though these questions are largely treated outside this chapter (in Appendix D).

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is squarely on the intelligence axis, with substantial engagement across all other axes. The data axis engagement is substantial (foundation models are data-shape-determined to an unusual degree). The system axis engagement is substantial (training and inference at scale are substantial systems engineering). The computation axis engagement is moderate but increasing as theoretical work on foundation models develops.

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized large language models and foundation models acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat foundation models as products to be used rather than as systems to be understood.

The first is *fluency with the contemporary foundation model landscape*. The trained practitioner knows the major foundation model families (the GPT family, the Claude family, the Gemini family, the Llama family, the various open-weight families), understands their characteristic differences, knows which is appropriate for which kinds of work, and can keep current with the rapidly developing landscape. This fluency is acquired through substantial engagement with the contemporary literature and through extended practice with the various models, and remains valuable as the specific systems evolve.

The second capacity is *the ability to evaluate foundation models seriously*. The trained practitioner can take a foundation model and a deployment context and evaluate whether the model is appropriate — what its capabilities are, what its limitations are, what failure modes are likely, what evaluation procedures would be informative. This capacity is substantial engineering work and is one of the more durable capacities the discipline produces. The various contemporary evaluation literatures (the substantial benchmark literature, the various evaluation methodology papers, the substantial work on capability characterization) provide the conceptual content this capacity requires.

The third capacity is *fluency with the adaptation techniques*. The trained practitioner is fluent with the various ways foundation models can be adapted to specific tasks: prompt engineering (when it suffices), in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation, fine-tuning approaches (supervised fine-tuning, instruction tuning, preference-based fine-tuning), parameter-efficient adaptation (LoRA and the various others). The fluency includes understanding when each technique is appropriate, what each provides, what the trade-offs among them are. This fluency is essential for practical work and is acquired through substantial practice with the various approaches.

The fourth capacity is *the discipline for using foundation models in production*. The trained practitioner can build production systems using foundation models with appropriate engineering discipline: appropriate evaluation, appropriate handling of failure modes, appropriate cost management, appropriate monitoring, appropriate handling of the substantial security and safety concerns that foundation models present. This discipline is substantial and is acquired through substantial production experience and through engagement with the substantial contemporary literature on foundation model deployment.

The fifth capacity is *the capacity for honest engagement with the field's open questions*. The trained practitioner recognizes that foundation models present substantial open questions — about capabilities, about limitations, about safety, about the various societal implications — and engages with these questions seriously rather than pretending they are solved. This capacity is genuinely valuable in a field that is substantially shaped by marketing-influenced presentations that overstate confidence and understate concerns. Practitioners who have absorbed it produce work substantially more reliable than the alternative.

The sixth capacity is *appreciation for the boundary between what can be done and what should be done*. The trained practitioner engages substantially with the substantial literature on AI safety, ethics, and the broader implications of foundation model deployment, and brings this engagement to engineering decisions. The capacity distinguishes engineers who can build substantial systems from engineers who can also evaluate whether those systems should be built, and is increasingly important as foundation models are deployed in substantial contexts.

### How the field sees the world

Large language models and foundation models embeds a worldview that has emerged rapidly and that is substantially distinctive among CS subjects.

The first commitment is *to general-purpose pretraining as a substantial paradigm*. The foundation model worldview holds that substantial pretraining on broad data produces models with substantial general-purpose capability, and that this approach has substantial advantages over the task-specific training that earlier paradigms practiced. This commitment runs against earlier engineering dispositions that preferred task-specific systems built for specific purposes. The foundation model practitioner takes a different view: a sufficiently capable general-purpose model serves substantial portions of what task-specific systems served, with substantial advantages in development cost, in capability transfer, and in the various other dimensions where general-purpose systems can be advantageous. The disposition has substantial consequences for system architecture: rather than building specialized models for each task, the foundation model practitioner asks how a general-purpose model can be adapted to the specific task, and the question has different answers and different engineering implications.

The second commitment is *to scale as constitutive*. The foundation model worldview holds that scale — substantial training data, substantial parameters, substantial compute — is constitutive of contemporary capability rather than incidental to it. This commitment is substantially shaped by the empirical scaling phenomena: capability appears to depend substantially on scale, with smaller models often qualitatively different from larger models in what they can and cannot do. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat scale as integral to the discipline, engineer for scale, and recognize that small-scale work has substantial limitations as guidance for large-scale work. The commitment has substantial consequences for who can do frontier work: the substantial compute requirements of frontier foundation models substantially limit frontier work to organizations with substantial computational resources, and this concentration has substantial implications for the field's character.

The third commitment is *to empirical and methodological discipline as primary epistemology*. The foundation model worldview takes seriously that contemporary models are substantially complex and substantially poorly understood, and that competent engagement requires substantial empirical and methodological discipline. This commitment is in some tension with the substantial marketing-influenced presentations of foundation models, which tend toward confident claims about capabilities and limited engagement with limitations. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat foundation models with substantial empirical care: evaluating systematically, measuring rigorously, distinguishing what is known from what is assumed, treating apparent capabilities with appropriate skepticism until validated.

The fourth commitment is *to honest engagement with uncertainty about capability*. The foundation model worldview takes seriously the substantial uncertainty about what these models can and cannot do, and treats this uncertainty as integral to engineering practice rather than as transient ignorance to be ignored. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat capability claims with substantial care, design systems that handle capability uncertainty, and contribute to the development of evaluation rather than treating evaluation as solved.

The fifth commitment is *to the substantial broader implications*. The foundation model worldview takes seriously the substantial broader implications of foundation model deployment — economic, social, political, ethical — and treats engagement with these implications as integral to engineering practice rather than as separate concern. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat broader implications as outside the scope of engineering. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with the substantial AI safety, AI ethics, and broader policy literatures as integral to their engineering work, recognizing that foundation model engineering is substantially consequential and that engineering decisions have substantial implications beyond technical performance.

The sixth commitment is *to the field as substantially incomplete*. The foundation model worldview takes seriously that contemporary foundation models are substantially poorly understood, that substantial open questions remain about capabilities and limitations and behavior, that substantial portions of contemporary practice rely on empirical observations that lack adequate theoretical explanation. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the field's incompleteness honestly, engage with the substantial open questions, and contribute to the development of understanding rather than treating contemporary techniques as final.

The seventh commitment is *to integrated capability, evaluation, and safety thinking*. The foundation model worldview holds that capability development, evaluation, and safety are integrated concerns rather than separable ones — that capability without appropriate evaluation cannot be safely deployed, that evaluation without engagement with capability is shallow, that safety without engagement with capability cannot accomplish its goals. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat these concerns as continuous activities, integrate them in their work, and produce engineering that addresses them coherently rather than separately.

These seven commitments — to general-purpose pretraining, to scale as constitutive, to empirical and methodological discipline, to honest engagement with uncertainty, to substantial broader implications, to honest engagement with incompleteness, and to integrated capability/evaluation/safety thinking — distinguish the foundation model worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in foundation models as serious intellectual and engineering practice rather than as application of products.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning large language models and foundation models are characteristic, well-documented, and unusually consequential — the discipline's substantial empirical character, its rapid pace, and the substantial marketing-influenced surrounding discourse create many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the API-as-discipline misconception*. Foundation models are most commonly accessed through APIs — the OpenAI API, the Anthropic API, the various others — and learners can come to treat learning the APIs as the substance of learning foundation models. The result is practitioners who can call APIs but cannot reason about what models are doing, cannot evaluate whether models are appropriate for specific tasks, cannot diagnose failures, cannot adapt approaches when default APIs do not suffice. The countermeasure is to engage with the underlying technical content seriously: to study transformer architectures, to understand pretraining and fine-tuning, to engage with the substantial research literature, to recognize that API fluency is necessary but not sufficient for foundation model competence.

A second pitfall is *the prompt-engineering-as-discipline misconception*. Substantial portions of contemporary foundation model practice involve crafting prompts to elicit desired behaviors from models, and learners can come to treat prompt engineering as the substance of foundation model work. In practice, prompt engineering is one technique among many, and substantial work requires engagement with substantially deeper concerns: when prompting suffices versus when fine-tuning is required, when retrieval-augmented generation is appropriate, when other adaptation approaches work better, when foundation models are appropriate at all. The countermeasure is to treat prompt engineering as one element in a broader toolkit, to engage with the alternatives and complementary approaches, and to recognize that prompt-only engagement substantially limits what work can be done.

A third pitfall is *the capability-overestimation problem*. Foundation models exhibit substantial capabilities, and learners can come to overestimate what they can do — believing claims about capabilities without verification, applying models to tasks they cannot reliably handle, treating model outputs with insufficient skepticism. The pattern is substantially reinforced by marketing-influenced presentations that emphasize capabilities and understate limitations. The countermeasure is substantial empirical engagement: actually testing models on the specific tasks of interest, with appropriate evaluation methodology, before relying on them. Models that appear to handle tasks may handle them unreliably, may handle them well in some cases and fail in others, may produce outputs that look correct but are systematically wrong. The discipline of empirical evaluation is the antidote to capability overestimation.

A fourth pitfall is *the capability-underestimation problem*. The opposite pattern — dismissing foundation models as fundamentally limited, treating their failures as evidence that they cannot do substantive work — is also common. The pattern is substantially reinforced by skeptical critics who emphasize failures and understate capabilities. In practice, contemporary foundation models can do substantial work, and dismissing them produces engineering that fails to use available tools effectively. The countermeasure is the same as for overestimation: substantial empirical engagement, with appropriate evaluation methodology, to establish what models can and cannot do for the specific tasks of interest. The discipline produces calibrated assessment that neither overestimates nor underestimates capability.

A fifth pitfall is *the hallucination-as-aberration misunderstanding*. Foundation models routinely produce outputs that are confidently wrong — the phenomenon commonly called "hallucination" — and learners often treat this as an aberration to be eliminated rather than as a fundamental characteristic of the technology. In practice, hallucination is integral to how these models work: they produce outputs based on patterns in training data without grounding in factual reality, and the various techniques for reducing hallucination (RAG, fine-tuning, the various others) reduce frequency without eliminating the phenomenon. The countermeasure is to engage with hallucination as a fundamental characteristic to be designed around rather than as a bug to be fixed: to build systems that handle hallucination, that verify model outputs when verification matters, that present model outputs with appropriate uncertainty.

A sixth pitfall is *the benchmark-driven understanding*. Foundation models are evaluated extensively on substantial benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, the various others), and learners can come to treat benchmark performance as a complete measure of model capability. In practice, benchmarks measure specific capabilities under specific conditions, and substantial portions of real-world performance are not captured by standard benchmarks. The countermeasure is to engage with benchmarks critically: to ask what specific capabilities each benchmark measures, what failure modes the benchmarks do not detect, what real-world capabilities differ from benchmark capabilities. The substantial contemporary literature on benchmark validity (the various papers documenting benchmark contamination, the various papers on benchmark gaming, the various papers on evaluation that better captures deployment performance) provides substantial conceptual content for this critical engagement.

A seventh pitfall is *the closed-source-only practice*. Substantial portions of contemporary foundation model practice operate exclusively with proprietary models accessed through APIs, without engagement with the open-weight alternatives. This pattern produces substantial limitations: practitioners cannot examine model internals, cannot fine-tune in non-standard ways, cannot study the substantial research questions that require model access, cannot engage with the substantial open-source ecosystem. The countermeasure is to engage with open-weight models alongside proprietary ones: to use Llama, Mistral, the various other open-weight families, to engage with the substantial open-source tooling (Hugging Face Transformers, the various inference frameworks), to work with model internals when the work requires it. The substantial open-source ecosystem provides substantial capability that closed-source-only practice misses.

An eighth pitfall is *the retrieval-augmented-generation-as-solved-problem misconception*. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) — the technique of augmenting model context with retrieved information from a knowledge base — has been substantially marketed as solving the various limitations of foundation models, particularly the hallucination problem. In practice, RAG substantially helps but does not solve the underlying problems: models can still hallucinate within retrieved context, retrieval quality substantially affects results, the various engineering challenges of RAG (chunking, embedding, retrieval quality, the various others) are substantial. The countermeasure is to treat RAG as a substantial technique with substantial engineering depth rather than as a finished solution: to engage with the substantial RAG literature, to understand what RAG does and does not provide, to engineer RAG systems with appropriate care.

A ninth pitfall is *the safety-as-someone-else's-problem disposition*. Foundation models have substantial safety implications, and engineers building applications with foundation models often treat safety as a concern of the foundation model providers rather than of the application engineers. This disposition produces applications that fail at safety even when the underlying foundation models are reasonably aligned: applications that ask models to do things outside their reliable capability, that present model outputs as authoritative when they are not, that exhibit failure modes that the foundation model providers cannot anticipate without seeing the application. The countermeasure is to take application-level safety seriously: to engage with the substantial AI safety literature, to evaluate applications for safety as well as capability, to recognize that foundation model providers' alignment work is necessary but not sufficient for application safety.

A tenth pitfall is *the rapid-obsolescence-induced-paralysis problem*. The foundation model field develops substantially rapidly, with substantial new models, techniques, and approaches appearing on monthly timescales. Learners can come to feel that any specific knowledge will be obsolete soon and that learning the field is therefore not worthwhile. The countermeasure is to recognize what is and is not durable in the field: the foundational understanding (transformers, pretraining, fine-tuning, evaluation methodology) is substantially durable, while the specific systems and benchmarks evolve quickly. Investing in the durable foundations and maintaining currency through ongoing engagement with contemporary sources is substantially more productive than either treating contemporary specifics as durable or dismissing the field as too fast-moving to engage with.

An eleventh pitfall, applicable specifically to this subject, is *the LLM-as-teacher overuse*. Foundation models can be used to learn about foundation models — answering questions, explaining concepts, providing examples — and learners can come to rely on this self-referential learning. The cost is substantial: the models' explanations of their own behavior are not necessarily reliable (the substantial literature on this question documents substantial failures), the models can produce confident explanations of phenomena they do not actually understand, the models cannot teach the empirical methodology and the diagnostic skill that hands-on engagement develops. The countermeasure is to use foundation models as supplements to substantive engagement with primary sources rather than as replacements for that engagement, particularly for foundational understanding.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on large language models and foundation models is unusually rapid-moving, with substantial new papers appearing weekly and the dominant systems changing substantially within timeframes of months to years. The literature is also substantially diverse: research papers from academic and industrial sources, technical reports from major AI labs, blog posts and informal writing from practitioners, evaluations and benchmarks, the various other forms. Engagement with the field requires substantial fluency with this diverse literature.

#### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text is currently emerging — no single book has yet established itself as the canonical introduction to foundation models with the authority that earlier books achieved for earlier deep learning material. Several substantial efforts are in progress, and the situation is likely to clarify in coming years.

The most authoritative current introduction is the Stanford CS336 / Center for Research on Foundation Models materials (freely available). The "On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models" report (Bommasani et al., 2021) provided the substantial articulation of the foundation model framing. The various course materials and lecture notes from major universities (Stanford, MIT, CMU, Berkeley, the various others) provide substantial pedagogical material on foundation models, and these materials are frequently updated to reflect the rapidly evolving field.

For learners with substantial deep learning preparation, the foundational research papers are the appropriate primary literature. The Vaswani et al. transformer paper (mentioned in Section 5.2) is foundational. The substantial scaling laws papers (Kaplan et al. 2020, Hoffmann et al. 2022 on Chinchilla) are substantial. The GPT-3 paper (Brown et al. 2020) is foundational for the contemporary era. The InstructGPT paper (Ouyang et al. 2022) and the various subsequent RLHF papers establish the alignment-focused fine-tuning paradigm. The Constitutional AI paper (Bai et al. 2022) covers Anthropic's approach.

For practical introduction, Howard's various subsequent fast.ai materials covering foundation models are substantially useful. The Hugging Face course (free) covers practical foundation model work with the dominant open-source ecosystem. The various tutorials from major AI labs (OpenAI's documentation, Anthropic's documentation, the various others) provide practical introduction to specific systems.

For learners interested in the broader foundation model context, Russell and Norvig's *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (fourth edition, 2020) provides substantial coverage of AI broadly, with the more recent material covering foundation models. The book is comprehensive across AI rather than focused on foundation models specifically, but it provides substantial context.

The reading-order recommendation: the foundational research papers (transformer, scaling laws, GPT-3, InstructGPT) as primary literature, supplemented with course materials from major universities for pedagogical structure, with the Hugging Face course and similar practical materials for hands-on engagement.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in foundation models branches according to specialization.

*Pretraining at depth.* For learners pursuing pretraining specifically, the literature is substantial. The various papers on pretraining data engineering (the various papers on Common Crawl filtering, on data quality, on data composition) cover the data engineering. The various papers on training infrastructure (Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, the various others) cover the systems engineering. The various scaling laws papers establish the empirical relationships. The various papers on training stability and on the various practical concerns (gradient clipping, learning rate schedules at scale, the various others) cover the practical engineering. The substantial industrial AI lab publications (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI, Google DeepMind) provide substantial practitioner literature on pretraining.

*Fine-tuning and alignment.* The literature on fine-tuning has substantial depth. The InstructGPT paper and the original RLHF work establish the foundation. The Constitutional AI paper covers an alternative approach. The Direct Preference Optimization paper (Rafailov et al. 2023) and the various subsequent variants cover the more recent approaches that simplify the RLHF pipeline. The various papers on Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) and on the various other variants extend the picture. The substantial parameter-efficient fine-tuning literature (the LoRA paper from Hu et al. 2021, the QLoRA paper from Dettmers et al. 2023, the various subsequent variants) covers efficient adaptation. The Hugging Face PEFT library documentation provides substantial practical reference.

*Inference at depth.* The literature on inference optimization for foundation models has substantial depth. The substantial quantization literature (the various papers on INT8 quantization, the GPTQ paper, the AWQ paper, the various subsequent work) covers numerical optimization. The various papers on speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., Chen et al.'s contemporary work) cover decoding optimization. The various papers on attention optimization (Flash Attention from Dao et al., the various other papers) cover the attention computation. The vLLM paper and documentation, the TensorRT-LLM documentation, and the various other inference framework documentation cover the systems engineering.

*Evaluation at depth.* The evaluation literature is substantial and rapidly developing. The various standard benchmark papers (MMLU, HumanEval, the various others) establish the contemporary evaluation infrastructure. The various papers on benchmark validity and on evaluation methodology (the various papers on benchmark contamination, on benchmark gaming, on the various ways benchmarks can mislead) constitute substantial methodological literature. The various papers on capability-specific evaluation (reasoning evaluation, coding evaluation, mathematical evaluation, the various others) cover specific capabilities. The various papers on adversarial evaluation and on red-teaming cover the safety-relevant evaluation. The contemporary work on dangerous capability evaluation from various AI safety organizations is increasingly substantial.

*Multimodal foundation models.* The literature on multimodal foundation models has substantial depth. The CLIP paper (Radford et al. 2021) is foundational for vision-language work. The various papers on multimodal models (Flamingo from DeepMind, the various GPT-4V style papers, the various multimodal Llama papers, the various Anthropic and Google multimodal work) cover the contemporary multimodal landscape. The various audio and speech work (Whisper, the various others) extends the multimodal picture. The various video understanding and generation work covers the increasingly substantial video dimension.

*Code generation and code models.* The substantial literature on code-generating foundation models has its own depth. The Codex paper (Chen et al. 2021) is foundational. The various subsequent code model papers (the StarCoder papers, the Code Llama papers, the various others) extend the picture. The substantial empirical work on coding capabilities and limitations (the HumanEval evaluations, the various papers on coding evaluation methodology, the various papers on real-world coding capability) covers what these models can and cannot do.

*Retrieval-augmented generation.* The substantial RAG literature has emerged rapidly. The original RAG paper (Lewis et al. 2020) established the framework. The various subsequent papers on retrieval techniques, on chunking strategies, on the various engineering choices, on evaluation of RAG systems constitute substantial contemporary literature. The various practitioner writings on RAG (the various blog posts from organizations operating substantial RAG systems) provide substantial engineering content.

*Agentic and multi-step systems.* The substantial contemporary work on agentic systems built on foundation models is rapidly developing. The various papers on tool use (the original tool use papers, the various subsequent work), on multi-step reasoning (chain-of-thought, the various subsequent variants), on agent architectures (the various papers on ReAct, on the various agent frameworks) constitute the contemporary literature. The substantial practitioner literature on agent engineering is growing rapidly.

*Theoretical foundations.* The theoretical literature on foundation models is rapidly developing. The various papers on in-context learning theory (the various papers attempting to explain how in-context learning works), on the various scaling phenomena, on the various interpretability-related theoretical work, constitute substantial contemporary literature. The intersection with the broader deep learning theory mentioned in Section 5.2 is substantial.

*Safety and alignment.* The substantial alignment literature is treated in detail in Section 5.7 but intersects substantially with foundation model work. The various papers on jailbreaking and on adversarial robustness, on the various forms of model behavior modification, on the substantial capability and safety evaluation work constitute substantial contemporary literature relevant to this section.

#### Reference works

The Hugging Face documentation and the Transformers library are the substantial reference for working with open-weight foundation models. The documentation is unusually well-developed and constitutes substantial reference material that practitioners consult continuously.

The OpenAI API documentation, the Anthropic API documentation, the Google API documentation, and the various other proprietary API documentation constitute reference for working with the major commercial foundation models.

The various model cards and technical reports from major models (the GPT-4 technical report, the various Claude model cards, the various Gemini technical reports, the various Llama technical reports) constitute substantial reference material on specific systems.

The Papers With Code site (mentioned in earlier sections) provides substantial reference for benchmark results.

The various contemporary survey papers attempt to map the rapidly developing field. These surveys age quickly but provide useful synthesis at points in time.

The arXiv repository, particularly the cs.CL category, is the substantial contemporary publication venue and constitutes ongoing reference for practitioners working at the frontier.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the foundation model mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective on foundation model capabilities.* Substantial critical engagement with foundation models provides perspective on what these systems can and cannot reliably do. The various papers from researchers like Bender, Gebru, and others raise substantial concerns about claimed capabilities. The various papers documenting failure modes of foundation models (the various papers on hallucination characterization, on the various forms of brittle behavior, on the various forms of distribution-sensitive failure) constitute substantial heterodox literature. Marcus and Davis's continued critical writing provides ongoing skeptical perspective.

*The classical NLP perspective.* The substantial classical NLP literature that preceded the foundation model era covers techniques and approaches that contemporary literature sometimes treats too briefly. Manning and Schütze's *Foundations of Statistical Natural Language Processing* and Jurafsky and Martin's *Speech and Language Processing* (third edition, freely available) provide substantial coverage of pre-foundation-model NLP. Engagement with this tradition gives perspective on what foundation models have replaced and what classical approaches still offer.

*The symbolic AI tradition.* The substantial symbolic AI tradition (treated more substantially in Section 5.5) represents an alternative to the foundation model paradigm. Engagement with classical AI gives perspective on what foundation models do and do not provide, and on what alternative approaches to intelligence might look like. The various contemporary neuro-symbolic approaches that combine symbolic and neural methods provide hybrid perspective.

*The cognitive science perspective on language models.* The substantial cognitive science engagement with language models provides perspective on the relationship between these systems and human language understanding. The various papers on the cognitive plausibility of language models, on what they can and cannot model about human language processing, on the substantial differences between language model behavior and human behavior, constitute substantial heterodox literature.

*The retrieval-and-symbolic-reasoning emphasis.* Substantial work argues that the limitations of foundation models can be substantially addressed by combining them with retrieval systems and with symbolic reasoning components, rather than by scaling foundation models alone. The various papers in this tradition (Karpas et al.'s work, the various papers on Toolformer-style approaches, the various papers on symbolic-neural hybrid systems) constitute substantial alternative direction.

*The AI safety critical perspectives.* The substantial AI safety literature includes substantial critical engagement with contemporary foundation model deployment. The various papers from AI safety researchers (treated more substantially in Section 5.7) raise substantial concerns about contemporary practice. Engagement with this literature gives perspective on what concerns are real and what mitigations are available.

#### What to skip and why

Most marketing-influenced books on "AI" or "ChatGPT" or similar that conflate foundation models with broader claims about artificial intelligence should generally be skipped. The genre is substantially uneven, and the canonical research literature is more reliable.

Most introductory books on "prompt engineering" should be approached with substantial caution. Some are valuable, but many treat prompt engineering as a more substantial discipline than it is, with substantial portions of their content being heuristics that age quickly.

Books on specific foundation model APIs (OpenAI API, the various others) should be approached as practical references rather than as foundational learning. The APIs evolve, and the books about them age quickly.

Most "Generative AI for Business" or similar industry-focused books should be skipped except as practical references. The genre exists and is substantially uneven.

Books on foundation models published before 2023 are now substantially dated and should be supplemented with current material. The field has changed substantially since 2023, and pre-2023 books may cover important foundational material but do not adequately cover contemporary practice.

Most online tutorials on foundation models are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained materials from major universities, the substantial Hugging Face course, the well-developed framework documentation, the substantial talks from prominent researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth and often perpetuates misunderstandings rather than addressing them.

Books that promise to teach "how AI thinks" or that anthropomorphize foundation models substantially should be approached with substantial skepticism. The science of what foundation models actually do internally is substantially incomplete (the interpretability work in Section 5.6 is the relevant literature), and confident claims about model internal processes that lack interpretability grounding are typically not reliable.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary foundation model research are conferences. NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR are the primary venues. ACL, EMNLP, and the various NLP-specific venues cover language model work specifically. The various more specialized workshops at these conferences (the alignment workshops, the interpretability workshops, the various others) cover specific subareas.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue. The cs.CL, cs.LG, and cs.AI categories cover the major subareas. Substantial portions of foundation model research appear on arXiv before or instead of conference publication.

The various major industrial AI lab publications constitute substantial contemporary research literature. Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI (FAIR), Google DeepMind, and Microsoft Research all publish substantial work on foundation models, often work that would have appeared in academic conferences in earlier eras. These labs' technical reports and blog posts frequently contain substantial research content.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The various AI lab blog posts (Anthropic's research and blog, OpenAI's research and blog, the various others) provide substantial contemporary content. The various practitioner blogs (Sebastian Raschka's writing, Lilian Weng's deep technical posts, Andrej Karpathy's various writings, Simon Willison's blog, the various others) provide ongoing perspective.

The various AI newsletters (Import AI from Jack Clark, The Batch, AI Snake Oil, Last Week in AI, the various others) provide curated coverage. The Import AI newsletter is particularly substantial and consistently valuable.

The various practitioner-focused podcasts and YouTube channels provide ongoing audio and video content. Lex Fridman's interviews with foundation model researchers often contain substantial technical content. The various conference talk recordings (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ACL, EMNLP talks freely available) provide substantial content.

The various model cards, technical reports, and system cards from major foundation models are substantial contemporary reference. Reading these systematically as new models are released gives ongoing engagement with the contemporary field.

The substantial AI safety organization publications (from Anthropic's safety team, from MIRI, from the various academic AI safety groups, from CSER, from FHI, from the various others) constitute substantial contemporary literature relevant to foundation models.
