### A note on contemporary relevance

Large language models and foundation models is the most contemporarily active subject in computer science as of the late 2020s, with substantial developments substantially reshaping the field on timescales of weeks to months.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial continued capability advancement. Frontier foundation models continue to acquire substantial new capabilities — reasoning capabilities approaching expert human performance on substantial benchmarks, coding capabilities approaching skilled human engineer performance on substantial coding tasks, multimodal capabilities handling text, images, audio, and video coherently, the various other capability advances. The trajectory in this area continues, with substantial uncertainty about how far it will extend and at what pace.

The second major development is the substantial deployment of foundation models in consequential contexts. Foundation models are increasingly deployed in medical, legal, educational, financial, and the various other contexts where the stakes are substantial. The trajectory suggests continued substantial deployment, with substantial implications for the engineering disciplines required and the safety concerns that must be addressed.

The third development is the substantial economic and social impact. Foundation models have become substantial economic factors — both as substantial businesses in their own right and as substantial productivity tools that reshape various industries. The substantial economic implications continue to develop, with substantial uncertainty about long-term effects.

The fourth development is the substantial growth of agent-based systems built on foundation models. The contemporary work on agents — systems that use foundation models for multi-step reasoning, tool use, and the various other agentic capabilities — has produced substantial new infrastructure and substantial new research. The trajectory in this area is substantial and ongoing, with substantial implications for what foundation-model-based systems can do.

The fifth development is the substantial growth of safety research. The substantial concerns about increasingly capable foundation models have produced substantial research community focused on safety. The substantial AI safety work, treated in Section 5.7, has become substantial enough to constitute its own subdiscipline. The trajectory continues, with substantial AI safety work directed at foundation models specifically.

The sixth development is the substantial open-source ecosystem maturation. The substantial open-weight model releases (Llama, Mistral, the various others), substantial open-source tooling (Hugging Face, vLLM, the various others), and substantial open research community provide substantial counterweight to the proprietary frontier models. The trajectory in this area suggests continued maturation, with substantial implications for who can do what kind of work.

The seventh development is the substantial regulatory engagement. Substantial regulatory frameworks are emerging or being proposed in major jurisdictions (the EU AI Act, the various US executive orders and proposed legislation, the various other national approaches). The trajectory suggests continued regulatory development, with substantial implications for how foundation models are developed and deployed.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of the discipline is more robust than its rapid pace might suggest. The core concerns — pretraining at scale, adaptation from pretraining to deployment, inference at scale, evaluation, alignment — remain as articulated in early work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to large language models and foundation models: the discipline is in a substantial moment, with substantial empirical successes, substantial economic and social implications, substantial uncertainty about future trajectory, and substantial substantial open questions about capabilities, limitations, and consequences. Practitioners pursuing this specialization should expect substantial change to continue, should maintain currency through ongoing engagement with contemporary sources, and should recognize that substantial portions of contemporary practice may look substantially different in even a few years. The substantial investment in foundation model competence is rewarded by substantial capability and substantial impact opportunities, but the discipline is one where the foundational mathematical, engineering, and methodological preparation transfers more reliably than the specific contemporary techniques. Learners building durable competence should invest substantially in the foundations alongside the contemporary specifics, recognizing that the foundations will remain valuable while the specifics will need ongoing refresh.

A second final observation specific to this subject: the discipline is unusual among CS subjects in the substantial integration of technical and societal concerns. Engineering decisions in foundation model work have substantial implications beyond technical performance, and competent engagement with the discipline requires substantial engagement with the broader implications. Practitioners who treat foundation model engineering as purely technical work miss substantial portions of what the discipline actually involves, and the integration of technical and broader concerns is one of the substantial sources of the discipline's distinctive intellectual character. The substantial AI safety material in Section 5.7 and the substantial broader context in Appendix D provide the framework for this integration that this section does not attempt to provide directly.

## 5.4 — Reinforcement Learning

### What it is, properly

Reinforcement learning is the discipline concerned with how agents can learn to act in environments through interaction, choosing actions to maximize cumulative reward signals over time. The subject treats learning from interaction as a substantial computational paradigm distinct from learning from labeled examples, with characteristic mathematical foundations (the Markov decision process framework and its various extensions), characteristic algorithmic approaches (value-based, policy-based, model-based, and the various hybrid methods), and characteristic application domains (game playing, robotics, recommender systems, the contemporary application to language model alignment, the various others). The discipline has substantial historical depth, with foundational ideas dating from the 1950s in the form of dynamic programming and from the 1980s in the form of temporal-difference learning, and substantial contemporary development driven by the integration of reinforcement learning with deep learning that began producing dramatic empirical results in the mid-2010s.

The conventional account treats reinforcement learning as the part of machine learning that handles "learning from rewards" — the practice of training agents through trial and error in environments where they receive numerical feedback on their actions. This account captures part of the activity but understates substantially what makes reinforcement learning a distinctive intellectual subject. The conceptual core is the recognition that *learning from interaction is fundamentally different from learning from labeled examples*, with characteristic difficulties (the credit assignment problem, the exploration-exploitation trade-off, the substantial sample inefficiency that characterizes most reinforcement learning) that supervised learning does not encounter. The discipline is also characterized by its substantial mathematical foundation in dynamic programming and optimal control, by its substantial empirical character with substantial gap between theoretical guarantees and practical performance, and by its substantial contemporary integration with deep learning that has produced both substantial successes and substantial open questions.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Tabular reinforcement learning* covers the foundational algorithms developed before deep learning — dynamic programming, Monte Carlo methods, temporal-difference learning, Q-learning, SARSA, the various others — that work in environments with small state and action spaces and that establish the conceptual foundations the more contemporary work builds on. *Deep reinforcement learning* extends these foundations with deep neural networks as function approximators, enabling reinforcement learning in substantially larger state spaces — the various deep Q-network (DQN) variants, the various policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, actor-critic methods, PPO, the various others), the various model-based deep RL approaches. *Model-based reinforcement learning* treats reinforcement learning as a problem of learning environment models alongside policies, with substantial intellectual content distinct from the more dominant model-free tradition. *Multi-agent reinforcement learning* extends to settings with multiple interacting agents — substantial in itself and substantial as the foundation for game-theoretic approaches to RL. *Inverse reinforcement learning and imitation learning* concern learning from demonstrations rather than from explicit rewards — substantially relevant for contemporary applications where reward specification is difficult. *Offline reinforcement learning* concerns learning from fixed datasets rather than from continuing interaction — increasingly important for practical applications where online interaction is costly or risky. *Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and related techniques* constitute the substantial contemporary application area where reinforcement learning is used to fine-tune foundation models toward desired behaviors.

The intellectual content of reinforcement learning is distinctive in several ways. The mathematical foundation in Markov decision processes provides substantial rigor that purely empirical machine learning sometimes lacks; engagement with the discipline at depth requires substantial preparation in probability theory, in dynamic programming, and in the various forms of mathematical optimization. The empirical character is substantial — substantial portions of contemporary deep reinforcement learning rely on empirical observations that lack adequate theoretical explanation, and the gap between theoretical guarantees and practical performance is genuinely substantial. The reproducibility difficulties have been substantial enough to constitute a methodological subdiscipline in their own right; the various papers documenting reinforcement learning's reproducibility problems are required reading for anyone doing serious empirical work. The contemporary integration with foundation models through RLHF and related techniques has substantially raised the visibility of reinforcement learning while also substantially extending its application domains.

The reason this account matters is that reinforcement learning has become substantially central to several contemporarily important applications — most prominently the alignment of foundation models, but also game-playing systems that have produced substantial empirical results, robotics applications, and substantial portions of contemporary recommender systems and operations research. The gap between informed reinforcement learning practice and uninformed practice is substantial, and the cost of the gap has grown as reinforcement learning has become more practically consequential. The discipline of reinforcement learning as serious engineering and research differs substantially from reinforcement learning as casual application of standard algorithms, and competent work requires substantial preparation that the surface-level marketing often understates.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize reinforcement learning as a discipline.

**The problem of credit assignment.** When an agent takes a sequence of actions and receives a reward at the end, determining which actions contributed to the outcome is genuinely difficult. The temporal credit assignment problem is one of reinforcement learning's central technical challenges: the agent took many actions; some contributed to the outcome and some did not; the agent must learn which were which from observed reward sequences. The discipline includes substantial work on credit assignment: the temporal-difference learning framework that uses bootstrapped value estimates to assign credit, the eligibility traces that interpolate between Monte Carlo and TD methods, the various advantage estimation approaches that compare actions to learned baselines, the various reward-shaping approaches that introduce intermediate rewards to make credit assignment easier. The intellectual content includes the mathematical machinery for credit assignment (the Bellman equation and its various forms, the various forms of return estimation, the substantial work on variance reduction in policy gradient methods) and the substantial practical work on what credit assignment approaches work in what circumstances. The credit assignment problem is among the most distinctive technical challenges of reinforcement learning, and substantial portions of the discipline's intellectual content address it.

**The problem of exploration versus exploitation.** An agent learning by interaction faces a fundamental tension: it can exploit current knowledge by taking actions that have produced rewards in the past, or it can explore by trying actions whose value is uncertain to gain information that might improve future decisions. Pure exploitation produces substantial losses when current knowledge is poor; pure exploration produces substantial losses when current knowledge is adequate. The exploration-exploitation trade-off is central to reinforcement learning, and substantial portions of the discipline's intellectual content address it. The intellectual content includes the various exploration strategies (epsilon-greedy as the simplest, softmax exploration, upper confidence bound methods from the bandit literature, Thompson sampling from the Bayesian tradition, the various intrinsic motivation approaches that incentivize exploration of unfamiliar states, the various curiosity-driven approaches), the substantial theoretical work on regret bounds in bandit problems, and the substantial work on exploration in deep reinforcement learning where the high-dimensional state spaces make naive exploration infeasible. The contemporary work on exploration in deep RL — the various papers on intrinsic motivation, on curiosity-driven exploration, on exploration through ensemble disagreement, on exploration through random network distillation — constitutes substantial active research.

**The problem of generalization across states and tasks.** Reinforcement learning agents must generalize: they cannot visit every state in any non-trivial environment, and they must develop policies that perform well on states they have not directly experienced. With function approximation (neural networks or otherwise), the generalization problem becomes both more tractable (function approximators can interpolate to unseen states) and more difficult (the various forms of approximation error can produce failures that tabular methods do not exhibit). The intellectual content includes the substantial theoretical work on function approximation in RL (the various analyses of when value function approximation is stable, the substantial work on the deadly triad of function approximation, off-policy learning, and bootstrapping), the substantial work on generalization across tasks (transfer learning in RL, multi-task RL, meta-learning approaches that aim at fast adaptation to new tasks), and the substantial work on representation learning for RL (the various approaches to learning state representations that support generalization). The contemporary integration of deep learning with RL has substantially advanced the practical state of generalization while leaving substantial theoretical questions open.

**The problem of sample efficiency.** Reinforcement learning has historically been substantially sample-inefficient — requiring substantially more interaction with environments to learn than seems necessary in principle, and substantially more than supervised learning typically requires. The various deep RL successes on Atari games and on similar benchmarks required millions to billions of environment steps; humans learn the same games in vastly fewer interactions. The discipline includes substantial work on sample efficiency: model-based methods that learn environment models and use them to reduce required interaction, off-policy methods that reuse data across multiple updates, the various forms of replay and prioritized replay, the various approaches that incorporate prior knowledge or demonstrations to reduce required interaction, the various meta-learning approaches that learn how to learn quickly. The substantial sample inefficiency of reinforcement learning has been a substantial limitation on its practical applicability, and the substantial work on addressing it is one of the discipline's central concerns.

**The problem of reward specification.** Reinforcement learning algorithms optimize for whatever reward signal is provided, and the substantial difficulty of specifying reward signals that produce desired behavior is genuinely substantial. The literature contains substantial documentation of reward hacking — agents finding unexpected ways to maximize specified rewards that do not produce the behavior the reward was intended to incentivize. The discipline includes substantial work on reward specification: the various papers on reward shaping (introducing intermediate rewards to guide learning), the substantial work on inverse reinforcement learning (learning rewards from demonstrations rather than specifying them directly), the substantial work on preference-based reinforcement learning (learning rewards from human comparisons rather than from explicit numeric specifications), the substantial contemporary work on RLHF that uses human preferences to learn rewards for foundation model fine-tuning. The reward specification problem has emerged as substantially central to contemporary applications of reinforcement learning, particularly the application to foundation model alignment, and engagement with it is essential for serious work in the area.

### Where it sits in the map

Reinforcement learning depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the machine learning foundations material (Section 5.1): the foundational material is largely required, particularly the probability theory, the optimization theory, and the methodological discipline. Reinforcement learning depends on substantial probability and statistics — Markov decision processes are fundamentally probabilistic objects, and the various RL algorithms depend on probabilistic reasoning throughout.

From the deep learning material (Section 5.2): substantial preparation is required for serious work in deep reinforcement learning, which dominates the contemporary practice. The architectural fluency, the training expertise, the optimization knowledge, and the methodological discipline that Section 5.2 develops are all foundational for deep RL work.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): substantial mathematical preparation is foundational. Dynamic programming is essential and is the historical mathematical foundation of reinforcement learning. The various forms of optimization theory provide foundation for the algorithmic procedures. Probability and statistics are foundational throughout. The various forms of stochastic processes provide foundation for understanding the probabilistic environments that RL operates in.

From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation, particularly for the dynamic programming and search algorithms that historical RL depends on. The theory of computation (Section 2.3) provides foundation for understanding what is and is not computationally tractable.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: the relationship to foundation models (Section 5.3) is substantial through the contemporary RLHF and related techniques. Substantial portions of contemporary alignment work uses reinforcement learning techniques applied to foundation models, and the techniques cross over substantially. The relationship to classical AI (Section 5.5) is historically substantial — reinforcement learning has substantial roots in the classical AI tradition, particularly through dynamic programming and through the search-and-planning literature, and contemporary RL substantially intersects with classical search techniques. The relationship to interpretability (Section 5.6) and AI safety (Section 5.7) is substantial — reinforcement learning systems present distinctive interpretability challenges, and substantial portions of contemporary AI safety work address reinforcement learning specifically.

From outside computer science: the relationship to optimal control theory is substantial. Reinforcement learning and optimal control are substantially overlapping disciplines that developed in parallel, and contemporary RL substantially incorporates control-theoretic ideas. The relationship to operations research is substantial through dynamic programming and through the various sequential decision-making problems that both fields address. The relationship to economics is substantial through game theory in multi-agent RL settings. The relationship to neuroscience is substantial — reinforcement learning has substantial connections to research on dopamine signaling and on reward learning in biological systems.

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is squarely on the intelligence axis, with substantial engagement on the computation axis (substantial theoretical content from dynamic programming, from convergence theory, from learning theory), moderate engagement on the system axis (RL infrastructure and training systems), and light engagement on the data axis (RL is somewhat less data-shape-determined than supervised learning, though data efficiency concerns produce substantial intersection).

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized reinforcement learning acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat reinforcement learning as a body of techniques to apply.

The first is *fluency with the Markov decision process framework*. The trained practitioner reasons naturally about decision problems in MDP terms — states, actions, transitions, rewards, policies, values — and can formulate practical problems as MDPs (or as one of the various extensions: partially observable MDPs, semi-Markov decision processes, the various others) when appropriate. This framework fluency is foundational and broadly applicable, transferring across specific RL algorithms and remaining relevant as specific techniques evolve. The framework is also unusually transferable to adjacent disciplines (control theory, operations research, decision theory), and learners with strong MDP fluency can engage with substantial portions of those disciplines productively.

The second capacity is *the disposition to think about decisions as sequential and interconnected*. Reinforcement learning's central insight is that decisions are not independent — actions affect future states, future states affect future opportunities for action, and short-term decisions can have substantial long-term consequences. The trained practitioner brings this sequential disposition to decision problems, recognizing when problems have substantial sequential structure that one-shot decision frameworks cannot capture and when they do not. This disposition reshapes how the practitioner approaches problems: rather than asking "what action is best now," the trained practitioner asks "what policy is best over time," and the question has different answers and different engineering implications.

The third capacity is *fluency with the contemporary RL algorithms*. The trained practitioner is fluent with the major contemporary RL algorithms — DQN and its variants, the various policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, actor-critic, PPO, TRPO), the various model-based approaches, the various contemporary approaches to RLHF and related preference-based methods. The fluency includes understanding what each algorithm does, what assumptions it makes, what trade-offs it embodies, what implementations look like in practice. This algorithmic fluency is essential for serious work and is acquired through substantial practice with implementing and using the various algorithms.

The fourth capacity is *the methodological discipline required for empirical RL work*. Reinforcement learning has substantial documented reproducibility problems, with substantial portions of published results proving difficult to reproduce. Competent empirical RL work requires substantial methodological discipline: appropriate baselines, appropriate hyperparameter tuning, appropriate handling of variance across random seeds, appropriate evaluation methodology, appropriate statistical analysis of results. This discipline is acquired through substantial practice and through engagement with the substantial methodological literature, and is one of the more important capacities the discipline produces.

The fifth capacity is *appreciation for the substantial sample inefficiency of standard RL*. The trained practitioner understands that reinforcement learning is substantially sample-inefficient compared to supervised learning, and that this sample inefficiency has substantial implications for what problems RL is appropriate for. This appreciation produces engineering that uses RL where it is appropriate (substantial environment access, low cost per environment step, problems with substantial sequential structure) and that uses alternative approaches where RL is inappropriate (substantial environment access is not available, environment steps are costly, the problem is essentially supervised). This judgment is one of the more durable capacities the discipline produces and distinguishes mature practitioners from practitioners who reach for RL reflexively.

The sixth capacity is *the appreciation for the substantial difficulty of reward specification*. The trained practitioner understands that specifying reward signals that produce desired behavior is genuinely difficult, recognizes the various forms of reward hacking and their causes, and engages with reward specification as substantive engineering rather than as straightforward technical detail. This appreciation has substantial implications for contemporary applications of RL, particularly RLHF: practitioners who understand reward specification difficulty engage with the contemporary alignment work substantially differently from practitioners who treat reward specification as solved.

The seventh capacity is *the integration of reinforcement learning with adjacent disciplines*. The trained practitioner can engage productively with adjacent disciplines — control theory, operations research, decision theory, economics, neuroscience — that share substantial foundations with RL. This integration distinguishes practitioners who can do substantial cross-disciplinary work from practitioners limited to RL narrowly understood, and is one of the more valuable capacities the discipline produces.

### How the field sees the world

Reinforcement learning embeds a worldview that is distinctive among intelligence-axis subjects.

The first commitment is *to learning through interaction as a substantial paradigm*. The reinforcement learning worldview holds that learning by interaction with environments — taking actions, observing consequences, adjusting behavior based on feedback — is a substantial and distinctive way for computational systems to acquire competence, complementary to learning from labeled examples. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer offline learning from prepared datasets, that treat environment interaction as expensive and uncertain. The RL practitioner takes a different view: there are problems for which interactive learning is appropriate or essential, and the development of agents that can learn from interaction is substantively valuable. The disposition has consequences for how problems are approached: rather than asking "what dataset would let me learn this," the RL practitioner asks "what environment would let me learn this through interaction," and the question has different engineering implications.

The second commitment is *to the centrality of the Markov decision process framework*. The reinforcement learning worldview takes the MDP framework — and its various extensions to partial observability, multiple agents, the various other complications — as the fundamental mathematical structure for sequential decision problems. This commitment provides substantial mathematical rigor and substantial cross-disciplinary connection (with control theory, operations research, decision theory). Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat MDP formalization as integral to RL work, recognize when problems do and do not fit the framework, and bring the framework to substantial portions of decision-theoretic work outside reinforcement learning narrowly understood.

The third commitment is *to taking the credit assignment problem seriously*. The RL worldview recognizes that determining which actions contributed to which outcomes in sequential decision problems is genuinely difficult, and that substantial portions of RL's distinctive intellectual content address this difficulty. This commitment shapes how practitioners think about learning from interaction — recognizing that the simple learning rules that work for one-shot decisions cannot work for sequential decisions, that substantial mathematical machinery (the Bellman equation and its various forms) is required, that the various practical approximations have substantial trade-offs. The commitment runs against simplistic views that treat reinforcement learning as just supervised learning with rewards as labels.

The fourth commitment is *to the exploration-exploitation trade-off as fundamental*. The RL worldview takes the tension between exploiting current knowledge and exploring to gain new knowledge as fundamental rather than as incidental difficulty. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat exploration as substantive engineering concern, design exploration strategies deliberately, and recognize that pure exploitation (which simpler ML paradigms can use) is fundamentally inadequate for learning by interaction.

The fifth commitment is *to honest engagement with sample inefficiency*. The RL worldview takes seriously the substantial sample inefficiency of contemporary methods and the substantial gap between RL's empirical capabilities and the capabilities of biological learners on similar problems. This commitment runs against marketing-influenced presentations that elide the substantial sample requirements of contemporary deep RL. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment evaluate methods honestly on sample efficiency, recognize when sample efficiency rules out RL approaches in practice, and contribute to the substantial work on sample efficiency rather than treating it as a solved problem.

The sixth commitment is *to the substantial difficulty of reward specification*. The RL worldview recognizes that specifying reward signals that produce desired behavior is genuinely difficult, and that substantial portions of RL applications require careful engineering of reward specification. This commitment shapes how practitioners approach RL applications: with substantial attention to what behaviors a given reward function actually incentivizes, with substantial skepticism about reward functions that have not been validated, with substantial recognition that alternative approaches (inverse RL, preference-based RL, the various others) may be needed when explicit reward specification is impractical.

The seventh commitment is *to honest engagement with reproducibility*. The RL worldview takes seriously the substantial documented reproducibility problems in RL research and the substantial methodological discipline required for reliable empirical work. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer to ignore methodological concerns. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat methodology as primary work, run experiments with substantial care, and report results with substantial honesty about uncertainty.

These seven commitments — to learning through interaction, to the MDP framework, to credit assignment, to the exploration-exploitation trade-off, to honest engagement with sample inefficiency, to the difficulty of reward specification, and to methodological discipline — distinguish the reinforcement learning worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in reinforcement learning as serious intellectual practice rather than as application of pre-built techniques.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning reinforcement learning are characteristic and substantially well-documented, with several specific to the substantial empirical character of contemporary deep RL.

The most common pitfall is *the algorithm-collection-without-foundations approach*. Reinforcement learning has substantial collection of algorithms — DQN, A3C, PPO, SAC, the various others — and learners can come to treat learning the algorithms as the substance of learning RL. The result is practitioners who can implement standard algorithms but cannot reason about what they are doing, cannot adapt them to non-standard problems, cannot diagnose failures, cannot evaluate when RL is or is not appropriate. The countermeasure is to engage with the foundational material seriously: to study the MDP framework, to engage with dynamic programming, to understand the credit assignment and exploration problems at depth, to recognize that algorithmic fluency follows from foundational understanding rather than substituting for it.

A second pitfall is *the deep-RL-without-RL-foundations problem*. The substantial visibility of deep reinforcement learning successes can produce learners who attempt deep RL without sufficient preparation in tabular RL foundations. The result is practitioners who can apply deep RL methods to standard benchmarks but who lack the conceptual context for understanding what deep RL is doing or for adapting methods to non-benchmark problems. The countermeasure is to engage with tabular RL seriously before moving to deep RL: to work through the standard tabular methods (dynamic programming, Monte Carlo, TD learning, Q-learning, SARSA) with substantial care, to understand the various theoretical results in tabular settings, to recognize that deep RL extends rather than replaces tabular RL.

A third pitfall is *the reproducibility-blindness problem*. Reinforcement learning has substantial documented reproducibility problems, with substantial portions of published results proving difficult to reproduce reliably. Learners who do not engage with reproducibility seriously can produce work that is not reliable: results that depend on specific random seeds, on specific implementation details, on specific hyperparameter choices that are not adequately documented. The countermeasure is explicit attention to reproducibility from the beginning of RL practice: running experiments with multiple random seeds (substantially more than in supervised learning), reporting variance across seeds rather than only means, engaging with the substantial reproducibility literature (Henderson et al.'s "Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters" paper and the various subsequent work), and recognizing that reproducibility is integral to honest RL work.

A fourth pitfall is *the benchmark-tunnel-vision problem*. RL evaluation typically involves standard benchmarks — Atari games, MuJoCo continuous control tasks, the various RL-specific benchmarks — and learners can come to optimize for benchmark performance without engaging with what the benchmarks measure. The pattern is particularly problematic in RL because the benchmarks have substantial known limitations: Atari games measure specific kinds of pattern recognition rather than general decision-making; MuJoCo tasks have characteristic reward specifications that may not transfer to real applications. The countermeasure is to treat benchmarks as one tool among many: to use them for what they measure, to recognize their limitations, to evaluate methods on the actual problems of interest where possible.

A fifth pitfall is *the reward-specification-as-trivial misunderstanding*. Reinforcement learning algorithms optimize for whatever reward signal is provided, and the substantial difficulty of specifying reward signals that produce desired behavior is sometimes underappreciated. Learners can specify reward functions casually without engaging with the substantial reward hacking and reward misalignment problems that follow. The countermeasure is to engage with reward specification as substantive engineering: to study the substantial literature on reward hacking and reward misalignment, to recognize that reward functions are essentially specifications of desired behavior and require the same engineering care that any specification requires, to consider alternative approaches (inverse RL, preference-based RL, the various others) when explicit reward specification is impractical.

A sixth pitfall is *the on-policy / off-policy confusion*. The distinction between on-policy methods (which learn from data generated by the current policy) and off-policy methods (which learn from data generated by different policies) is fundamental in RL, with substantial implications for which algorithms can use which data. Learners often confuse the distinction or fail to engage with its implications, with the result that they apply algorithms in inappropriate ways. The countermeasure is to engage with the on-policy / off-policy distinction seriously: to understand what each requires, what the various theoretical results say about when off-policy learning is stable, why the deadly triad (function approximation, off-policy learning, bootstrapping) produces substantial difficulties, and how the various contemporary algorithms address these difficulties.

A seventh pitfall is *the simulation-to-reality gap underestimation*. Substantial portions of RL research operate in simulated environments because simulation provides the substantial environment access that RL requires. Learners can come to underestimate the gap between simulation-trained agents and agents that work in real environments. The pattern is particularly substantial in robotics applications where the simulation-to-reality gap is among the central practical challenges. The countermeasure is to engage with the simulation-to-reality gap seriously: to study the substantial literature on domain randomization, on sim-to-real transfer, on the various forms of robustness that real-world deployment requires, and to recognize that simulation success is necessary but not sufficient for real-world deployment.

An eighth pitfall is *the safety-and-robustness-as-afterthought disposition*. RL agents can produce surprising and undesirable behaviors when deployed in real environments, and safety considerations are integral to substantial RL applications. Learners often treat safety as an afterthought to be addressed after the agent is trained, rather than as a primary concern integrated throughout. The countermeasure is to engage with safety considerations from the beginning: to study the safe RL literature, to understand the various forms of constrained RL that incorporate safety constraints into training, to recognize that for substantial applications (robotics, safety-critical control, the substantial alignment applications) safety is constitutive of correct training rather than a post-training concern.

A ninth pitfall, increasingly relevant, is the *RLHF-as-magic problem*. The substantial contemporary application of RL to foundation model alignment through RLHF and related techniques can produce engineers who treat RLHF as a kind of magic that solves alignment problems. In practice, RLHF has substantial known limitations: it depends substantially on the quality of human feedback, it produces models that are aligned to the specific preferences expressed in the training data rather than to broader notions of correctness, it has various documented failure modes (sycophancy, the various forms of mode collapse, the various others). The countermeasure is to engage with RLHF as substantial engineering with substantial known limitations: to study the contemporary literature on RLHF's effectiveness and limits, to recognize the various failure modes, and to treat alignment as substantive open problem rather than as solved through standard RLHF techniques.

A tenth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied specifically to RL. Modern AI tools can produce substantial RL code, can answer many RL questions, and can guide learners through standard exercises. The cost is unusually high in RL because the discipline depends substantially on developing intuition through hands-on experimentation with environments, on debugging the various subtle failure modes that RL exhibits, on the substantial methodological discipline that comes from running experiments and engaging with their variability. Learners who use LLMs to bypass hands-on engagement do not develop these capacities. The countermeasure is to do early RL work with deliberate engagement, even when LLM assistance could produce working code, and to use the tools as supplements to direct engagement rather than as replacements.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on reinforcement learning has a distinctive shape. The discipline has substantial historical depth, with foundational texts dating from the late 1990s and early 2000s that remain authoritative on conceptual fundamentals. The contemporary literature is unusually diverse: research papers from academic and industrial sources, technical reports from major AI labs (substantially active in RL through the contemporary RLHF work), substantial open-source code repositories, the various other forms.

#### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text is Sutton and Barto's *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* (second edition, 2018, freely available). The book is unusually authoritative — Sutton and Barto are the substantial founders of the field, and the book presents the discipline as its founders see it. The treatment is mathematically careful but accessible, with substantial intuition-building alongside formal development. The second edition substantially extends the first to cover more contemporary developments while maintaining the foundational character. The book is essentially the appropriate primary text for serious RL study, and engineers who have worked through it have substantial foundation that those who have not lack. The book's freely available status removes any practical barrier to engagement.

For more mathematical depth, Bertsekas's *Reinforcement Learning and Optimal Control* (2019) covers the integration of reinforcement learning with optimal control theory at substantial depth. Bertsekas's earlier *Dynamic Programming and Optimal Control* (in its various editions) covers the dynamic programming foundations more thoroughly. These are appropriate for learners who want substantial connection to the broader optimal control tradition and substantial mathematical rigor.

For deep reinforcement learning specifically, the contemporary canonical text is still emerging. Plaat's *Deep Reinforcement Learning* (2022) provides current coverage. Various course materials from major universities (Berkeley CS285, the various others) provide substantial pedagogical content on deep RL. The OpenAI Spinning Up resource (free) provides accessible entry to deep RL with substantial code. Stable Baselines3 documentation provides practical reference for working with the standard deep RL algorithms.

For the contemporary RLHF and alignment-related applications, the relevant literature is substantially the foundation model literature covered in Section 5.3 — the InstructGPT paper, the Constitutional AI paper, the DPO paper, and the various subsequent work. The integration of RL with foundation model fine-tuning has produced substantial new literature that practitioners working in this area engage with directly.

The reading-order recommendation: Sutton and Barto as the foundational text (essentially required for serious RL study), supplemented with Bertsekas for mathematical depth and connections to optimal control, with deep RL course materials for the contemporary deep RL specifics, with the foundation model RLHF literature for the contemporary alignment applications.

The various course materials from major universities (Sutton's course at Alberta, the Berkeley deep RL course CS285 with David Silver's lecture series for deep RL specifically, the various MIT and Stanford courses) constitute substantial pedagogical resources that complement the textbook material.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in reinforcement learning branches according to specialization.

*Tabular RL at depth.* For learners pursuing the foundational tabular methods at depth, Puterman's *Markov Decision Processes: Discrete Stochastic Dynamic Programming* is the foundational reference for the MDP mathematical theory. The book is mathematically demanding but authoritative on the underlying mathematics. Bertsekas's *Dynamic Programming and Optimal Control* provides similar mathematical depth from the optimal control perspective.

*Deep RL algorithms at depth.* For depth on specific deep RL algorithms, the foundational papers are substantial. Mnih et al.'s DQN papers ("Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning" 2013, "Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning" 2015) launched the deep RL era. The various policy gradient papers (Sutton et al.'s policy gradient theorem, Williams's REINFORCE, the various actor-critic papers, Schulman et al.'s TRPO and PPO papers) constitute foundational deep RL literature. The various papers on continuous control (DDPG, SAC from Haarnoja et al., the various others) cover the continuous action setting. Each of these papers warrants direct engagement for serious deep RL practitioners.

*Model-based RL at depth.* For learners pursuing model-based RL, the foundational literature includes the various Dyna papers from Sutton, the various MuZero papers from DeepMind (Schrittwieser et al.), the various world model papers (Ha and Schmidhuber's World Models, the various subsequent work), and the various Dreamer papers (Hafner et al.'s sequential publications). The contemporary model-based RL literature is substantial and rapidly developing.

*Multi-agent RL at depth.* For learners pursuing multi-agent RL, Shoham and Leyton-Brown's *Multiagent Systems* (free) provides foundational coverage of multi-agent decision-making. The various contemporary multi-agent RL papers (the OpenAI Five and AlphaStar work, the various MARL benchmark papers, the substantial cooperative MARL literature) constitute the contemporary deep multi-agent RL literature.

*Inverse RL and imitation learning at depth.* For learners pursuing inverse RL and imitation learning, Ng and Russell's foundational inverse RL paper provides the foundational framework. Ziebart et al.'s maximum entropy IRL paper is foundational for the contemporary IRL tradition. The various behavioral cloning and imitation learning papers (DAgger from Ross et al., the various subsequent work) cover imitation learning. Schulman's lectures on IRL and imitation learning provide pedagogically substantial introduction.

*Offline RL at depth.* For learners pursuing offline RL specifically, Levine et al.'s "Offline Reinforcement Learning: Tutorial, Review, and Perspectives on Open Problems" (2020) provides the contemporary survey. The various offline RL algorithm papers (Conservative Q-Learning from Kumar et al., Implicit Q-Learning from Kostrikov et al., the various others) constitute the contemporary algorithmic literature. Offline RL is increasingly important for practical applications where online interaction is costly.

*Exploration at depth.* For learners pursuing exploration specifically, the foundational bandit literature (Lattimore and Szepesvári's *Bandit Algorithms*, free) provides the theoretical foundation. The various deep RL exploration papers (Bellemare et al.'s pseudo-counts, the various intrinsic motivation papers, Burda et al.'s Random Network Distillation, the various curiosity-driven exploration papers) cover the deep RL specifics.

*RL for foundation models at depth.* For learners pursuing the contemporary RLHF and related work, the relevant literature is substantially in Section 5.3's coverage of foundation models. The InstructGPT paper, the Constitutional AI paper, the DPO paper and its variants, and the substantial subsequent literature on preference-based fine-tuning constitute the relevant material. The substantial recent work on alternative approaches to RLHF (the various direct preference optimization variants, the various papers on off-policy preference learning, the various papers on constrained preference learning) extends the picture.

*Theoretical RL at depth.* For learners pursuing theoretical RL, the foundational papers on convergence (the various papers on Q-learning convergence, on policy gradient convergence, on the various stability results) are substantial. The contemporary theoretical RL literature (the various papers on PAC learning bounds, on regret bounds, on sample complexity, the various papers on the deadly triad and on stable function approximation) constitutes substantial active research. Agarwal, Jiang, Kakade, and Sun's *Reinforcement Learning: Theory and Algorithms* (free) provides contemporary theoretical treatment.

*Robotics-specific RL.* For learners pursuing RL for robotics, the substantial robotics-specific literature is relevant. Kober, Bagnell, and Peters's "Reinforcement Learning in Robotics: A Survey" provides substantial introduction. The substantial contemporary work on sim-to-real transfer, on domain randomization, on the various forms of robotic RL constitutes ongoing research.

*Game-playing RL.* The substantial literature on RL for game playing is foundational. Tesauro's TD-Gammon papers established the foundation. Silver et al.'s AlphaGo and AlphaZero papers are substantial. The various Go, chess, and broader game-playing literature constitutes substantial historical and contemporary material.

#### Reference works

Sutton and Barto's *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* serves as reference work that practitioners consult throughout their careers.

The OpenAI Spinning Up resource is the substantial reference for getting started with deep RL practice.

The Stable Baselines3 documentation is the authoritative reference for the dominant standard implementation library for deep RL.

The various deep RL framework documentation (the RLlib documentation, the CleanRL documentation, the various others) constitutes reference for specific implementation contexts.

Lattimore and Szepesvári's *Bandit Algorithms* is the foundational reference for bandit problems and their connections to RL.

Puterman's *Markov Decision Processes* is the foundational reference for the MDP mathematical theory.

The various OpenAI Gym (now Gymnasium) and DeepMind Control Suite documentation is the authoritative reference for the standard RL benchmarks.

The various major RL conferences' proceedings (RLDM, the various NeurIPS RL workshops, the broader AI conference RL tracks) constitute substantial reference for contemporary research.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the reinforcement learning mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The optimal control perspective.* The substantial optimal control tradition (substantially associated with Bertsekas, with the broader control engineering community, with the various MIT optimal control programs) covers substantially the same mathematical territory as RL but with different emphasis. Engaging with optimal control gives perspective on what RL is doing and on alternatives to the dominant deep RL framing. Bertsekas's various books are the canonical references.

*The Bayesian RL perspective.* The substantial Bayesian RL literature treats RL through the Bayesian framework — maintaining distributions over MDPs, using these distributions to balance exploration and exploitation, providing principled uncertainty quantification. Ghavamzadeh et al.'s "Bayesian Reinforcement Learning: A Survey" provides foundational treatment. The argument is that the Bayesian framework provides substantially better uncertainty handling than standard RL and that the various engineering challenges (computational tractability) are worth addressing for the substantial benefits. The position has substantial proponents and substantial intellectual coherence.

*The neuroscience perspective.* The substantial connections between RL and biological learning (particularly through dopamine signaling and through the substantial work on reward learning in the brain) provide alternative framing. Sutton and Barto's book includes substantial neuroscience-relevant material. The various papers connecting RL algorithms to biological learning (the various papers on TD learning and dopamine, the various papers on actor-critic architectures and basal ganglia) constitute substantial alternative literature. Engagement with this tradition gives perspective on whether contemporary RL approaches biological learning meaningfully or whether substantial differences remain.

*The skeptical perspective on deep RL.* Substantial critical engagement with deep RL provides perspective on the dominant paradigm's limitations. The various papers documenting deep RL's reproducibility problems (Henderson et al. and the various subsequent work), the various papers documenting brittleness of deep RL agents, the various papers on the substantial sample inefficiency of deep RL constitute substantial heterodox literature. Marcus's various critical writings on deep RL specifically extend the picture.

*The decision-theoretic perspective.* The substantial decision theory tradition (substantially associated with the broader operations research community, with the substantial economics literature on decision-making, with the philosophical decision theory literature) provides alternative framing for sequential decision problems. Engaging with this tradition gives perspective on what RL is doing within the broader landscape of approaches to sequential decision problems.

*The "RL is fundamentally limited" perspective.* Various researchers argue that RL as currently constituted is fundamentally limited in ways that scaling cannot address — that the substantial sample inefficiency, the reward specification difficulty, the various other problems are not transient but fundamental. The arguments are sometimes overstated but contain genuine insight. The various papers from researchers like Lecun (whose self-supervised learning advocacy includes critical engagement with RL) extend the picture.

#### What to skip and why

Books on reinforcement learning that predate Sutton and Barto's first edition (1998) should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The field has evolved substantially since then.

Books that focus exclusively on the deep RL period without engaging with the foundational tabular material should be supplemented with foundational study rather than relied on as primary references. The deep RL methods are extensions of tabular RL, and engaging with deep RL without tabular RL preparation produces incomplete understanding.

Books on RL applications in specific industries that abstract over the algorithmic content should be skipped as primary references. They produce technicians rather than practitioners.

Marketing-driven books on "AI" or "self-improving systems" that conflate RL with broader claims should generally be skipped.

Books on RL that promise quick mastery should be approached with skepticism. RL has substantial conceptual depth, and treatments that abstract over the depth produce incomplete preparation.

Most online tutorials on RL topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — Sutton and Barto's accompanying materials, the OpenAI Spinning Up resource, the major university course materials, the substantial talks from prominent RL researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth.

Books on specific RL libraries (specific TF-Agents tutorials, specific Stable Baselines tutorials, the various others) should be approached as practical references rather than as foundational learning. The libraries evolve, and the books about them age quickly.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary RL research are conferences. NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR (mentioned in earlier sections) are the primary venues. RLDM (Reinforcement Learning and Decision Making) is a more focused venue. The various conference workshops on RL specifically (the deep RL workshops at NeurIPS, the various others) cover specific subtopics.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue, with substantial portions of RL research appearing on arXiv. The cs.LG, cs.AI, and stat.ML categories cover the relevant subareas.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The DeepMind blog covers substantial RL research that emerges from DeepMind. The OpenAI blog (where it covers RL topics) provides substantial content. The Berkeley AI Research blog (BAIR blog) covers substantial academic RL research. Sutton's blog provides ongoing perspective from one of the field's founders. The various practitioner blogs provide ongoing technical content.

The various practitioner-focused podcasts (Talking Machines, where it covers RL topics; the various others) provide ongoing audio content.

The substantial RL-focused YouTube content includes lecture series from major courses (Silver's DeepMind RL lectures, the various Berkeley CS285 lectures), conference talk recordings, and substantial tutorial content.

The various AI lab publications (DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, the various others) include substantial RL content, particularly the substantial contemporary work on RL for foundation models.
