### A note on contemporary relevance

Reinforcement learning is among the more contemporarily active subjects on the intelligence axis, with several developments substantially reshaping the discipline.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial application of reinforcement learning to foundation model alignment through RLHF and related techniques. The InstructGPT paper and the substantial subsequent work have established RLHF as a substantial technique with substantial industrial deployment. The contemporary alternatives (DPO and its variants, the various preference-based fine-tuning approaches) extend the picture. The substantial implications for foundation model alignment continue to develop, with substantial uncertainty about which contemporary approaches will prove most durable.

The second major development is the substantial maturation of offline reinforcement learning. The substantial limitations of online RL — sample inefficiency, the various forms of unsafe exploration, the cost of environment interaction — have produced substantial interest in learning from fixed datasets rather than from continuing interaction. The contemporary offline RL literature is substantial, with substantial industrial deployment in specific contexts (recommender systems, healthcare, the various others). The trajectory suggests continued offline RL development.

The third development is the substantial integration of RL with foundation models more broadly. Beyond RLHF specifically, foundation models are increasingly used as components within RL systems — as world models, as policy initializers, as reward models, as the various other forms. The contemporary work on foundation-model-augmented RL is substantial and continues to develop.

The fourth development is the substantial growth of multi-agent RL research. The substantial successes of multi-agent systems in games (StarCraft, Dota 2, the various others) have produced substantial methodological development that has spread to broader multi-agent applications. The trajectory suggests continued multi-agent development, with substantial implications for substantial application areas.

The fifth development is the substantial reckoning with reinforcement learning's reproducibility and rigor problems. The substantial documented reproducibility issues have produced substantial methodological response, with better practices for evaluation, for hyperparameter tuning, for handling variance across seeds. The trajectory suggests continued methodological development.

The sixth development is the increasing engagement with safety and alignment concerns. The substantial deployment of RL in consequential contexts — particularly through foundation model alignment — has produced substantial attention to whether and how RL-trained systems behave correctly. The substantial intersection with the AI safety material in Section 5.7 continues to develop.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of reinforcement learning is robust. The core concerns — credit assignment, exploration versus exploitation, generalization, sample efficiency, reward specification — remain as articulated in earlier work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to reinforcement learning: among the intelligence-axis subjects, this is the one with the most substantial mathematical foundation in established disciplines outside computer science (optimal control, operations research, decision theory, neuroscience), and the depth of RL competence is bounded substantially by engagement with these foundations. Learners pursuing this specialization should expect to draw substantially on dynamic programming, on optimization theory, on probability theory, and on the broader context that the established mathematical disciplines provide. The discipline is unusual among intelligence-axis subjects in this requirement, and the integration of computer science training with mathematical-and-control-theoretic preparation is one of the substantial sources of the discipline's distinctive intellectual character.

A second observation specific to this subject: reinforcement learning has had substantial cycles of attention through its history — substantial enthusiasm in the early 1990s, partial retreat through the 2000s, substantial enthusiasm again with the deep RL successes of the mid-2010s, and substantial contemporary attention through foundation model alignment applications. The cyclical pattern suggests that practitioners should engage with the discipline on its substantial merits rather than as response to current attention, and should recognize that the substantial fundamental difficulties of RL (sample efficiency, reward specification, the various others) have not been solved by the contemporary developments and may produce future cycles of disillusionment alongside the contemporary enthusiasm.

## 5.5 — Classical AI and Symbolic Approaches

### What it is, properly

Classical AI and symbolic approaches is the discipline concerned with the substantial body of artificial intelligence research and practice that operates on explicit symbolic representations rather than on the distributed numerical representations that contemporary deep learning emphasizes. The subject treats intelligence as substantially a matter of manipulating symbolic structures — logical formulas, search states, knowledge graphs, planning operators, the various others — through algorithms that operate on these structures explicitly rather than learning representations from data. The discipline has substantial historical depth, extending from the founding of artificial intelligence in the 1950s through the substantial expert systems era of the 1980s, and continuing through contemporary work on automated reasoning, knowledge representation, planning, and the various neuro-symbolic approaches that combine symbolic and neural methods. Classical AI dominated artificial intelligence research through approximately the 1990s, was substantially eclipsed by statistical and learning-based approaches in the 2000s and 2010s, and has experienced a partial contemporary renaissance both in its own right and in combination with the contemporary deep learning paradigm.

The conventional account treats classical AI as obsolete — as the predecessor to contemporary machine learning that has been superseded by statistical and deep learning methods. This account captures part of the discipline's historical trajectory but misrepresents its contemporary status and substantially understates its enduring intellectual content. The conceptual core of classical AI is the recognition that *substantial intelligent behavior involves manipulating structured representations of knowledge and reasoning over those structures*, that *explicit representation makes certain kinds of inference and verification possible that distributed representations make difficult*, and that *intelligence is not a single phenomenon to be addressed by a single technique but a substantial collection of capabilities (reasoning, planning, knowledge integration, the various others) that may be best addressed by techniques specifically suited to each*. These recognitions remain substantially correct despite the contemporary dominance of statistical methods, and substantial portions of the classical AI tradition remain practically important and intellectually substantial.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Search* is the foundational subdiscipline concerned with finding solutions in structured spaces of possibilities — uninformed search algorithms (BFS, DFS, iterative deepening), informed search algorithms (A* and its variants, IDA*, the various heuristic search methods), the various forms of game-playing search (minimax with alpha-beta pruning, Monte Carlo tree search). Search is substantial in its own right and is foundational for substantial portions of the rest of classical AI. *Knowledge representation* is the subdiscipline concerned with how knowledge can be represented in structured form for computational use — propositional and first-order logic, the various description logics, frame and slot representations, semantic networks, the various ontology frameworks (RDF, OWL, the various others). Knowledge representation has substantial intellectual depth and remains practically important in substantial application areas. *Automated reasoning* is the subdiscipline concerned with deriving consequences from knowledge bases — theorem proving, model checking, satisfiability and constraint solving, the substantial work on tractable reasoning fragments. Contemporary automated reasoning is substantially active, with substantial applications in software verification, in circuit verification, in mathematics. *Planning* is the subdiscipline concerned with finding sequences of actions that achieve goals — classical planning, hierarchical task networks, planning under uncertainty, temporal planning. Planning has substantial historical depth and continues to develop, with substantial contemporary applications in robotics, in operations research, and in various forms of decision support. *Constraint satisfaction* is the subdiscipline concerned with finding assignments to variables that satisfy specified constraints — substantial overlap with search but with distinctive techniques and applications. *Knowledge graphs and semantic web* extend symbolic knowledge representation to the substantial industrial scale at which contemporary applications operate. *Neuro-symbolic AI* is the contemporary subdiscipline concerned with combining symbolic and neural methods — substantially active research that engages with the limitations of both pure approaches.

The intellectual content of classical AI is distinctive in several ways. The mathematical foundation in logic, in graph theory, in algorithms is substantial — substantial portions of classical AI are essentially algorithm engineering applied to specific problem structures. The connection to substantially established disciplines outside computer science is substantial: classical AI shares foundations with mathematical logic, with operations research, with linguistics (particularly through the substantial NLP work that preceded statistical NLP), with cognitive science (particularly through the symbolic cognitive science tradition). The engineering character is substantial in specific application domains — automated reasoning systems, planning systems, knowledge graph systems — that have substantial industrial deployment. The contemporary engagement with deep learning is substantial through the neuro-symbolic work, with substantial portions of contemporary AI safety and AI reasoning work treating symbolic methods as substantially relevant rather than as obsolete.

The reason this account matters is that the contemporary marginalization of classical AI in the standard machine learning curriculum produces practitioners with substantial blind spots. Substantial portions of practical AI work involve problems where symbolic methods are appropriate or essential — software verification, certain forms of reasoning, applications where explicit knowledge integration matters, the various contexts where the explainability of symbolic methods is required. Practitioners trained only in contemporary deep learning lack the conceptual context for engaging with these substantial application areas. Furthermore, the contemporary engagement with limitations of pure deep learning (the substantial discussions of reasoning failures in foundation models, the substantial work on neuro-symbolic integration, the various contemporary efforts to combine learned and symbolic components) cannot be productively pursued without substantial engagement with the classical AI tradition. The substantial investment in classical AI competence remains substantively valuable for practitioners working at the contemporary frontier, despite its non-canonical status in much contemporary curriculum.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Four problems organize classical AI as a discipline.

**The problem of representing knowledge for computational use.** A central question classical AI addresses is how knowledge can be represented in structured form that supports reasoning, integration with other knowledge, and various forms of computational use. The discipline includes substantial work on this question: the various logical formalisms (propositional logic, first-order logic, modal logics, the various description logics), the various structured representations (semantic networks, frames, the various ontology languages), the substantial work on knowledge engineering (the discipline of constructing knowledge bases). The intellectual content includes both foundational questions (what kinds of knowledge can be represented in what kinds of formalisms, what trade-offs different formalisms entail) and substantial engineering questions (how to construct large knowledge bases practically, how to maintain them as the world changes, how to integrate knowledge from diverse sources). The substantial contemporary work on knowledge graphs (Wikidata, the various enterprise knowledge graphs, the various biomedical knowledge graphs) is substantial application of this work to industrial scale. The contemporary engagement with knowledge representation in the context of foundation models — through retrieval-augmented generation, through structured prompting, through the various neuro-symbolic approaches — extends the picture into the contemporary context.

**The problem of efficient search through structured spaces.** Substantial AI problems can be formulated as search problems: finding a path through a graph, a sequence of moves through a game tree, a configuration that satisfies constraints, a sequence of actions that achieves a goal. The discipline includes substantial work on search: the foundational uninformed search algorithms (BFS, DFS, iterative deepening), the informed search algorithms (A* and its variants, IDA*, the various heuristic search methods), the substantial work on heuristic design (admissible and consistent heuristics, the various approaches to constructing heuristics automatically through abstraction and pattern databases), the substantial work on adversarial search for games (minimax with alpha-beta pruning, the various extensions, Monte Carlo tree search and its variants). The intellectual content includes both algorithmic depth (the various forms of analysis, the various theoretical results on completeness and optimality) and substantial practical work on what search techniques work for what problem structures. The substantial contemporary game-playing systems (AlphaGo, the various others) substantially integrate classical search techniques with learned components, making engagement with search foundational for understanding the contemporary work.

**The problem of automated reasoning.** Given a body of knowledge expressed in some formalism, deriving consequences automatically is genuinely difficult. The discipline includes substantial work on this question: theorem proving (resolution-based proving, tableau methods, natural deduction systems, the various others), model checking (the substantial body of techniques for verifying that systems satisfy specifications), satisfiability solving (the substantial SAT solver tradition, the various SMT extensions, the contemporary substantial work on industrial-scale solvers), constraint satisfaction (the various propagation techniques, the various search heuristics specific to constraint problems). The contemporary state of automated reasoning is substantial: industrial SAT and SMT solvers handle problems with millions of variables, contemporary theorem provers can prove substantial mathematical results, model checking has produced substantial industrial verification (notably in hardware verification). The intersection with formal methods (Section 3.4) is substantial, and the contemporary work substantially blurs the boundaries between AI and formal methods.

**The problem of decision-theoretic and planning reasoning.** A substantial portion of intelligence involves planning — choosing sequences of actions to achieve goals, taking into account the effects of actions, the constraints of the environment, the limited resources available, the substantial uncertainty about the world. The discipline includes substantial work on planning: classical planning (STRIPS-style planning and its successors, the substantial PDDL standardization, the various planning algorithms including state-space search, plan-space search, planning graph approaches like GraphPlan), hierarchical task network planning, planning under uncertainty (POMDP-based approaches with substantial connection to reinforcement learning), temporal planning, the various forms of multi-agent planning. The intellectual content includes both algorithmic depth and substantial conceptual content about what planning is and how it relates to reasoning more broadly. The substantial contemporary applications include robotics (where planning is integral), automated decision support, the various forms of operations research where AI planning techniques apply, and the various contemporary efforts to integrate planning with learned components.

### Where it sits in the map

Classical AI depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) is foundational, particularly the substantial graph algorithms and search algorithms that classical AI depends on. The theory of computation (Section 2.3) provides foundation for understanding what is and is not computationally tractable. Discrete mathematics (Section 2.2) and logic provide essential foundation, particularly for the symbolic logic and proof theory that classical AI uses substantially.

From the computation axis: the relationship to logic in computer science (Section 3.5) is substantial — classical AI uses substantially the same logical foundations that the broader logic-in-CS tradition uses. The relationship to formal methods (Section 3.4) is increasingly close — automated reasoning techniques developed in the AI tradition substantially overlap with the verification techniques used in formal methods, and contemporary work blurs the boundaries between AI and formal methods substantially. The relationship to programming language theory (Section 3.1) is also substantial through the connections to logic programming and through the various semantic approaches that classical AI uses.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): substantial mathematical preparation is required, particularly logic, graph theory, and discrete mathematics. The foundational mathematical work that supports classical AI is substantial and continues to develop.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: the relationship to machine learning foundations (Section 5.1), deep learning (Section 5.2), and foundation models (Section 5.3) is substantial in two distinct ways. Historically, classical AI and statistical/learning-based AI represented competing paradigms within artificial intelligence. Contemporarily, the relationship is substantially more integrative — the various neuro-symbolic approaches combine elements of both, the various contemporary applications of foundation models to reasoning tasks engage with the substantial limitations of pure foundation models on tasks that classical AI handles well, the substantial contemporary interest in using foundation models for theorem proving and program synthesis substantially intersects with classical AI techniques. The relationship to reinforcement learning (Section 5.4) has substantial historical depth — RL has substantial roots in the dynamic programming tradition that overlaps substantially with classical AI's planning work, and contemporary RL substantially incorporates classical search techniques (Monte Carlo tree search in AlphaGo and AlphaZero being a salient example).

From the broader CS context: the relationship to software verification (substantially through formal methods) is substantial. The relationship to operations research is substantial through planning and through constraint satisfaction. The relationship to databases (Section 4.2) is increasing through the work on knowledge graphs and through various forms of structured knowledge integration with database systems.

From outside computer science: the relationship to mathematical logic is substantial and foundational. The relationship to philosophy of mind and cognitive science is substantial — classical AI has substantial connections to symbolic theories of cognition, and the various philosophical debates about computational theories of mind substantially involve classical AI. The relationship to linguistics is substantial historically (the substantial classical NLP work that preceded statistical NLP).

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is on the intelligence axis, with substantial engagement on the computation axis (substantial theoretical content from logic and from algorithm theory), light engagement on the system axis (some substantial industrial systems, but not the systems-engineering character of foundation model infrastructure), and moderate engagement on the data axis (knowledge representation has substantial overlap with the data-structure concerns of the data axis).

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized classical AI and symbolic approaches acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners trained only in contemporary statistical and learning-based approaches.

The first is *fluency with structured representation*. The trained practitioner reasons naturally about structured representations — graphs, logical formulas, planning operators, knowledge bases — and can choose appropriate representations for specific problems. This fluency is substantially valuable in application areas where explicit knowledge integration matters and where the explainability of structured representations is required. The capacity is acquired through substantial practice with the various symbolic representations and through engagement with the conceptual content that the various representations encode.

The second capacity is *fluency with search*. The trained practitioner is fluent with the major search algorithms, can analyze search problems to determine what techniques are appropriate, can design heuristics for specific problems, can implement and debug search algorithms. This fluency transfers substantially: substantial portions of practical computing involve search problems in disguise, and engineers with strong search foundations can recognize and address them productively. The substantial classical literature on search remains substantially valuable as preparation, despite the contemporary statistical methods that have displaced it in some application areas.

The third capacity is *fluency with logical reasoning*. The trained practitioner can read and write formal logical specifications, can reason about logical consequence, can use the various automated reasoning tools (SAT solvers, SMT solvers, theorem provers) productively. This logical fluency is foundational and broadly applicable, transferring across specific AI applications and remaining relevant for substantial portions of computer science work that goes beyond AI narrowly understood.

The fourth capacity is *the disposition to think about problems decompositionally*. Classical AI substantially depends on decomposing problems into structured parts and reasoning about each part separately, in contrast to the more end-to-end approach that contemporary deep learning emphasizes. The trained practitioner brings this decompositional disposition to problems, recognizes when decompositional approaches are appropriate, and can engage with the substantial design work that decomposition requires. This disposition produces engineering substantially different from end-to-end approaches and remains valuable for substantial portions of practical work.

The fifth capacity is *the historical and conceptual perspective on AI*. The trained practitioner who has engaged with classical AI has substantial historical and conceptual context for understanding contemporary AI. This perspective recognizes that contemporary deep learning is one phase in a longer-developing field, that earlier paradigms had substantial successes and failures that the contemporary work can learn from, that the limitations of contemporary deep learning are sometimes addressed by classical techniques and that the integration of approaches may be more productive than the dominance of any single paradigm. The perspective distinguishes practitioners who engage with AI as substantial intellectual discipline from practitioners who engage with it as collection of contemporary techniques.

The sixth capacity is *the ability to engage with neuro-symbolic and hybrid approaches*. The substantial contemporary work on combining symbolic and neural methods requires substantial preparation in both. Practitioners who have substantial classical AI preparation alongside their deep learning preparation can engage with this contemporary work productively, while practitioners with only one preparation cannot. The contemporary direction of substantial portions of AI research toward hybrid approaches makes this capacity increasingly valuable.

### How the field sees the world

Classical AI embeds a worldview that is distinctive among intelligence-axis subjects and that contrasts substantially with the worldview of contemporary statistical and learning-based AI.

The first commitment is *to explicit representation as substantively important*. The classical AI worldview holds that representing knowledge explicitly — in logical formulas, in structured representations, in the various symbolic forms — is substantively important rather than incidental implementation detail. This commitment runs against the contemporary deep learning view that learned distributed representations are sufficient. The classical AI practitioner takes a different view: there are properties of explicit representations (inspectability, modularity, the ability to reason about them with the substantial machinery of logic, the ability to integrate them across diverse sources) that distributed representations do not provide and that matter for substantial application areas. The disposition has substantial consequences for system design and produces engineering substantially different from end-to-end neural approaches.

The second commitment is *to compositionality and systematicity*. The classical AI worldview holds that intelligence substantially involves compositionality — combining substantial structures from simpler parts in systematic ways — and that this compositionality is substantially better captured by symbolic methods than by distributed neural representations. The argument has substantial intellectual depth, with substantial connection to the cognitive science debates about compositionality and to the various philosophical debates about the nature of mental representation. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat compositionality as substantive engineering concern and recognize when problems have substantial compositional structure that benefits from explicit treatment.

The third commitment is *to reasoning as central to intelligence*. The classical AI worldview holds that reasoning — deriving consequences from knowledge, planning sequences of actions, the various forms of inference — is central to intelligence rather than peripheral to it. This commitment runs in some tension with contemporary views that emphasize learning over reasoning, but it remains substantively defended particularly given the documented reasoning limitations of contemporary foundation models. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat reasoning as primary subject of study and engineering, with substantial machinery for reasoning explicitly rather than relying on reasoning emerging implicitly from learning.

The fourth commitment is *to the value of formal foundations*. The classical AI worldview holds that the substantial mathematical foundations of the discipline (logic, graph theory, algorithm analysis) provide substantial value — both for what they enable and for the rigor they impose. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer empirical methodology over formal foundations. The classical AI practitioner takes a different view: the formal foundations enable various forms of analysis (correctness, completeness, complexity) that empirical methodology cannot provide, and that these analyses matter for substantial application areas. The disposition produces engineering with substantial formal grounding and connects classical AI to the broader theoretical computer science tradition.

The fifth commitment is *to the modular construction of intelligent systems*. The classical AI worldview holds that intelligent systems should be constructed from substantial modules with clear interfaces — knowledge bases, inference engines, planning systems, the various others — rather than as monolithic learned systems. This commitment runs against the contemporary preference for end-to-end learning. The classical AI practitioner takes a different view: modular construction enables substantial engineering practices (testing modules independently, replacing modules as better approaches develop, integrating knowledge from diverse sources) that monolithic approaches cannot support. The disposition produces engineering substantially different from end-to-end approaches.

The sixth commitment is *to the long-term durability of foundational ideas*. The classical AI worldview takes seriously that the substantial intellectual content of the discipline has substantial durability — that the foundational ideas (search, logical reasoning, planning, knowledge representation) have remained substantively important for decades and will continue to be important regardless of what specific techniques are currently fashionable. This commitment runs against the contemporary tendency to treat AI primarily through the lens of currently dominant techniques. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment invest in foundational understanding alongside contemporary techniques, recognizing that the foundations transfer more reliably than specific contemporary approaches.

The seventh commitment is *to honest engagement with the limitations of pure approaches*. The classical AI worldview, particularly in its contemporary form, takes seriously that neither pure symbolic approaches nor pure learning-based approaches are adequate for substantial portions of intelligent behavior. Contemporary classical AI practitioners (particularly those working on neuro-symbolic methods) treat the integration of approaches as substantive engineering concern and recognize that the limitations of each approach are addressed by combining them productively. This commitment runs against partisan views that advocate for one approach over the other and produces engineering substantially more thoughtful than the alternatives.

These seven commitments — to explicit representation, to compositionality, to reasoning as central, to formal foundations, to modular construction, to durability of foundations, and to honest engagement with limitations — distinguish the classical AI worldview from the contemporary deep learning mainstream. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in classical AI as serious intellectual practice rather than as historical curiosity.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning classical AI are characteristic and substantially distinct from the pitfalls of contemporary deep learning study.

The most common pitfall is *the obsolescence misconception*. The contemporary marginalization of classical AI in mainstream curriculum produces learners who treat the field as obsolete and not worth substantial engagement. The result is practitioners who lack the conceptual context for engaging with substantial application areas where classical methods remain dominant or essential, who cannot productively engage with the contemporary neuro-symbolic work, and who are limited to the contemporary deep learning paradigm without the broader context that classical AI provides. The countermeasure is to engage with classical AI on its merits rather than through the lens of contemporary fashion: to recognize that the substantial intellectual content of the field has substantial durability, that substantial application areas continue to depend on classical methods, and that the contemporary direction of AI substantially involves integration of classical and learning-based approaches.

A second pitfall is *the pure-AI-history misunderstanding*. Some learners engage with classical AI primarily as historical study, reading the foundational papers and books as historical artifacts rather than as substantive technical content. The result is conceptual familiarity without practical engagement: learners who can describe what classical AI was but cannot productively use classical AI techniques. The countermeasure is to engage with classical AI as ongoing technical practice: implementing search algorithms, working with logical formalisms, using contemporary classical AI tools (modern SAT solvers, modern planners, modern theorem provers), and engaging with the contemporary literature in addition to the historical literature.

A third pitfall is *the symbolic-versus-neural false dichotomy*. The historical opposition between symbolic and statistical approaches to AI has produced learners who treat the approaches as incompatible alternatives, requiring choice between them. The contemporary practice is substantially more integrative: substantial portions of contemporary AI work productively combine symbolic and learning-based components, and the various neuro-symbolic approaches are substantively active research. The countermeasure is to engage with both traditions and to study the integration explicitly: the substantial contemporary literature on neuro-symbolic AI, the various papers on combining learned and symbolic components, the various practical systems that integrate the approaches productively.

A fourth pitfall is *the toy-problem confinement*. Substantial introductory classical AI material focuses on toy problems — small puzzles, small game positions, small knowledge bases — that illustrate the algorithms but do not represent the contemporary state of the field. Learners can come to associate classical AI exclusively with these toy contexts, missing the substantial contemporary work on industrial-scale classical AI (SAT solvers handling millions of variables, planners handling substantial industrial planning problems, knowledge graphs at scale). The countermeasure is to engage with contemporary industrial-scale classical AI: to study contemporary SAT and SMT solvers, to engage with contemporary planning competitions and the substantial work that produces, to use contemporary knowledge graph systems at substantial scale.

A fifth pitfall is *the underestimation of mathematical preparation required*. Classical AI has substantial mathematical content — particularly logic, graph theory, and algorithm analysis — and learners who underestimate this preparation produce work substantially limited by mathematical gaps. The countermeasure is to engage with the mathematical foundations seriously: to study mathematical logic substantively, to engage with the substantial graph theory that classical AI uses, to study algorithm analysis with the depth that classical AI's substantial algorithmic content requires.

A sixth pitfall is *the heuristic-design-as-art misconception*. Substantial portions of practical classical AI involve designing heuristics — for search, for constraint satisfaction, for the various other applications where heuristic guidance matters. Learners can come to treat heuristic design as art rather than as engineering, with the result that their heuristic design is unsystematic and fails on problems where the standard heuristics do not apply. The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial body of work on systematic heuristic design: the work on admissible and consistent heuristics, the various approaches to automatic heuristic generation through abstraction and pattern databases, the various theoretical results on heuristic quality. Heuristic design has substantial intellectual content that systematic engagement makes accessible.

A seventh pitfall is *the contemporary-tools-avoidance trap*. Substantial portions of classical AI literature emphasize foundational concepts and historical algorithms, with limited attention to the substantial contemporary tools that practitioners actually use (modern SAT solvers like Z3 and Glucose, modern planners like Fast Downward, modern theorem provers like Coq and Lean, modern knowledge graph systems). Learners can come to know the foundational concepts without fluency with the contemporary tools that put the concepts into practice. The countermeasure is to engage with contemporary tools substantively: to learn to use Z3, to work through Coq or Lean tutorials, to use contemporary planning systems, to recognize that contemporary classical AI is practiced through these tools.

An eighth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied specifically to classical AI. Modern AI tools can produce code for many classical AI exercises, can answer questions about classical AI concepts, and can guide learners through standard exercises. The cost is substantial in classical AI because the discipline depends substantially on internalized understanding of how the various algorithms work, on the cognitive struggle of working through proofs and analyses, on the development of intuition through hands-on work with the algorithms. Learners who use LLMs to bypass this development do not develop the capacities that classical AI requires. The countermeasure is to do early classical AI work without LLM assistance, even when assistance is available, and to use AI tools as supplements to direct engagement only after the foundational competence is established.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on classical AI is substantial, well-developed, and contains several books that have achieved substantial canonical status. The literature has substantially stabilized — the foundational texts have been reasonably durable — though the contemporary work on knowledge graphs, on neuro-symbolic AI, and on the various contemporary applications produces substantial recent literature that complements the foundational material.

#### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text for AI broadly, including substantial classical AI content, is Russell and Norvig's *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (fourth edition, 2020). The book is comprehensive across AI rather than focused exclusively on classical approaches, but its coverage of search, logical reasoning, knowledge representation, and planning is substantial and authoritative. The book has been the standard AI textbook for nearly three decades and substantially defines what counts as the canonical AI curriculum. For learners pursuing classical AI, the relevant chapters of Russell and Norvig provide the standard foundational coverage. The fourth edition substantially updates the earlier editions to cover contemporary developments while maintaining the substantial classical AI coverage.

For search specifically, Russell and Norvig's coverage is substantial, but Pearl's *Heuristics: Intelligent Search Strategies for Computer Problem Solving* (1984) provides foundational depth on heuristic search that remains substantively valuable. The book is older but its treatment of heuristic search concepts has substantial enduring value.

For knowledge representation specifically, Brachman and Levesque's *Knowledge Representation and Reasoning* (2004) is the standard text. The book provides substantial coverage of the various knowledge representation formalisms with appropriate depth. Sowa's *Knowledge Representation: Logical, Philosophical, and Computational Foundations* (2000) provides broader treatment that connects knowledge representation to its philosophical and logical foundations.

For automated reasoning specifically, Russell and Norvig provides substantial introduction. For depth, the various more specialized texts are appropriate: Robinson and Voronkov's *Handbook of Automated Reasoning* (2001) is the substantial reference work for the field. Bradley and Manna's *The Calculus of Computation: Decision Procedures with Applications to Verification* covers the contemporary decision procedure work that connects to formal methods.

For planning specifically, Ghallab, Nau, and Traverso's *Automated Planning: Theory and Practice* (2004) is the standard text, with the more recent *Automated Planning and Acting* (2016) providing contemporary update.

For SAT and constraint satisfaction specifically, Biere, Heule, van Maaren, and Walsh's *Handbook of Satisfiability* (second edition, 2021) is the substantial reference for contemporary SAT solving. Rossi, van Beek, and Walsh's *Handbook of Constraint Programming* (2006) is the substantial reference for constraint programming.

The reading-order recommendation: Russell and Norvig as the foundational text covering the breadth of classical AI, supplemented by the more specialized texts as specific subareas warrant deeper study. The associated course materials from major universities (Stanford CS221, Berkeley CS188, MIT 6.034, the various others) provide substantial pedagogical complement.

The Stanford and Berkeley AI courses (substantially associated with Russell and Norvig and with the broader academic AI tradition) are freely available and provide substantial pedagogical structure for working through the classical AI material.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in classical AI branches according to specialization.

*Search at depth.* For learners pursuing search specifically, beyond the material in Russell and Norvig and Pearl, the substantial contemporary literature on heuristic search is relevant. The various papers on pattern databases, on abstraction-based heuristics, on the various forms of meta-reasoning about search constitute substantial contemporary literature. Korf's various papers on heuristic search remain substantively important. The substantial contemporary work on Monte Carlo tree search (Browne et al.'s "Survey of Monte Carlo Tree Search Methods" and the various subsequent work) extends the search tradition into contemporary applications.

*Logic and automated reasoning at depth.* For learners pursuing automated reasoning specifically, the foundational literature is substantial. Chang and Lee's *Symbolic Logic and Mechanical Theorem Proving* (1973) is the foundational text for resolution-based theorem proving. Bibel's *Automated Theorem Proving* covers similar territory with different emphasis. The various contemporary papers on theorem proving extend the picture into contemporary research. For SMT solving specifically, Kroening and Strichman's *Decision Procedures: An Algorithmic Point of View* (second edition, 2016) is the standard contemporary text. The Z3 documentation and tutorials provide substantial practical reference. The contemporary Lean theorem prover documentation and tutorials cover the contemporary functional theorem proving direction.

*Knowledge representation at depth.* For depth on knowledge representation, the substantial description logic literature is relevant. Baader, Calvanese, McGuinness, Nardi, and Patel-Schneider's *The Description Logic Handbook* (second edition, 2007) is the standard reference. The various ontology engineering literature (Gómez-Pérez, Fernández-López, and Corcho's *Ontological Engineering*, the various others) covers the engineering practice. The substantial contemporary work on knowledge graphs (Hogan et al.'s "Knowledge Graphs" survey, the various papers on contemporary knowledge graph systems) extends the picture into industrial contemporary practice.

*Planning at depth.* For planning specifically, beyond Ghallab, Nau, and Traverso, the substantial planning research literature is relevant. The various papers from the International Planning Competitions document the contemporary state of automated planning. Hoffmann's various papers on planning heuristics, the various papers on partial-order planning, the various papers on hierarchical task network planning, the various papers on planning under uncertainty all extend the picture. The PDDL specification and the various PDDL extensions constitute the standard input language for contemporary planning systems.

*Constraint satisfaction at depth.* Beyond the constraint programming handbook, the substantial CP and CSP literature includes substantial papers on specific solving techniques (the various propagation algorithms, the various forms of search heuristics specific to constraint problems, the various global constraints), substantial papers on industrial applications (scheduling, configuration, the various others), and substantial papers on the relationship between CP and SAT.

*Game playing.* The substantial literature on game-playing AI is foundational and continues. Shannon's foundational paper on chess, the various computer chess papers, the various Go AI papers (culminating in AlphaGo and AlphaZero), the various other game-playing literature constitutes substantial historical and contemporary material. Browne et al.'s MCTS survey is foundational for the contemporary tree search work. The substantial AlphaGo and AlphaZero papers (Silver et al.) substantially integrate classical search with deep learning, and engagement with this work productively connects classical AI with contemporary deep learning.

*Logic programming.* Logic programming represents a distinctive thread in classical AI with substantial intellectual content. Lloyd's *Foundations of Logic Programming* (second edition, 1987) is the foundational mathematical text. The various Prolog books (Sterling and Shapiro's *The Art of Prolog*, the various others) cover the practical programming language. The contemporary Answer Set Programming work (substantial through Gebser, Kaminski, Kaufmann, and Schaub's *Answer Set Solving in Practice*) extends logic programming into contemporary form. The substantial contemporary work on Datalog (the various Datalog research, the various industrial applications) connects logic programming to database systems and to substantial industrial practice.

*Neuro-symbolic AI.* The contemporary neuro-symbolic literature is rapidly developing. The various surveys (Garcez et al.'s "Neural-Symbolic Computing: An Effective Methodology for Principled Integration of Machine Learning and Reasoning", the various subsequent surveys) provide synthesis. The various specific neuro-symbolic systems papers (the various Logic Tensor Networks papers, the various Neural Theorem Prover papers, the various Differentiable Logic papers) constitute the contemporary research literature. The contemporary work on using foundation models for theorem proving and program synthesis substantially intersects with this literature.

*Cognitive architecture.* The substantial work on cognitive architectures — comprehensive computational frameworks intended to model cognition — provides perspective on classical AI's broader aspirations. SOAR (Newell, Laird, Rosenbloom and successors) is the substantial historical example. ACT-R (Anderson and successors) provides cognitive-science-grounded architecture. The various contemporary cognitive architecture work continues this tradition. Engagement with cognitive architectures gives perspective on what comprehensive symbolic AI was and is attempting.

#### Reference works

Russell and Norvig's *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* is the standard reference work that practitioners consult throughout their careers, particularly for the breadth of classical AI material it covers.

The Z3 documentation and tutorials are the substantial reference for the contemporary dominant SMT solver.

The Coq and Lean documentation and tutorials are the substantial references for the contemporary functional theorem provers.

The various PDDL documentation and the planning competition materials are reference for contemporary automated planning.

The W3C semantic web standards (RDF, RDFS, OWL, SPARQL) constitute reference for the standardized knowledge representation languages.

Wikidata, DBpedia, and the various open knowledge graphs constitute substantial reference for what large-scale knowledge graphs look like in practice.

The various contemporary survey papers in classical AI subareas (the SAT handbook, the constraint programming handbook, the description logic handbook, the various others) constitute substantial reference for the various subdisciplines.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within the classical AI tradition and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The connectionist counter-tradition.* The substantial connectionist tradition (originating in the 1940s and 1950s, suppressed in the 1970s, revived in the 1980s, and substantially dominant today as deep learning) represents the substantial alternative to classical symbolic AI. The various foundational connectionist works (Rumelhart and McClelland's *Parallel Distributed Processing*, the various subsequent works) constitute substantial alternative literature. The historical opposition between symbolic and connectionist approaches has been substantially productive, and engagement with both traditions gives perspective on what each does well and poorly.

*The probabilistic / Bayesian challenge to symbolic AI.* The substantial probabilistic tradition (substantially through Pearl's work on belief networks and on causal reasoning, through the broader probabilistic graphical models literature) represents an alternative direction within AI that sits between symbolic and pure neural approaches. Pearl's *Probabilistic Reasoning in Intelligent Systems* (1988) and *Causality* (second edition, 2009) are foundational. The probabilistic tradition has substantial proponents and substantial argument that purely symbolic methods inadequately handle uncertainty.

*The embodiment / situated cognition critique.* The substantial body of work in robotics and embodied cognition (substantially associated with Brooks's "Intelligence Without Reason" and the broader behavior-based robotics tradition) provides substantial critique of classical AI's substantial focus on disembodied symbolic reasoning. The argument is that intelligence is fundamentally situated and embodied rather than abstract symbol manipulation, and that classical AI's focus produces systems that work only in artificial laboratory settings. The position has substantial proponents and substantial intellectual depth.

*The fast-and-frugal heuristics tradition.* The substantial work in cognitive science on fast-and-frugal heuristics (substantially associated with Gigerenzer and the ABC research group) provides alternative perspective on how intelligence might work — through simple heuristics that exploit environmental structure rather than through elaborate symbolic reasoning. The argument has substantial intellectual content and provides perspective on whether classical AI's complexity is essential or excessive.

*The skeptical perspective on contemporary AI's neglect of classical methods.* Various practitioners argue that contemporary AI's substantial neglect of classical methods is substantively problematic — that substantial classical methods address concerns that contemporary deep learning addresses poorly, that the substantial historical lessons of classical AI are being forgotten, that the contemporary practice is partly reinventing classical concepts in inferior form. Marcus's various writings make versions of these arguments. Engagement with these critical perspectives gives perspective on what the contemporary mainstream may be missing.

#### What to skip and why

Most older books on AI that predate Russell and Norvig (the various 1970s and 1980s AI textbooks) should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The field has moved substantially, and the contemporary references are more reliable for current learning.

Most marketing-influenced books on "AI" that conflate classical AI with broader claims about artificial intelligence should generally be skipped.

Books on specific 1980s expert system technologies should be approached as historical references. The expert system era produced substantial literature, much of which is dated for contemporary practice.

Books on AI that promise to teach the field "without programming" or "without math" should be approached with substantial skepticism. Classical AI has substantial mathematical and algorithmic content that serious practice requires.

Most online tutorials on classical AI topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained materials from major universities, the substantial documentation from contemporary tools (Z3, Coq, Lean, the various others), the substantial talks from prominent researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth.

Books that present classical AI as exclusively historical (treating the field as concluded rather than ongoing) substantially miss the contemporary work and should be supplemented with current literature.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary classical AI research include AAAI (the substantial AI conference covering the breadth of AI including classical methods), IJCAI (the International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence), the more specialized conferences (KR for knowledge representation, ICAPS for planning, CP for constraint programming, SAT for satisfiability solving, CADE and IJCAR for automated reasoning, the various others). These conferences' proceedings constitute substantial contemporary research literature.

The various journals (Artificial Intelligence, Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, the various others) provide substantial peer-reviewed venue for AI research including classical methods.

The arXiv repository covers classical AI research in the cs.AI, cs.LO, and various other categories.

Several contemporary practitioner resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The various blogs and writings from researchers in classical AI subareas (the various SAT solver developers' writings, the various planning researchers, the various knowledge graph practitioners) provide ongoing perspective. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's articles on AI topics provide substantial scholarly synthesis on conceptual issues.

The various competition results (SAT competitions, planning competitions, theorem proving competitions, the various others) provide ongoing benchmarks for the contemporary state of various classical AI subdisciplines.

The contemporary neuro-symbolic AI workshops at major conferences (NeurIPS, AAAI, ICML) provide ongoing venue for the substantial contemporary work integrating symbolic and neural approaches.

The contemporary work on AI for theorem proving (substantially through Lean's mathematical library development, through the various AI-assisted theorem proving systems, through the substantial intersection of foundation models with formal mathematics) constitutes substantial ongoing work that bridges classical AI and contemporary deep learning.
