### A note on contemporary relevance

Classical AI is among the more contemporarily complex subjects on the intelligence axis from the perspective of relevance — the field has substantial contemporary relevance in specific application areas while being substantially marginalized in mainstream AI curriculum, and the contemporary trajectory involves substantial integration with the dominant deep learning paradigm.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial maturation of industrial-scale automated reasoning. Contemporary SAT and SMT solvers handle substantial industrial problems with millions of variables, contemporary theorem provers verify substantial software and hardware systems, contemporary automated planning handles substantial industrial planning problems. The trajectory suggests continued maturation, with substantial industrial deployment of these technologies in contexts where their substantial advantages over learning-based approaches (correctness guarantees, explainability, integration with formal specifications) matter.

The second major development is the substantial growth of knowledge graphs as industrial infrastructure. Contemporary knowledge graphs at substantial industrial scale (Wikidata, the various enterprise knowledge graphs from Google, Amazon, the various others, the substantial biomedical knowledge graphs) constitute substantial deployment of classical AI knowledge representation at scale. The trajectory suggests continued substantial knowledge graph development, with substantial implications for various application areas.

The third development is the substantial growth of neuro-symbolic AI as research direction. The substantial limitations of pure deep learning approaches — particularly on tasks involving substantial reasoning, on tasks requiring substantial knowledge integration, on tasks where substantial guarantees about behavior matter — have produced substantial interest in combining symbolic and neural methods. The contemporary neuro-symbolic literature is substantial and rapidly developing, and the trajectory suggests continued growth.

The fourth development is the substantial application of foundation models to classical AI tasks. Contemporary foundation models are increasingly applied to substantially classical AI tasks: theorem proving (substantially through projects like AlphaProof and the various Lean-foundation-model integrations), program synthesis, planning, the various others. The substantial empirical successes of these applications — on tasks that classical AI struggled with for decades — represent substantial contemporary developments. The trajectory suggests continued integration of foundation models with classical AI tasks, with substantial implications for both fields.

The fifth development is the substantial reckoning with foundation models' reasoning limitations. The substantial documented limitations of contemporary foundation models on tasks requiring substantial reasoning have produced substantial interest in classical AI approaches that handle reasoning explicitly. The various contemporary approaches that supplement foundation models with classical reasoning components — the various retrieval-augmented generation systems incorporating reasoning, the various agent architectures that include classical planners or theorem provers as tools, the various others — represent substantial contemporary developments. The trajectory suggests continued substantial integration of classical reasoning with foundation models.

The sixth development is the substantial growth of formal verification of AI systems. The substantial deployment of AI systems in consequential contexts has produced substantial interest in formal verification of these systems, with substantial overlap between the formal methods tradition (Section 3.4) and the classical AI tradition. The trajectory suggests continued substantial work at this intersection.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of classical AI is substantially robust. The core concerns — knowledge representation, search, automated reasoning, planning — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to classical AI: the discipline has experienced substantial cycles of attention through its history — substantial enthusiasm in the 1950s-1960s, partial retreat in the 1970s, substantial enthusiasm in the 1980s expert system era, substantial retreat in the 1990s as statistical methods rose, partial revival in the contemporary period. The cyclical pattern suggests that practitioners should engage with the discipline on its substantial merits rather than as response to current attention. The substantial intellectual content of classical AI — its mathematical rigor, its substantial connection to logic and algorithm theory, its substantial application areas where it remains dominant — has substantial enduring value regardless of current attention levels. Learners building durable AI competence benefit substantially from engagement with classical AI alongside the contemporary deep learning material, recognizing that the substantial integration of approaches is increasingly important and that practitioners with both preparations have substantial advantages over practitioners with only one.

A second observation specific to this subject: classical AI is unusual among contemporary CS subjects in the substantial gap between what mainstream curriculum teaches and what serious contemporary practice in specific application areas requires. Practitioners working in software verification, in planning-based applications, in knowledge-graph-based industrial systems, in the various reasoning-intensive contexts substantially require classical AI competence that mainstream machine-learning-focused curricula do not provide. The substantial investment in classical AI competence is substantially valuable for practitioners working in these areas, even though the investment is non-canonical from the perspective of contemporary mainstream AI curriculum. The judgment of whether to invest in classical AI competence depends substantially on the practitioner's specific career trajectory and on which application areas the practitioner expects to work in, with substantial investment warranted for areas where classical AI remains essential and lighter engagement appropriate for practitioners working primarily in contemporary deep learning contexts.
## 5.6 — Mechanistic Interpretability

### What it is, properly

Mechanistic interpretability is the discipline concerned with understanding what trained neural networks actually do internally — at the level of individual neurons, circuits, attention patterns, and the various other internal structures — rather than treating them as black boxes characterized only by input-output behavior. The subject treats neural networks as substantial computational artifacts whose internal mechanisms can be studied empirically, with characteristic methods drawn from neuroscience, from reverse engineering, and from the various forms of empirical investigation, and with the characteristic aim of producing genuine understanding rather than only summary statistics. The discipline has emerged primarily over the past decade, with substantial foundational work appearing through Distill publications around 2017-2020, substantial subsequent development through the work of Anthropic's interpretability team and various academic groups, and substantial contemporary growth driven by the substantial deployment of large foundation models and the corresponding substantial interest in understanding what these models actually do.

The conventional account treats interpretability as the practice of making model decisions "explainable" — producing outputs alongside model predictions that supposedly explain why the model made those predictions, typically through saliency maps, attention visualization, or various other surface-level techniques. This account captures part of what is sometimes called interpretability research but understates substantially what mechanistic interpretability has become as an intellectual subject. The conceptual core of mechanistic interpretability is the recognition that *trained networks contain substantial internal structure that can be reverse-engineered through careful empirical investigation*, that *such reverse engineering can produce genuine understanding of how networks compute their outputs rather than only post-hoc rationalizations*, that *the substantial internal mechanisms involve substantial conceptual content (features, circuits, representations) that can be characterized substantively*, and that *understanding model internals matters substantively for the substantial deployment contexts where model behavior must be reliable, safe, and predictable*. These recognitions distinguish mechanistic interpretability from the broader interpretability literature, which contains substantial work that makes weaker claims about what interpretability methods actually reveal.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Feature visualization and analysis* is the foundational subdiscipline concerned with what individual neurons and groups of neurons represent — the substantial work on visualizing what features convolutional network neurons detect, the various techniques for characterizing what features mean substantively, the substantial work on the substantial diversity of features that emerge in training. *Circuit analysis* extends feature analysis to the connections among features, identifying substantial functional circuits that compute specific aspects of network behavior — substantial work on circuits in image classifiers, on circuits in language models, on the various forms of circuit characterization. *Mechanistic analysis of attention and transformers* is the substantial subdiscipline concerned specifically with understanding the transformer architecture that dominates contemporary deep learning — the various work on attention patterns, on residual stream interactions, on the substantial work characterizing what transformer components compute. *Sparse autoencoders and dictionary learning* is the contemporary subdiscipline concerned with addressing the substantial problem of polysemanticity (where individual neurons represent multiple features simultaneously) through training auxiliary networks that decompose neural activity into more interpretable components. *Causal intervention and circuit-level testing* is the methodological subdiscipline concerned with verifying interpretability claims through causal interventions on model internals — substantial work on activation patching, on causal scrubbing, on the various forms of circuit verification. *Behavioral interpretability and probing* is the broader subdiscipline concerned with characterizing what models can and cannot do through behavioral tests — substantially distinct from but complementary to mechanistic work. *Representational analysis* is the broader subdiscipline concerned with what representations emerge in trained networks, with substantial overlap with mechanistic interpretability proper but with somewhat different emphasis on representational geometry rather than on specific computational mechanisms.

The intellectual content of mechanistic interpretability is distinctive in several ways. The empirical character is unusually strong — even more so than the broader deep learning empirical character, substantial portions of what is known about model internals come from careful empirical investigation that lacks adequate theoretical framework. The methodological care required is substantial — interpretability claims are notoriously easy to make and notoriously easy to make incorrectly, and the discipline has developed substantial methodological sophistication around what counts as evidence for interpretability claims. The connection to neuroscience is substantial historically (substantial vocabulary and approaches drawn from neuroscience) and substantively contested (the question of whether artificial neural networks and biological neural networks are sufficiently similar that neuroscience methods transfer is substantially open). The contemporary growth has been substantial, with the substantial deployment of foundation models producing substantial demand for understanding what these models do internally and the substantial alignment-related work producing substantial demand for safety-relevant interpretability specifically.

The reason this account matters is that mechanistic interpretability has become substantially central to the contemporary AI safety effort and substantially relevant to the broader deployment of AI systems. The substantial deployment of foundation models in consequential contexts produces substantial demand for understanding what these models do internally — both for reliability concerns (understanding why models fail when they do) and for safety concerns (understanding whether models are doing what users intend or have learned undesirable behaviors that might emerge in deployment). The discipline of mechanistic interpretability as serious empirical practice differs substantially from the surface-level interpretability work that produces saliency maps without engaging with what models actually compute, and the difference matters substantially for whether interpretability work produces genuine understanding or only rhetorical reassurance.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Four problems organize mechanistic interpretability as a discipline.

**The problem of what features models represent.** A central question mechanistic interpretability addresses is what features individual neurons (and groups of neurons) detect or represent in trained networks. The discipline includes substantial work on this question: the foundational visualization work showing that early convolutional network layers detect edges and gradients while later layers detect substantial higher-level features (Zeiler and Fergus, the various subsequent work), the substantial subsequent work characterizing features in increasingly substantial detail (the Distill Circuits work on visual features, the various work on language model features), the substantial contemporary work on sparse autoencoders attempting to decompose neural activity into more interpretable feature dictionaries. The intellectual content includes both methodological questions (how to verify that a proposed feature description accurately characterizes what a neuron does) and substantive questions (what features actually emerge in different architectures trained on different data, why these features rather than others, how features compose to produce network outputs). The contemporary work on monosemantic features (Anthropic's substantial work, the various academic work on similar approaches) addresses the substantial polysemanticity problem where individual neurons represent multiple features superposed in ways that complicate interpretation.

**The problem of how features combine into circuits.** Beyond identifying individual features, substantial mechanistic interpretability work addresses how features combine into circuits that compute specific aspects of network behavior. The discipline includes substantial work on circuit analysis: the foundational Distill work on circuits in image classifiers (curve detectors, the various other circuits identified through careful empirical work), the substantial subsequent work on circuits in language models (induction heads as a foundational example, the various other circuits identified through similar methods), the contemporary work on substantial circuit-level analysis of substantial portions of model behavior. The intellectual content includes both methodological development (how to identify circuits reliably, how to verify circuit claims through causal intervention) and substantive findings (what circuits exist in trained networks, what they compute, how they compose). The induction heads paper from Anthropic and the substantial subsequent work on transformer circuits constitute substantial foundational literature for this subdiscipline.

**The problem of causal verification.** Interpretability claims are notoriously easy to make and notoriously easy to make incorrectly. A substantial methodological challenge of mechanistic interpretability is establishing that claimed mechanisms actually drive model behavior rather than being post-hoc rationalizations of correlations that do not reflect causal structure. The discipline includes substantial work on this methodological problem: activation patching (replacing activations in one forward pass with activations from another pass to test whether specific components matter for specific behaviors), causal scrubbing (a more systematic approach to verifying mechanistic claims through targeted interventions), the various other forms of causal intervention. The substantial methodological development has produced substantial improvements in the rigor of interpretability claims over the past several years, though substantial methodological challenges remain. The work on causal verification is substantially what distinguishes serious mechanistic interpretability from less rigorous interpretability work that makes claims without verification.

**The problem of scaling interpretability to substantial models.** The substantial size of contemporary foundation models produces substantial challenges for interpretability work. Methods that work on small toy models often do not transfer to substantial production models; the substantial number of parameters, the substantial number of features per layer, and the substantial complexity of computations make exhaustive analysis infeasible. The discipline includes substantial work on scaling: the substantial work on sparse autoencoders specifically aims at extracting interpretable features from substantial models efficiently, the various efforts at automated circuit discovery aim at identifying circuits without substantial human effort per circuit, the various efforts at hierarchical interpretation aim at understanding models at multiple levels of abstraction simultaneously. The contemporary work substantially focuses on this scaling problem, with substantial implications for whether interpretability can keep pace with the substantial scale of contemporary models. The trajectory in this area is substantial, with substantial advances over the past several years, but substantial questions remain about whether the contemporary methods will scale to the substantial models being deployed.

### Where it sits in the map

Mechanistic interpretability depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the deep learning material (Section 5.2): substantial preparation is required. Mechanistic interpretability is substantially the empirical study of trained neural networks, and substantial fluency with neural network architectures, training procedures, and the substantial deep learning practice is essential. Practitioners attempting interpretability without substantial deep learning preparation operate at substantial disadvantage.

From the foundation models material (Section 5.3): substantial preparation is required for serious work on interpretability of contemporary foundation models, which constitutes substantial portions of contemporary interpretability research. Understanding transformers, understanding pretraining, understanding the substantial contemporary fine-tuning practices is essential for productive engagement with contemporary mechanistic interpretability work.

From the machine learning foundations material (Section 5.1): the foundational mathematical preparation is required, particularly probability theory, statistics, and linear algebra. Mechanistic interpretability uses substantial linear algebra (substantial portions of contemporary mechanistic work involves operations on activation vectors), uses substantial statistics (causal verification depends on appropriate statistical methodology), and uses substantial probability theory throughout.

From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation, particularly for the various computational tools that interpretability work uses substantially. Programming language theory and formal methods (Sections 3.1 and 3.4) provide foundation through the connection between mechanistic interpretability and the broader question of understanding computational systems.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: the relationship to AI safety (Section 5.7) is substantial — substantial portions of contemporary interpretability work are motivated by safety concerns, and substantial portions of contemporary safety work depend on interpretability progress. The relationship is substantially symbiotic and substantially tight, to the point that the boundary between mechanistic interpretability and AI safety is genuinely fuzzy. The relationship to reinforcement learning (Section 5.4) is substantial through the substantial interpretability work on RL-trained systems, particularly in the contemporary RLHF context. The relationship to classical AI (Section 5.5) is substantial through the substantial conceptual overlap — both fields concern understanding computational systems, with classical AI emphasizing systems that are interpretable by construction and mechanistic interpretability emphasizing systems that are interpretable through reverse engineering.

From outside computer science: the relationship to neuroscience is substantial historically and methodologically. Substantial portions of mechanistic interpretability methodology draws from neuroscience, and the substantial conceptual vocabulary (circuits, features, the various others) is borrowed substantially from neuroscience. The relationship is substantively contested — the question of whether artificial and biological neural networks are sufficiently similar that neuroscience methods transfer is substantially open — but the methodological influence is substantial. The relationship to cognitive science is substantial through the substantial work on what representations emerge in trained networks and how these representations relate to cognitive representations. The relationship to philosophy of mind is substantial through the substantial questions about what it means to understand a computational system.

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is on the intelligence axis, with substantial engagement on the computation axis (substantial methodological content drawn from formal verification and from causal inference), light engagement on the system axis (some substantial systems engineering for scaling interpretability tools), and moderate engagement on the data axis (substantial work on what models learn from particular data distributions).

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized mechanistic interpretability acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat neural networks as black boxes characterized only by input-output behavior.

The first is *the disposition to investigate model internals empirically*. The trained practitioner approaches trained networks as substantial empirical objects to be investigated rather than as black boxes to be evaluated only through input-output behavior. This disposition reshapes how the practitioner approaches problems: when models behave unexpectedly, the practitioner asks what internal mechanisms produced the behavior rather than only adjusting inputs to produce different outputs; when models fail, the practitioner investigates what failed internally rather than only documenting that failure occurred. The disposition is acquired through substantial practice with empirical investigation of models and through engagement with the substantial methodological literature.

The second capacity is *fluency with the standard interpretability techniques*. The trained practitioner is fluent with the major techniques: feature visualization, attention pattern analysis, activation patching, sparse autoencoder methods, the various probing approaches, and the various other techniques. The fluency includes understanding what each technique does, what claims each can support, what each cannot reveal, and what methodological care each requires. This technical fluency is essential for serious work and is acquired through substantial practice with the various techniques and through engagement with the substantial methodological literature on what these techniques reveal.

The third capacity is *the methodological discipline required for honest interpretability work*. Interpretability claims are notoriously easy to make and notoriously easy to make incorrectly, and serious interpretability work requires substantial methodological discipline: appropriate causal verification, appropriate skepticism about apparent patterns, appropriate distinction between what evidence shows and what is plausible but unverified, appropriate engagement with the substantial literature on interpretability illusions and their causes. This methodological discipline is one of the more important capacities the discipline produces, and the substantial literature documenting interpretability failures (work showing that various interpretability methods produce misleading results, work documenting cases where apparent interpretations did not survive verification) provides substantial conceptual content for developing this discipline.

The fourth capacity is *the ability to read and contribute to the contemporary interpretability literature*. The contemporary interpretability literature is substantial and rapidly developing, with substantial new techniques, substantial new findings about specific models, and substantial methodological refinements appearing on monthly timescales. The trained practitioner can read this literature productively, evaluate contributions, and contribute new work. This skill is essential for serious work in the field and is acquired through substantial practice with reading papers and through engagement with the substantial community.

The fifth capacity is *the perspective that understanding model internals matters substantively*. The trained practitioner takes seriously that the internal mechanisms of trained networks matter substantively — for reliability, for safety, for the various deployment contexts where black-box behavior characterization is inadequate. This perspective distinguishes practitioners who engage seriously with model internals from practitioners who treat models as black boxes by default. The perspective is increasingly important as foundation models are deployed in substantial consequential contexts.

The sixth capacity is *the integration of empirical investigation with conceptual development*. Mechanistic interpretability requires substantial integration of careful empirical work with substantial conceptual development — empirical findings inform what conceptual frameworks are needed, conceptual frameworks shape what empirical investigations are productive. The trained practitioner integrates these activities productively rather than treating them as separable. This integration is one of the more durable capacities the discipline produces and remains valuable as specific techniques evolve.

The seventh capacity is *appreciation for the substantial open questions of the field*. The trained practitioner takes seriously that mechanistic interpretability has substantial open questions — about whether contemporary methods scale, about whether the conceptual frameworks are adequate, about whether interpretability can keep pace with model capability development. This appreciation produces honest engagement with the field rather than overconfident claims about what interpretability has accomplished.

### How the field sees the world

Mechanistic interpretability embeds a worldview that has emerged rapidly and that is substantially distinctive among intelligence-axis subjects.

The first commitment is *to neural networks as empirically investigable artifacts*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview holds that trained neural networks are substantial computational artifacts whose internal mechanisms can be investigated empirically, in the same way that other complex systems can be investigated. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat neural networks as black boxes by definition or as too complex for productive internal investigation. The mechanistic interpretability practitioner takes a different view: substantial empirical investigation of trained networks is productive, has produced substantial findings, and will continue to produce findings. The disposition has substantial consequences for how networks are approached: rather than treating networks as opaque, the practitioner asks what mechanisms can be characterized through careful empirical work.

The second commitment is *to genuine understanding over rhetorical reassurance*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview holds that the goal of interpretability work is genuine understanding of what models do, not rhetorical reassurance that models are interpretable. This commitment runs against the substantial broader interpretability literature that produces interpretations without verification, that treats saliency maps as explanations without engaging with whether they accurately characterize model behavior, that produces post-hoc rationalizations rather than mechanistic understanding. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat interpretation claims with substantial care, demand causal verification, and recognize that producing rhetorical reassurance about interpretability is substantively different from and substantially worse than producing genuine understanding.

The third commitment is *to causal verification as substantively important*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview takes seriously that correlations between proposed mechanisms and model behavior are not adequate evidence for those mechanisms — that establishing causal structure requires substantial methodological work. This commitment shapes how practitioners approach interpretability work: with substantial use of causal interventions, with substantial skepticism about correlational claims, with substantial appreciation for the various ways correlational work can mislead. The substantial methodological development around causal verification (activation patching, causal scrubbing, the various other approaches) is the discipline's response to the substantial difficulty of establishing causal claims about model internals.

The fourth commitment is *to the empirical character of the field*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview takes seriously that what is known about model internals is substantially empirically established and that substantial portions of contemporary practice depend on empirical findings that lack adequate theoretical framework. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer theoretical understanding over empirical investigation. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat empirical investigation as primary work, develop strong empirical methodology, and recognize that the field's progress depends substantially on careful empirical work rather than only on theoretical development.

The fifth commitment is *to the substantial methodological care required*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview takes seriously that interpretability claims are notoriously easy to make incorrectly, that the substantial literature documents many cases where apparent interpretations did not survive scrutiny. This commitment produces substantial methodological discipline in serious interpretability work and substantial skepticism about claims that have not been adequately verified. The substantial methodological literature (the various papers on interpretability illusions, on the limitations of saliency methods, on the various ways interpretability work can mislead) provides substantial conceptual content for developing this discipline.

The sixth commitment is *to interpretability as substantively connected to safety and reliability*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview holds that understanding model internals matters substantively for substantial deployment contexts — for reliability, for safety, for the various contexts where understanding model behavior is required for responsible deployment. This commitment connects mechanistic interpretability to the broader AI safety effort and to the substantial deployment concerns of contemporary foundation models. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat interpretability work as substantially consequential rather than as purely intellectual exercise.

The seventh commitment is *to appropriate humility about what is known and what is not*. The mechanistic interpretability worldview takes seriously that substantial portions of model internals remain poorly understood, that contemporary methods have substantial limitations, that interpretability has not kept pace with model capability development. This commitment runs against marketing-influenced claims that exaggerate what interpretability has accomplished. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment communicate honestly about what interpretability work has and has not established, and contribute to the development of methods rather than treating contemporary methods as adequate.

These seven commitments — to empirical investigability, to genuine understanding, to causal verification, to the empirical character, to substantial methodological care, to safety-relevance, and to appropriate humility — distinguish the mechanistic interpretability worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in interpretability as serious empirical practice rather than as production of rhetorical reassurance.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning mechanistic interpretability are characteristic and substantially well-documented, with several specific to the discipline's substantial empirical character and substantial methodological challenges.

The most common pitfall is *the saliency-maps-as-interpretation misconception*. Substantial portions of broader interpretability literature focus on saliency maps and attention visualizations as primary techniques, and learners can come to treat these as adequate interpretation methods. The substantial mechanistic interpretability literature has documented that saliency methods often fail to faithfully represent what models compute, that they can produce misleading results across substantial circumstances, that they are often inadequate as explanations of model behavior. The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial methodological literature on saliency limitations (the various papers documenting saliency failures), to recognize that mechanistic interpretability requires substantially more than saliency-style visualization, and to engage with the substantial mechanistic methods that go beyond saliency.

A second pitfall is *the interpretability-illusions trap*. The substantial literature documents many cases where apparent interpretations did not survive verification — where mechanisms that appeared to explain model behavior turned out to be incorrect characterizations, where features that appeared interpretable turned out to encode something different from what was claimed, where circuits that appeared to compute specific functions turned out to be implementing different computations. Learners who do not engage with this literature can produce interpretation work that suffers from similar illusions. The countermeasure is explicit attention to interpretability illusions: studying the substantial documented cases, engaging with the methodological literature on what causes illusions, developing the methodological discipline to avoid them in one's own work.

A third pitfall is *the cherry-picking problem*. Mechanistic interpretability work often involves identifying specific examples or specific cases where particular mechanisms appear to operate, and learners can come to focus exclusively on cases that support claimed interpretations while neglecting cases that contradict them. The result is substantial overstatement of what interpretations establish — interpretations that work for cherry-picked examples may not generalize to broader behavior. The countermeasure is systematic evaluation: testing interpretations across substantial samples of model behavior, looking explicitly for cases where claimed mechanisms should but do not operate, treating apparent confirmations with substantial skepticism until systematic evaluation establishes generalization.

A fourth pitfall is *the toy-model overgeneralization problem*. Substantial mechanistic interpretability work operates on small models — toy transformers, small CNNs, the various manageably-sized models that admit substantial empirical investigation. Findings on these small models do not always transfer to substantial production models, and learners can come to overestimate how much small-model findings reveal about large-model behavior. The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial work specifically on large models (the various Anthropic interpretability work on substantial Claude models, the various academic work on substantial open-weight models), and to maintain humility about how much small-model work transfers.

A fifth pitfall is *the framework-confusion problem*. Mechanistic interpretability draws substantial vocabulary and methodology from neuroscience, and learners can come to assume that this vocabulary maps reliably onto neural network mechanisms. The substantial open question of whether artificial and biological neural networks are similar enough that neuroscience concepts transfer is substantially genuine, and the various discussions in the literature engage with this question carefully. The countermeasure is to engage with the conceptual question explicitly rather than assuming that neuroscience-derived vocabulary captures neural network mechanisms accurately.

A sixth pitfall is *the verification-skipping problem*. The substantial methodological development around causal verification (activation patching, causal scrubbing, the various other approaches) is substantially demanding to apply rigorously, and learners can come to skip verification to make work tractable. The result is interpretation work that may or may not be correct — that may produce findings that do not survive scrutiny. The countermeasure is to take verification seriously as integral to interpretation work: to apply appropriate causal interventions, to engage with the substantial methodological literature on verification, to recognize that unverified interpretations are substantially weaker than verified ones.

A seventh pitfall is *the polysemanticity-blindness problem*. The substantial polysemanticity of neurons in many trained networks (where individual neurons participate in representing multiple unrelated features) is one of the substantial methodological challenges of interpretability work. Learners who do not engage with polysemanticity can produce interpretations that treat polysemantic neurons as if they had single interpretations, producing substantially misleading characterizations. The countermeasure is to engage with polysemanticity seriously: to study the substantial literature documenting it, to engage with the contemporary work (sparse autoencoders, the various dictionary learning approaches) that addresses it, to recognize that single-neuron interpretations are often inadequate and that more sophisticated approaches are required.

An eighth pitfall is *the over-claiming problem*. Mechanistic interpretability findings are sometimes presented with substantially more confidence than the underlying evidence warrants. Learners can come to make claims about what models do that go beyond what their evidence supports, particularly when working in environments that reward apparent insight over methodological rigor. The countermeasure is to maintain appropriate humility about what one's findings actually establish, to communicate honestly about uncertainty, to distinguish between what the evidence directly shows and what is plausible but unverified.

A ninth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied specifically to mechanistic interpretability. Modern AI tools can produce substantial code for interpretability experiments and can guide learners through standard exercises. The cost is substantial in interpretability work because the discipline depends substantially on developing intuition through hands-on engagement with model internals — an intuition that develops through substantial empirical work that LLM shortcuts bypass. Furthermore, the substantial methodological care required for interpretability work depends on internalized appreciation for the various ways interpretation can mislead, an appreciation that develops through experience rather than through reading. The countermeasure is to do early interpretability work with substantial direct engagement, even when LLM assistance could produce working code, recognizing that the development of interpretability intuition substantially requires hands-on practice.

A tenth pitfall, increasingly relevant, is *the field-fragmentation problem*. Mechanistic interpretability has substantial overlap with broader interpretability work (saliency methods, attention visualization, the various other techniques), with explainable AI work, with the various broader efforts at making AI systems understandable. The substantial field has produced substantial conflation of these distinct subfields, and learners can come to confuse mechanistic interpretability with the broader and methodologically weaker interpretability literature. The countermeasure is to engage explicitly with what distinguishes mechanistic interpretability from the broader literature: the substantial methodological care, the substantial focus on causal verification, the substantial commitment to genuine understanding rather than rhetorical reassurance.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on mechanistic interpretability is unusually diverse — substantial portions exist as Distill articles, blog posts from major AI labs, arXiv papers that have not gone through traditional peer review, and various other non-traditional venues. The discipline has emerged primarily through these non-traditional channels, and engagement with the field requires substantial fluency with this diverse literature.

#### Canonical entry points

The canonical introduction to mechanistic interpretability is the substantial body of Distill articles produced primarily between 2017 and 2020. Olah et al.'s "Feature Visualization" article (2017), the various Distill articles on Circuits (2020), and the various other Distill articles constitute the foundational literature for the field. The articles are unusually clear and unusually visual, with substantial interactive content that provides pedagogically substantial introduction to the subject. The Distill publication has substantially ceased active publication, but its archive remains substantively important and freely available.

The contemporary canonical introduction is substantially the body of interpretability work from Anthropic. Elhage et al.'s "A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits" (2021) provides the foundational framework for understanding transformer interpretability. The Anthropic interpretability team's substantial subsequent work — the various papers on superposition, on monosemantic features, on the various circuit-level findings, on the various sparse autoencoder work — constitutes substantial contemporary canonical literature. The Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread (publishing on the Anthropic website) provides substantial ongoing contemporary content.

For more general introduction to interpretability concepts, Christoph Molnar's *Interpretable Machine Learning* (freely available online) provides accessible coverage of interpretability methods broadly, including substantial coverage of methods that mechanistic interpretability practitioners would consider less rigorous. The book is appropriate as breadth-first introduction with the substantial caveat that engagement with mechanistic interpretability specifically requires substantial supplementation from the more specialized literature.

For learners interested in the connection to neural network basics, the foundational interpretability literature is substantially complemented by deep learning preparation — substantial preparation in transformers, in attention, in the substantial architectural details that interpretability work substantially depends on.

The reading-order recommendation: the foundational Distill articles for accessible introduction with substantial visual content, Elhage et al.'s "Mathematical Framework" for substantial conceptual foundation specific to transformers, the substantial Anthropic interpretability publications for contemporary state of the field, with general interpretability literature (Molnar) as breadth complement.

The various tutorials and educational materials emerging from major interpretability research groups provide substantial pedagogical complement. The Neel Nanda interpretability tutorials and exercises (substantially associated with academic and Anthropic interpretability work, freely available) provide substantial hands-on entry to the practical work.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in mechanistic interpretability branches according to specialization.

*Feature analysis at depth.* For learners pursuing feature analysis, the foundational Distill Circuits work provides substantial entry. The substantial subsequent work on features in vision models (the various OpenAI Microscope work, the various academic work on visual features) extends the picture. The contemporary work on features in language models (Anthropic's substantial monosemantic features work, the various academic work on similar approaches) covers the language model side. The substantial work on sparse autoencoders (Anthropic's "Towards Monosemanticity" papers, the various academic work on sparse autoencoders, the contemporary substantial work on sparse autoencoder scaling) is increasingly central.

*Circuit analysis at depth.* For depth on circuits, the foundational Distill Circuits work in vision is supplemented by substantial subsequent work. Olsson et al.'s "In-context Learning and Induction Heads" (Anthropic, 2022) is foundational for transformer circuit work. The various papers on specific transformer circuits (the various "Indirect Object Identification" work, the various work on specific circuits in language models, the substantial subsequent academic work) extend the picture. The Garcez et al. work and the substantial subsequent work on automated circuit discovery represent contemporary direction.

*Causal verification at depth.* For depth on methodology, the substantial work on activation patching (Meng et al.'s "Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT" and the various subsequent work) is foundational. The Chan et al. "Causal Scrubbing" work provides substantial methodological development. The various papers on the various forms of causal intervention extend the picture.

*Sparse autoencoder methodology.* The contemporary substantial work on sparse autoencoders has produced substantial literature. Anthropic's "Towards Monosemanticity" (Bricken et al.) and the substantial subsequent "Scaling Monosemanticity" (Templeton et al.) papers are foundational. The substantial academic work on sparse autoencoders extends the picture. The various papers on specific sparse autoencoder techniques (the various papers on TopK SAEs, on Gated SAEs, on the various refinements) constitute contemporary methodological literature.

*Behavioral interpretability and probing.* The substantial work on probing — training auxiliary classifiers on internal model activations to test what information is represented — constitutes a distinct interpretability tradition with substantial overlap with mechanistic work. Hewitt and Liang's "Designing and Interpreting Probes with Control Tasks" provides substantial methodological foundation. The various papers on probing in language models (the various BERT probing work, the various subsequent work) extend the picture. The methodological challenges of probing (whether probes reveal what models actually use or what is merely available in representations) have produced substantial literature in their own right.

*Polysemanticity and superposition.* The substantial conceptual problem of polysemanticity — that individual neurons participate in representing multiple unrelated features — has produced substantial literature. Elhage et al.'s "Toy Models of Superposition" (Anthropic, 2022) is foundational for the contemporary understanding. The substantial subsequent work on superposition extends the picture. The various papers on what causes superposition and how to address it constitute substantial contemporary research.

*Adversarial and robustness perspective.* The substantial work on adversarial examples and on the relationship between interpretability and robustness provides perspective on interpretability work. The various papers on whether interpretability methods reveal what models actually use to make decisions extend this perspective. The substantial intersection between adversarial robustness and interpretability is substantial active research area.

*Vision interpretability beyond Distill.* The post-Distill work on vision model interpretability has continued substantially. The various OpenAI Microscope work, the various academic work on visual feature interpretation, the contemporary work on multimodal model interpretability extend the foundational vision interpretability tradition. The substantial work on interpreting CLIP and similar vision-language models is increasingly active.

*Language model interpretability beyond circuits.* Substantial language model interpretability work goes beyond circuit analysis. The various work on what language models know about syntax and semantics, the various work on factual knowledge representation, the various work on reasoning representations — all constitute substantial language model interpretability literature. The substantial contemporary work on representations of values and beliefs in models is increasingly active and substantially intersects with safety concerns.

*Theoretical interpretability.* The substantial work attempting to develop theoretical frameworks for interpretability includes the various work on the geometry of representations, the various work on what kinds of features networks can and cannot learn to represent, the various work connecting interpretability to mathematical foundations. The substantial work on representation theory in this context (the various papers on the linear representation hypothesis, on the various forms of representational structure) is increasingly substantial.

*Safety-relevant interpretability.* The substantial intersection between mechanistic interpretability and AI safety produces substantial literature. The various work on detecting deceptive behavior in models, on identifying hidden goals, on the various other safety-relevant interpretability concerns substantially overlaps with the AI safety material in Section 5.7. The contemporary work on this intersection is rapidly developing.

*Evaluation and benchmarks.* The substantial methodological challenge of evaluating interpretability methods has produced substantial literature. The various papers on how to evaluate whether an interpretation is correct, the various benchmarks for interpretability methods, the various approaches to systematic evaluation constitute substantial methodological literature.

#### Reference works

The Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread serves as substantial reference work for transformer circuit interpretability.

The Distill archive serves as substantial reference for foundational visual and conceptual interpretability work.

The various papers from major interpretability research groups (Anthropic, the various academic groups including those at MIT, Stanford, Cambridge, the various others) constitute substantial reference for contemporary research.

The various interpretability-focused libraries (TransformerLens by Neel Nanda, the various others) constitute reference for the practical tools that practitioners use.

The Christoph Molnar interpretable machine learning book serves as substantial reference for the broader interpretability landscape.

The various model-specific interpretability resources (various neuron databases, the various circuit catalogs, the various model-specific tools) constitute reference for working with specific models.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within the broader interpretability landscape and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The interpretability-skeptical perspective.* Substantial work questions whether interpretability methods substantially produce genuine understanding versus producing rhetorical reassurance. Lipton's "The Mythos of Model Interpretability" and the various subsequent skeptical writings raise substantial concerns about whether interpretability claims are well-founded. The arguments are sometimes overstated but contain substantial insight that engagement with only the mainstream interpretability literature does not provide. The countermeasure for the field has substantially been the substantial methodological development around causal verification and rigorous evaluation, and engagement with the skeptical literature gives perspective on what motivates that methodological development.

*The "interpretability is fundamentally limited" position.* Various researchers argue that interpretability of substantial neural networks is fundamentally limited in ways that contemporary methods cannot address — that the substantial complexity of trained networks may exceed what humans can usefully understand even with substantial methodological development. The arguments have substantial intellectual content and provide perspective on what interpretability can and cannot accomplish. The position is contested but the questions it raises are substantively serious.

*The classical AI / neuro-symbolic perspective.* The classical AI tradition (treated in Section 5.5) emphasizes systems that are interpretable by construction — systems whose mechanisms are explicit symbolic structures that can be examined directly. From this perspective, the substantial difficulty of interpreting trained neural networks is partly self-imposed: building systems with opaque internals and then trying to understand them is harder than building systems with transparent internals from the start. The neuro-symbolic literature substantially advocates for this perspective. Engaging with this position gives perspective on what the contemporary interpretability project is doing and on whether alternative engineering approaches might be more productive.

*The simulator hypothesis perspective.* Various researchers argue that contemporary foundation models should be understood as substantially performing simulation rather than as substantially having goals or beliefs in the conventional sense. This framing has substantial implications for what mechanistic interpretability should aim at. The various writings on this perspective (substantially associated with Janus and similar writers) constitute alternative framing that engaging with provides perspective on what assumptions underlie interpretability work.

*The neuroscience-grounded perspective.* The substantial neuroscience tradition provides alternative methodology for interpretability work that contemporary mechanistic interpretability has substantially adopted but not always integrated thoroughly. The various papers on neural network interpretability from researchers with substantial neuroscience background provide perspective. The substantial differences between artificial and biological neural networks are substantial reasons for caution about transfer of methods, but the substantial similarities are reasons for substantial cross-disciplinary engagement.

*The behavioral / external perspective.* Various researchers argue that the substantial focus on internal mechanisms is partly misplaced — that what matters substantially is model behavior, that internal mechanisms are interesting only insofar as they affect behavior, that the substantial investment in mechanistic interpretability could be better directed at behavioral characterization. The arguments have substantial proponents and substantial intellectual content. The mechanistic position is that internals matter substantially because behavior in deployment may differ from behavior in evaluation in ways that internal structure can predict, but the behavioral skeptics raise genuine questions about how this prediction actually works.

#### What to skip and why

Most older saliency-method-focused books should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary mechanistic interpretability. The methodological limitations of saliency methods are substantial, and the contemporary mechanistic interpretability literature has substantially moved beyond them.

Most "explainable AI" books that conflate broader interpretability with mechanistic interpretability should be approached with caution. The fields are related but distinct, and the methodological standards differ substantially.

Books on interpretability that focus exclusively on classical machine learning methods (decision trees, linear models, the various others) without engaging with neural network interpretability should be supplemented with neural-network-specific literature for learners pursuing that subject.

Most marketing-driven "AI transparency" books should be skipped. The genre is uneven, and the substantial research literature is more reliable.

Books published before 2020 on neural network interpretability are substantially dated for contemporary practice. The field has developed substantially since then, and contemporary primary sources are more reliable.

Most online tutorials on interpretability are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained resources from major research groups, the substantial Distill archive, the substantial Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread, the substantial educational materials from researchers like Neel Nanda — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth and often perpetuates methodological shortcuts that the serious literature has identified as problematic.

Books that promise to teach "how AI thinks" or that anthropomorphize neural networks substantially should be approached with substantial skepticism. The substantial science of what models actually do internally is incomplete, and confident claims about model "thinking" that lack mechanistic grounding are typically not reliable.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary mechanistic interpretability research include conferences (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR have substantial interpretability content; the substantial workshops on interpretability at these conferences provide focused venues). The conferences proceedings substantially include interpretability work.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue. The cs.LG and cs.AI categories cover the relevant subareas.

The substantial Anthropic interpretability research is published primarily through the Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread and through the Anthropic blog. These constitute substantial ongoing contemporary content.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. Neel Nanda's substantial writing on interpretability is consistently valuable. Chris Olah's continued writing (largely through Anthropic publications) provides substantial conceptual content. The various other interpretability researcher blogs (the various academic researchers' blogs, the various practitioner blogs) provide ongoing perspective.

The various interpretability-focused YouTube content provides substantial supplementary material. The substantial conference talk recordings (NeurIPS interpretability workshop talks, the various others) provide substantial video content.

The Less Wrong AI forum and similar venues contain substantial interpretability content from researchers and practitioners, often with substantial discussion. The quality is variable but the substantial engagement of the community is valuable.

The various AI safety organization publications include substantial interpretability content. The substantial Anthropic safety publications, the various academic AI safety group publications, the various other AI safety organization publications include substantial relevant content.

The substantial monthly and weekly publications covering AI research (Import AI, the various others mentioned in earlier sections) include substantial coverage of contemporary interpretability work.
