### A note on contemporary relevance

Mechanistic interpretability is among the most contemporarily active subjects on the intelligence axis, with substantial developments substantially reshaping the discipline on timescales of months.

The most consequential contemporary development is the substantial maturation of sparse autoencoder methodology. The substantial work over 2023-2025 on training sparse autoencoders on substantial production models has produced substantial improvements in feature interpretability — substantial portions of contemporary substantial models can now be analyzed at the feature level with substantially higher resolution than previously possible. The trajectory suggests continued substantial development of sparse autoencoder methodology, with substantial implications for what interpretability work can accomplish.

The second major development is the substantial growth of interpretability research community. The substantial growth in the number of researchers working on mechanistic interpretability — across Anthropic, academic groups, and various other organizations — has produced substantial increase in research output and substantial methodological development. The trajectory suggests continued substantial growth, with substantial implications for the pace of progress.

The third development is the substantial integration of interpretability with AI safety. The substantial concerns about contemporary foundation model deployment have substantially increased the practical importance of interpretability work, with substantial portions of contemporary research motivated by safety concerns. The integration is substantial enough that the boundary between mechanistic interpretability and AI safety is genuinely fuzzy. The trajectory suggests continued substantial integration.

The fourth development is the substantial contemporary work on automated circuit discovery and on scaling interpretability. The substantial scale of contemporary models makes manual interpretation infeasible for substantial portions of model behavior, and substantial contemporary research aims at automating substantial portions of interpretation work. The trajectory suggests continued substantial automation effort, with substantial implications for whether interpretability can keep pace with model capability development.

The fifth development is the substantial growing engagement with interpretability of safety-relevant capabilities specifically. The substantial work on detecting and characterizing potentially concerning capabilities (deception, hidden goals, the various others) has produced substantial contemporary research with substantial implications. The trajectory suggests continued substantial work in this direction, particularly as foundation models continue to increase in capability.

The sixth development is the substantial maturation of methodology for distinguishing genuine interpretability from rhetorical reassurance. The substantial methodological development around causal verification, around systematic evaluation of interpretation claims, around the various forms of methodological rigor has substantially distinguished serious mechanistic interpretability from the broader interpretability literature. The trajectory suggests continued substantial methodological development.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of mechanistic interpretability is substantially robust. The core concerns — what features models represent, how features combine into circuits, how to verify interpretability claims, how to scale interpretability methods — remain as articulated in earlier work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

A final observation specific to mechanistic interpretability: the discipline is in a substantial moment of growth and substantial moment of practical importance. Practitioners pursuing this specialization should expect substantial change to continue, should maintain currency through substantial ongoing engagement with the contemporary literature, and should recognize that substantial portions of contemporary practice may look substantially different in even a few years. The substantial investment in mechanistic interpretability competence is rewarded by substantial intellectual depth and substantial impact opportunities, particularly through the substantial intersection with AI safety. The discipline is one where substantial methodological foundation transfers reliably while specific contemporary techniques evolve, and learners building durable competence should invest substantially in methodology — in causal verification, in systematic evaluation, in the substantial discipline of distinguishing genuine understanding from rhetorical reassurance — alongside the contemporary specifics.

A second observation specific to this subject: mechanistic interpretability is unusual among intelligence-axis subjects in the substantial integration with practical safety concerns. Engineering decisions in interpretability work substantially affect substantial deployment contexts, and competent engagement with the discipline requires substantial engagement with the practical implications. Practitioners who treat mechanistic interpretability as purely academic exercise substantially miss what makes the discipline contemporarily important, and the integration of empirical investigation with substantial practical concerns is one of the substantial sources of the discipline's distinctive intellectual character.

A third observation specific to this subject: among intelligence-axis subjects, mechanistic interpretability has substantially the strongest concentration of substantial research at industrial AI labs (particularly Anthropic) rather than at academic institutions. The substantial concentration reflects substantial advantages of access to substantial production models and substantial computational resources, and has substantial implications for the field's character. Practitioners should engage with the substantial industrial research substantially, while recognizing that substantial academic research provides substantial complementary perspective and that the substantial concentration of research at a small number of organizations has substantial implications for the field's development.

## 5.7 — AI Safety and Alignment

### What it is, properly

AI safety and alignment is the discipline concerned with ensuring that increasingly capable AI systems behave in ways that are beneficial, predictable, and consistent with human intentions — addressing the substantial technical, methodological, and conceptual problems that arise when computational systems become substantially powerful and substantially autonomous. The subject treats safety not as a peripheral concern to be addressed after capabilities are developed, but as a substantial intellectual and engineering problem in its own right, with characteristic technical questions (how to specify desired behavior, how to verify that systems behave as intended, how to detect emergent capabilities or behaviors that were not anticipated), characteristic methodological challenges (how to evaluate systems for safety properties before deployment, how to maintain safety as systems scale), and characteristic conceptual difficulties (how to formalize concepts like "beneficial" or "consistent with human intentions" that resist direct technical specification). The discipline has emerged primarily over the past two decades, with substantial early work appearing through the broader AI safety community in the 2000s and 2010s, substantial subsequent development through the substantial growth of academic AI safety groups and through industrial AI safety teams at major labs (Anthropic substantially constituted around safety as primary mission, OpenAI's safety team, DeepMind's safety team, the various others), and substantial contemporary growth driven by the substantial deployment of large foundation models and the corresponding substantial visibility of safety concerns.

The conventional account treats AI safety either as the practice of preventing AI systems from producing offensive content (the narrow corporate compliance framing) or as speculative concern about hypothetical superintelligent AI (the framing that some critics of AI safety adopt). Both accounts substantially misrepresent what AI safety has become as an intellectual subject. The conceptual core of contemporary AI safety is the recognition that *substantial computational systems with substantial autonomy and substantial capability present substantial novel problems that ordinary engineering practices do not address*, that *these problems include but extend substantially beyond the immediately visible concerns about content moderation*, that *the technical and conceptual challenges are genuine and substantively unsolved*, and that *engineering responsibility for substantial AI systems includes substantial engagement with these challenges rather than treating them as someone else's problem*. The discipline is neither narrowly about content filtering nor exclusively about hypothetical futures; it addresses the substantial range of problems that arise when capable AI systems are deployed in consequential contexts.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Specification problems* concern the substantial difficulty of specifying what we want AI systems to do in ways that produce the desired behavior — including the substantial work on reward specification (substantially overlapping with reinforcement learning concerns covered in Section 5.4), the various forms of value learning, the substantial work on capturing human preferences in training signals. *Robustness and reliability* concern ensuring that AI systems behave reliably across the substantial variation they encounter in deployment — including the substantial work on adversarial robustness, on distribution shift, on the various ways systems can fail when conditions differ from training. *Evaluation and capability assessment* concern the substantial work on evaluating what AI systems can do, including the substantial dangerous capability evaluation work that aims at understanding whether systems have capabilities that could enable substantial harms. *Interpretability for safety* concerns the substantial intersection with mechanistic interpretability (Section 5.6), with particular focus on identifying potentially concerning behaviors, hidden goals, or other safety-relevant model properties. *Alignment techniques* concerns the substantial body of techniques developed to align AI systems with human intentions — RLHF and its various successors, Constitutional AI and similar approaches, the various fine-tuning approaches that aim at safe behavior, the various forms of constitutional and preference-based training. *Deployment safety* concerns the substantial engineering work required to deploy AI systems safely — monitoring, the various forms of guardrails, the substantial work on appropriate use limitations, the various incident response practices. *Governance and policy* concerns the broader institutional questions about how AI development should be organized — though substantial portions of this concern are treated in Appendix D rather than in this section. *Long-term and existential safety* concerns the substantial work on safety problems that may arise with AI systems substantially more capable than current systems — including the substantial work on scalable oversight, on corrigibility, on the various theoretical approaches to maintaining alignment as systems become substantially capable.

The intellectual content of AI safety is distinctive in several ways. The discipline is unusually integrative — drawing on substantial portions of machine learning, on substantial portions of mechanistic interpretability, on substantial portions of philosophy and ethics, on substantial portions of game theory and economics, on substantial portions of organizational and policy thinking. The empirical character is substantial — substantial portions of contemporary AI safety practice depend on empirical observations about how systems behave, and the field has developed substantial methodology for evaluation. The conceptual character is also substantial — substantial portions of AI safety involve genuine conceptual difficulty about what safety means, what alignment means, what intentions are, how to formalize concepts that resist direct technical specification. The contested character is substantial — there is substantial genuine disagreement within the field about which problems are most important, about which approaches are most promising, about what assumptions are warranted, about how the field's various concerns relate to each other. The substantial diversity of views within AI safety is substantively appropriate to the subject matter and reflects the genuine difficulty of the problems rather than indicating that the field is unsettled in any pejorative sense.

The reason this account matters is that AI safety has become substantially central to contemporary AI development and deployment. The substantial deployment of foundation models in consequential contexts produces substantial demand for safety work, both from the substantial industrial AI labs themselves (where safety teams have grown substantially) and from the broader regulatory and policy environment. The discipline of AI safety as serious technical and conceptual practice differs substantially from the surface-level treatment that some discussions adopt, and the difference matters substantially for whether substantial AI deployment proceeds responsibly. The substantial open questions of the field cannot be productively engaged with through dismissal in either direction — neither through dismissing safety concerns as overblown nor through treating safety as solved by current techniques.

### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize AI safety and alignment as a discipline.

**The problem of specification.** A central technical problem of AI safety is specifying what we want AI systems to do in ways that actually produce the desired behavior. The substantial difficulty arises because human values, intentions, and preferences resist direct technical specification — what is wanted is often complex, context-dependent, and difficult to articulate completely, and any specification that captures part of what is wanted may produce systems that optimize for the captured specification while producing behaviors that violate the unstated portion. The discipline includes substantial work on this problem: the substantial work on reward specification in reinforcement learning (substantially overlapping with Section 5.4), the substantial work on value learning (learning what humans value rather than specifying it explicitly), the contemporary substantial work on RLHF and related preference-based approaches that learn from human feedback rather than from explicit specifications, the substantial work on Constitutional AI and similar approaches that use written principles to guide behavior. The intellectual content includes both technical questions (what specification methods work for what kinds of behavior, what failures various methods exhibit) and conceptual questions (what kinds of human values can be specified at all, what limitations any specification approach faces). The contemporary work on this problem is substantial and continues to develop, but the problem is substantively unsolved — current specification methods produce systems that exhibit substantial behaviors their designers did not intend.

**The problem of evaluation and capability assessment.** Determining what AI systems can and cannot do is genuinely difficult. Systems exhibit substantial capabilities on standard benchmarks but exhibit substantial failures in real applications; systems pass certain evaluations and fail closely related ones in non-obvious ways; systems may have capabilities that are not visible until specific prompting or fine-tuning elicits them. The substantial difficulty has substantial safety implications — if we cannot reliably evaluate what systems can do, we cannot reliably evaluate whether they are safe to deploy. The discipline includes substantial work on this problem: the substantial standard benchmark work (covered in Sections 5.3 and elsewhere), the substantial work on dangerous capability evaluation specifically (the substantial work from various AI safety organizations on evaluating capabilities relevant to potential harms — biological weapons, cyberweapons, the various forms of autonomous action), the substantial work on adversarial evaluation (red teaming, the various forms of stress testing), the substantial work on capability elicitation (the substantial work on how to determine what models can do given that capabilities may not be visible without specific elicitation). The contemporary work on this problem is substantial, with substantial engagement from industrial AI labs and from various government and academic evaluators. The problem is substantively unsolved — substantial methodological challenges remain, and current evaluation methodology has substantial known limitations.

**The problem of robustness and reliability.** AI systems must behave reliably across the substantial variation they encounter in deployment, including circumstances substantially different from training conditions, including circumstances designed adversarially to elicit failures, including the various forms of distribution shift that occur naturally in production. The discipline includes substantial work on robustness: the substantial adversarial robustness literature (including the foundational work on adversarial examples and the substantial subsequent development), the substantial distribution shift literature, the substantial work on out-of-distribution detection, the substantial work on uncertainty quantification, the various forms of red teaming and adversarial evaluation. The intellectual content is substantial, with substantial connection to broader machine learning robustness work and substantial specific concerns for safety-critical deployment. The substantial work on jailbreaks (the various forms of prompts that elicit undesired model behaviors) and on robustness against jailbreaks is one substantial subdiscipline of contemporary robustness work.

**The problem of scalable oversight.** As AI systems become more capable, the substantial difficulty of overseeing them becomes substantial. Human evaluators cannot directly verify the correctness of substantial portions of what capable AI systems do — for problems beyond human capability, for tasks that operate on too much information for humans to process, for situations where the substantial speed of AI operation exceeds human evaluation speed. The substantial difficulty raises foundational questions about how to maintain meaningful oversight as AI capability grows. The discipline includes substantial work on this problem: the substantial work on debate (using AI systems to argue against each other to enable human oversight of complex questions), the substantial work on iterated amplification and similar approaches, the substantial work on scalable evaluation more broadly, the substantial work on AI-assisted oversight (using AI tools to help humans evaluate AI behavior). The contemporary work on this problem is substantial, with substantial engagement from various AI safety research groups. The problem is substantively unsolved and is among the more conceptually challenging of the safety problems — substantial portions of the contemporary literature engage with the question of whether scalable oversight is achievable in principle and through what means.

**The problem of alignment as AI capabilities grow.** A substantial subset of AI safety concerns the substantial possibility that AI systems substantially more capable than current systems may be substantively more difficult to align than current systems, and that contemporary alignment techniques may not extend to substantially more capable systems. The substantial concern arises because contemporary alignment techniques (RLHF, Constitutional AI, the various others) substantially depend on humans being able to evaluate model outputs, on humans being able to provide preference data that captures what is wanted, on the substantial broader assumptions that may not hold for substantially more capable systems. The discipline includes substantial work on this problem: the substantial work on corrigibility (designing systems that remain controllable as they become more capable), the substantial work on value learning approaches that may scale better than preference-based approaches, the substantial work on inner alignment (the substantial concern about whether systems trained for one objective develop internal objectives that diverge from the training objective), the substantial work on the various approaches to making alignment robust to substantial capability increases. The work is substantively contested — there is substantial genuine disagreement about whether the concerns are justified, about which approaches are most promising, about whether the problem will become substantially harder or substantially easier as capabilities grow. The substantial diversity of views reflects genuine difficulty rather than confused thinking, and engagement with the field requires substantial engagement with multiple perspectives.

### Where it sits in the map

AI safety and alignment depends on substantial preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the deep learning material (Section 5.2): substantial preparation is required. AI safety is substantially the safety engineering of deep learning systems, and substantial fluency with neural network architectures, training procedures, and substantial deep learning practice is essential for serious safety work.

From the foundation models material (Section 5.3): substantial preparation is required for serious work on safety of contemporary foundation models, which constitutes substantial portions of contemporary safety research. Understanding pretraining, fine-tuning, deployment, and the substantial contemporary practice of foundation model engineering is essential.

From the reinforcement learning material (Section 5.4): substantial preparation is required, particularly for the substantial contemporary work on RLHF and related alignment techniques. The substantial connection between alignment work and reinforcement learning is substantial — substantial portions of contemporary alignment work uses reinforcement learning techniques, and the substantial conceptual concerns about reward specification and credit assignment apply substantially to alignment.

From the mechanistic interpretability material (Section 5.6): substantial preparation is increasingly required. The substantial intersection between AI safety and mechanistic interpretability is substantial — substantial portions of contemporary safety research depend on interpretability progress, and substantial portions of contemporary interpretability research are motivated by safety concerns. The boundary between the two fields is genuinely fuzzy.

From the machine learning foundations material (Section 5.1): the foundational mathematical and methodological preparation is required. AI safety depends substantially on the substantial methodological discipline of machine learning broadly, and substantial portions of safety work involve careful empirical evaluation that requires substantial methodological foundation.

From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation for various computational tools. The theory of computation (Section 2.3) provides foundation for understanding what is and is not computationally tractable in safety problems.

From the computation axis: the relationship to formal methods (Section 3.4) is substantial through the substantial work on formal verification of AI systems and through the substantial conceptual overlap between formal verification and the various forms of safety guarantees. The relationship to programming language theory (Section 3.1) is substantial through similar concerns. The relationship to logic in computer science (Section 3.5) is substantial through the substantial conceptual content from logic that AI safety uses.

From the systems axis: the engagement is substantial. AI safety in deployment is substantially a systems engineering problem, with substantial intersection with reliability engineering (Section 4.4) and with the substantial deployment infrastructure work covered in other systems sections.

From outside computer science: the relationships are unusually substantial. The relationship to philosophy is substantial — substantial portions of AI safety engage with substantial philosophical questions about agency, intentions, values, and the various conceptual content that resists technical specification. The relationship to ethics specifically is substantial. The relationship to game theory and economics is substantial through the substantial work on multi-agent dynamics, on principal-agent problems, on the various forms of strategic interaction that AI safety substantially involves. The relationship to political science and policy studies is substantial through the substantial governance and policy dimensions of the field. The relationship to organizational and decision sciences is substantial through the substantial work on how AI development organizations should be structured and how decisions should be made.

The position within the four axes of Chapter 1 is on the intelligence axis, with substantial engagement on the computation axis (substantial methodological content from formal methods, from theoretical analysis of various safety properties), moderate engagement on the system axis (deployment safety, the substantial systems engineering of safe AI deployment), and moderate engagement on the data axis (substantial work on training data quality, on data poisoning, on the various data-related safety concerns).

### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized AI safety and alignment acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat safety as someone else's problem or as solved by current techniques.

The first is *the disposition to think rigorously about safety*. The trained practitioner approaches AI development with substantial attention to safety considerations as integral to engineering rather than as auxiliary concern. This disposition reshapes how the practitioner approaches problems: questions about what could go wrong, about how to detect failures, about what guarantees can and cannot be provided are integrated into engineering work from the start rather than addressed retrospectively. The disposition is acquired through substantial practice with safety-focused work and through engagement with the substantial literature documenting how AI systems fail.

The second capacity is *fluency with the contemporary alignment techniques*. The trained practitioner is fluent with the major contemporary alignment approaches: RLHF and its substantial variants, Constitutional AI and similar approaches, DPO and the various direct preference optimization methods, the various other contemporary techniques. The fluency includes understanding what each approach does, what assumptions it makes, what failure modes it exhibits, what its fundamental limitations are. This technical fluency is essential for serious safety work and is acquired through substantial practice with the various approaches and through engagement with the substantial methodological literature.

The third capacity is *the methodological discipline required for serious evaluation work*. AI safety depends substantially on careful evaluation — of capabilities, of alignment, of robustness, of the various other safety-relevant properties. The trained practitioner has substantial methodological discipline: appropriate experimental design, appropriate baselines, appropriate skepticism about apparent successes, appropriate engagement with the substantial literature on evaluation pitfalls. This methodological discipline is one of the more important capacities the discipline produces, and the substantial literature on evaluation methodology provides the conceptual content for developing it.

The fourth capacity is *appreciation for the substantial open questions of the field*. The trained practitioner takes seriously that AI safety has substantial open questions — that current alignment techniques have substantial known limitations, that scalable oversight is substantively unsolved, that substantial portions of the field's concerns may become substantially more difficult as AI capability grows. This appreciation produces honest engagement with the field rather than overconfident claims that current techniques solve the substantial problems.

The fifth capacity is *the integration of technical and conceptual thinking*. AI safety substantially requires integration of substantial technical work with substantial conceptual work — empirical findings inform what conceptual frameworks are needed, conceptual frameworks shape what empirical investigations are productive, technical solutions depend on conceptual clarity about what they are solving. The trained practitioner integrates these activities productively rather than treating them as separable. This integration is substantively important and is one of the more durable capacities the discipline produces.

The sixth capacity is *the ability to engage with contested questions productively*. AI safety has substantial genuine disagreement within the field — about which problems are most important, about which approaches are most promising, about what assumptions are warranted. The trained practitioner can engage with this disagreement productively: understanding the substantial arguments on multiple sides, evaluating evidence honestly, holding views provisionally rather than dogmatically, contributing to the substantial work without requiring premature resolution of substantively difficult questions. This capacity is unusual in CS subjects and reflects the substantively contested character of the field.

The seventh capacity is *appreciation for the substantial broader context*. The trained practitioner takes seriously the substantial broader context within which AI safety operates — the substantial economic incentives, the substantial regulatory environment, the substantial public concerns, the substantial broader societal implications of AI deployment. This appreciation distinguishes practitioners who can do substantial safety work from practitioners limited to narrow technical concerns, and is increasingly important as AI safety becomes substantially intertwined with substantial broader societal questions.

The eighth capacity is *the disposition to take responsibility*. The trained practitioner recognizes that engineering substantial AI systems involves substantial responsibility, that the substantial deployment of substantial AI systems has substantial consequences, that engineering decisions matter substantively. This disposition runs against the engineering culture that treats safety as someone else's problem and produces engineering substantially more thoughtful than the alternative. The disposition is acquired through substantial engagement with the substantial literature on AI safety and through substantial engagement with the substantial broader context.

### How the field sees the world

AI safety and alignment embeds a worldview that is substantially distinctive among intelligence-axis subjects and that has substantially shaped contemporary AI development.

The first commitment is *to safety as substantively important rather than peripheral*. The AI safety worldview holds that ensuring AI systems behave beneficially is a substantial intellectual and engineering problem in its own right, not a peripheral concern to be addressed after capabilities are developed. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prioritize capability development and treat safety as auxiliary. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat safety considerations as integral to engineering work, integrate safety thinking from the start of projects rather than at the end, and recognize that the substantial deployment of substantial AI systems substantially changes what counts as responsible engineering.

The second commitment is *to honest engagement with substantial uncertainty*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that the field operates with substantial uncertainty — about what AI systems will become, about how they will fail, about what techniques will work, about how the various concerns will develop. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer confident answers. The AI safety practitioner takes a different view: substantial portions of the field's substantial questions are substantively uncertain, and pretending to certainty that does not exist is substantively worse than acknowledging uncertainty honestly. The disposition produces engineering and research that engages with uncertainty productively rather than dismissing it.

The third commitment is *to taking failure modes seriously*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that AI systems can fail in substantial and substantially varied ways, that documenting and understanding these failures is substantial work, and that designing for failure modes is integral to engineering responsible AI systems. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that focus exclusively on capability and treat failures as edge cases. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment study failure modes substantively, design systems that handle them gracefully, and recognize that the substantial difference between systems built with and without serious failure-mode engineering is substantial.

The fourth commitment is *to evaluation as substantively important*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that determining what AI systems can and cannot do is genuinely difficult, that current evaluation methodology has substantial known limitations, and that substantial methodological development is required to evaluate AI systems adequately. This commitment shapes how practitioners approach safety work: with substantial methodological care, with substantial development of new evaluation methods, with substantial recognition that evaluation is integral to safety rather than separate from it.

The fifth commitment is *to substantial broader engagement*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that AI development has substantial broader implications — economic, political, social, ethical — and that engineering responsibility includes substantial engagement with these broader concerns. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat broader implications as outside engineering scope. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with the substantial broader context, recognize that engineering decisions have substantial implications beyond technical performance, and treat the integration of technical and broader concerns as integral to responsible AI work.

The sixth commitment is *to honest engagement with disagreement*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that the field has substantial genuine disagreement about substantively important questions, that this disagreement reflects substantive difficulty rather than confusion, and that engagement with the field requires engagement with multiple perspectives. This commitment runs against tendencies to dismiss perspectives one disagrees with as unserious. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with the substantial diversity of views productively, evaluate arguments on their merits, and contribute to the substantial work without requiring premature resolution of substantively difficult questions.

The seventh commitment is *to taking the long term seriously*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that AI development has substantial long-term implications, that decisions made now affect substantial future trajectories, and that engineering responsibility extends beyond the substantial immediate concerns to the substantial longer-term consequences. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that focus exclusively on immediate concerns. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with the substantial long-term concerns of the field — including the substantial questions about how AI development should proceed, about how various capability advances should be approached, about the substantial questions of who should make various decisions — as integral to safety work rather than as separate concerns.

The eighth commitment is *to the substantial possibility that the field's central problems are substantively difficult*. The AI safety worldview takes seriously that the substantial central problems of the field — specification, robustness, scalable oversight, alignment as capabilities grow — may be substantively difficult and may not be solved by incremental improvement of current techniques. This commitment runs against complacent views that treat current alignment work as solving the substantial problems. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment recognize the substantial difficulty of the problems, engage with the substantial work on possible solutions, and contribute to the development of substantially new approaches rather than treating the substantial work as essentially complete.

These eight commitments — to safety as substantive, to honest engagement with uncertainty, to taking failure modes seriously, to evaluation as substantive, to substantial broader engagement, to honest engagement with disagreement, to taking the long term seriously, and to substantive difficulty of central problems — distinguish the AI safety worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in AI safety as serious intellectual and engineering practice rather than as compliance theater or speculation.

### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning AI safety and alignment are characteristic and substantially distinctive — the field's substantial integration of technical and conceptual concerns, its substantial diversity of views, and its substantial broader implications produce many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the safety-as-content-moderation misconception*. Substantial portions of public discussion of AI safety treat the field as essentially concerned with preventing AI systems from producing offensive content, missing the substantial broader scope of safety concerns. Learners can come to treat safety as essentially a content filtering problem and miss the substantial substantive content of the field. The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial AI safety research literature: to study the substantial work on alignment, on capability evaluation, on robustness, on scalable oversight, on the various other substantial topics that constitute AI safety as serious technical and conceptual practice. The substantial difference between content moderation and AI safety as research discipline is substantial, and engagement with the research literature establishes the difference.

A second pitfall is *the safety-as-existential-risk-only misconception*. The opposite pattern — treating AI safety as exclusively concerned with hypothetical existential risks from substantially future AI systems, missing the substantial work on contemporary deployment safety, alignment of current systems, and the various near-term concerns — is also common. Learners can come to dismiss AI safety as speculative or, conversely, can come to engage exclusively with long-term concerns while neglecting the substantial near-term work. The countermeasure is to recognize that AI safety covers the substantial spectrum from immediate deployment concerns through medium-term alignment problems through longer-term considerations, and to engage with the substantial breadth of work rather than focusing on either extreme exclusively.

A third pitfall is *the alignment-as-solved misconception*. Substantial portions of contemporary practice involve standard alignment techniques (RLHF, Constitutional AI, the various others), and learners can come to treat these techniques as solving the alignment problem. In practice, contemporary alignment techniques have substantial known limitations: they depend substantially on the quality of training feedback, they produce systems aligned to specific preferences expressed in training data rather than to broader notions of correctness, they have various documented failure modes (sycophancy, the various forms of mode collapse, the various others). The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial literature on the limitations of contemporary alignment: to study the various papers documenting failure modes, to engage with the substantial work on what current techniques accomplish and what they do not, to recognize that substantial open problems remain.

A fourth pitfall is *the dismissal-of-disagreement problem*. AI safety has substantial genuine disagreement, and learners can come to engage with the field through one perspective while dismissing others as unserious. The pattern is substantially harmful: substantial portions of the field's substantial questions are substantively contested, and engagement requires substantial engagement with multiple perspectives. The countermeasure is to engage with the substantial diversity of views productively: to read substantial work from researchers with different views, to evaluate arguments on their merits, to recognize that disagreement reflects substantive difficulty rather than confusion. Engaging with views one is initially skeptical of is substantively important for productive engagement with the field.

A fifth pitfall is *the security-mindset-without-application problem*. Substantial AI safety work draws substantial inspiration from security engineering, where the discipline of thinking adversarially about how systems can fail is substantial. Learners can come to adopt the rhetoric of security thinking without applying it productively to AI safety problems — producing work that uses security vocabulary without producing the substantial methodological discipline that security thinking actually requires. The countermeasure is to engage with security thinking as substantive practice: to study the substantial security literature, to apply adversarial thinking systematically to AI safety problems, to recognize that the rhetoric of security is no substitute for the substantial methodological work.

A sixth pitfall is *the political-position-driven engagement problem*. AI safety has become substantially associated with various political and ideological positions, and learners can come to engage with the field primarily through political lens rather than through engagement with substantive technical and conceptual content. The pattern is substantially harmful: substantial portions of the field's substantial work are substantively distinct from any political position, and engagement filtered through political affiliation substantially distorts what is learned. The countermeasure is to engage with the technical and conceptual content directly: to study the substantial research literature, to evaluate arguments on technical and conceptual merits, to recognize that AI safety is substantively a research discipline rather than a political movement, even though substantial broader political and policy concerns substantially intersect with it.

A seventh pitfall is *the empirical-without-conceptual problem*. AI safety substantially requires integration of empirical and conceptual work — empirical findings without conceptual grounding produce limited understanding, conceptual work without empirical grounding produces limited engagement with what AI systems actually do. Learners can come to focus on one to the exclusion of the other, producing work that is substantively limited. The countermeasure is to take the integration seriously: to engage with substantial empirical work on how AI systems behave, to engage with substantial conceptual work on what alignment and safety mean, to recognize that productive AI safety work substantially requires both.

An eighth pitfall is *the institutional-only or critic-only engagement problem*. AI safety has substantial work happening at industrial AI labs (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, the various others) and substantial work happening outside these labs (academic groups, nonprofit research organizations, the various others). Learners can come to engage exclusively with one side or the other, missing substantial work and substantial perspective. The countermeasure is to engage with both: to read industrial AI lab safety publications substantively, to read external critical perspectives substantively, to recognize that productive engagement requires substantial engagement with both.

A ninth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied to AI safety. Modern AI tools can produce substantial code for safety-relevant experiments, can answer many AI safety questions, and can guide learners through standard topics. The cost is unusually high in AI safety because the discipline depends substantially on developing judgment about substantively contested questions, on substantial methodological discipline, on substantial engagement with substantive uncertainty — capacities that develop through substantial direct engagement that LLM shortcuts bypass. Furthermore, LLMs may have substantial blind spots about safety topics specifically, including potentially having been trained or fine-tuned in ways that affect what they say about AI safety. The countermeasure is to engage with primary sources directly, to develop substantive judgment through engagement with multiple perspectives, to use AI tools as supplements to direct engagement rather than as replacements.

A tenth pitfall is *the over-confidence-about-the-future problem*. AI safety substantially involves reasoning about future trajectories of AI development, and learners can come to develop confident views about how AI will develop without recognizing the substantial uncertainty involved. Confident views about future AI capabilities, about which approaches will work, about which concerns will become important — held without appropriate epistemic humility — substantially distort engagement with the field. The countermeasure is to maintain appropriate humility about the future: to engage with the substantial uncertainty seriously, to recognize that confident predictions about future AI development have a substantial track record of being wrong, to hold views provisionally and update them in response to evidence.

An eleventh pitfall is *the under-engagement-with-broader-implications problem*. AI safety substantially involves engagement with substantial broader implications — economic, political, ethical, social — and learners can come to focus exclusively on technical concerns while neglecting these broader dimensions. The countermeasure is to engage with the broader context: to read the substantial literature on AI policy, on AI ethics, on the substantial broader implications of AI development. The integration of technical and broader concerns is integral to AI safety as discipline, and substantial work suffers from focusing on either technical or broader concerns to the exclusion of the other.

### The literature of the subject

The literature on AI safety and alignment has substantially diverse character. Substantial portions exist as research papers from industrial AI labs and academic groups, substantial portions exist as longer-form writing from various AI safety organizations and individual researchers, substantial portions exist as policy documents and reports from various organizations engaging with broader AI policy, substantial portions exist as ongoing online discussion in various venues. The field has emerged in part through non-traditional channels (the LessWrong forum, the Alignment Forum, various blogs and online publications) alongside traditional academic venues, and engagement with the field requires substantial fluency with this diverse literature.

#### Canonical entry points

A canonical introduction to AI safety as a unified discipline does not yet substantially exist in book form — the field is sufficiently rapidly developing that book-length introductions become outdated quickly, and substantial portions of the most important contemporary work exists in more rapidly publishable formats. Several substantial efforts at synthesis have appeared, with substantial limitations.

For an accessible introduction to many AI safety concerns, Russell's *Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control* (2019) provides substantial readable coverage of substantial portions of the alignment problem from a computer science perspective. The book is somewhat dated for contemporary work but provides substantial conceptual foundation. Christian's *The Alignment Problem: Machine Learning and Human Values* (2020) provides substantial complementary coverage with more focus on the substantial development of contemporary alignment work.

For more technical introduction, Ngo, Chan, and Mindermann's "The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective" (2022) provides substantial technical introduction to alignment specifically from a contemporary deep learning perspective. The Anthropic "Core Views on AI Safety" essay and the various Anthropic publications on safety strategy provide substantial perspective from one major AI safety organization. The various Concrete Problems in AI Safety paper (Amodei et al., 2016) provides substantial early articulation of specific technical safety concerns; though the paper is now older, substantial portions of its framing remain relevant.

For AI safety from the perspective of the longer-term and existential safety concerns, Bostrom's *Superintelligence* (2014) is the substantial canonical work, though it has substantial limitations from its publication date and from its specific framing. The substantial subsequent work has extended and in some respects substantially revised the framing. Karnofsky's various writings on AI safety strategy provide more recent perspective on these concerns. Yudkowsky's various writings (substantially associated with the Machine Intelligence Research Institute) provide perspective on a particularly substantial concern about alignment difficulty.

For the substantial intersection of AI safety with contemporary deep learning, the substantial body of papers from Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, and various academic groups constitutes the contemporary canonical literature. The various RLHF papers, the Constitutional AI paper, the various papers on alignment-focused fine-tuning, the various papers on dangerous capability evaluation — all constitute substantial contemporary canonical literature.

The reading-order recommendation: an accessible introduction (Russell or Christian) for substantial conceptual foundation, the technical alignment-from-deep-learning perspective (Ngo et al., the various Anthropic publications) for contemporary technical state of the field, with engagement with multiple perspectives (academic safety researchers, industrial lab perspectives, external critics) substantially required throughout. The Concrete Problems paper as foundational technical articulation. The various courses and educational materials emerging from major AI safety organizations and academic groups provide substantial pedagogical complement.

The Stanford CS 25 (covering transformers) and various AI safety-focused courses (AISES, the various others) provide substantial pedagogical resources. The 80,000 Hours research on AI safety as a career provides substantial broader context for those considering substantial engagement with the field.

#### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in AI safety branches according to specialization.

*Alignment techniques at depth.* For learners pursuing alignment techniques specifically, the substantial RLHF literature is foundational. The InstructGPT paper (Ouyang et al. 2022), the Constitutional AI paper (Bai et al. 2022), the DPO paper (Rafailov et al. 2023) and the substantial subsequent variants constitute the contemporary canonical literature. The various papers on the limitations of RLHF (Casper et al.'s "Open Problems and Fundamental Limitations of RLHF" and the various subsequent work) constitute substantial methodological literature. The contemporary work on alternative approaches to alignment (the various papers on debate, on iterated amplification, on various forms of scalable oversight) extends the picture.

*Evaluation and capability assessment at depth.* For learners pursuing evaluation work, the substantial literature on dangerous capability evaluation is increasingly substantial. The various papers and reports from Apollo Research, METR (formerly ARC Evals), and similar organizations document the contemporary practice. The various papers on specific capability categories (cyber capabilities, biological capabilities, the various forms of autonomous action) extend the picture. The substantial methodological literature on evaluation reliability, on capability elicitation, on the various methodological challenges constitutes substantial contemporary research.

*Robustness at depth.* The substantial adversarial robustness literature is foundational. The Goodfellow et al. adversarial examples papers and the substantial subsequent work constitute the foundational literature. The substantial contemporary work on jailbreaks, on adversarial attacks against language models specifically, on the various forms of robustness for foundation models constitutes contemporary literature. The various papers on certified robustness, on formal verification of robustness properties, on the various theoretical frameworks for robustness extend the technical depth.

*Scalable oversight at depth.* The substantial work on scalable oversight is substantively challenging and substantially research-active. The Christiano et al. debate paper and the substantial subsequent debate work constitute substantial foundational literature. The various papers on iterated amplification (substantial Christiano work and the various subsequent work) extend the picture. The contemporary work on AI-assisted evaluation, on weak-to-strong generalization (the substantial OpenAI paper and the various subsequent work), on the various other approaches to scalable oversight constitutes substantial contemporary research.

*Inner alignment and mesa-optimization.* The substantial concern about inner alignment — the question of whether systems trained for one objective develop internal objectives that diverge from the training objective — has produced substantial conceptual literature. The Hubinger et al. "Risks from Learned Optimization" paper (2019) is foundational. The substantial subsequent work on mesa-optimization, on deceptive alignment, on the various conceptual frameworks for thinking about inner alignment constitutes substantial literature. The contemporary work on detecting and characterizing potentially concerning model properties (substantial portions of the mechanistic interpretability work in Section 5.6) substantially intersects with this concern.

*Long-term safety considerations.* The substantial work on long-term and existential safety constitutes a substantial subdiscipline. Bostrom's *Superintelligence* and the substantial subsequent work, the various MIRI publications, the substantial portions of the Open Philanthropy AI safety publications, the various EA-affiliated AI safety publications constitute the literature. The substantial contemporary work has substantially refined and in some cases substantially revised the earlier framings, and engagement requires substantial engagement with both earlier and contemporary work.

*AI governance and policy.* The substantial literature on AI governance and policy is substantially distinct from but substantially intersects with technical AI safety. The various Frontier Model Forum publications, the various government reports (the EU AI Act and surrounding documentation, the various US executive orders and proposed legislation, the substantial UK AI Safety Institute publications, the substantial NIST publications), the various academic governance work — all constitute the contemporary literature. Substantial portions of this material is treated more substantially in Appendix D.

*AI safety in deployment.* The substantial work on deployment safety covers monitoring, guardrails, incident response, and the various engineering practices for deploying AI systems safely. The various industrial AI lab publications on deployment practices, the various papers on specific deployment concerns (jailbreaks and responses, the various forms of misuse), the substantial work on responsible AI deployment more broadly constitute the literature.

*Critical perspectives on AI safety.* The substantial critical literature on AI safety provides essential alternative perspective. The various papers from researchers with substantial concerns about how AI safety is currently practiced (concerns about the substantial industrial concentration of AI safety research, concerns about specific framings, concerns about resource allocation, the various others) constitute substantial heterodox literature. Engaging with these perspectives is integral to substantive engagement with the field.

*Specific safety topics.* AI safety has substantial specific subdisciplines that deserve targeted engagement: prompt injection and adversarial attacks, content moderation in deployment, the various forms of bias and fairness concerns, the substantial work on AI agents and their specific safety challenges. Each has substantial literature.

*Cooperative AI and multi-agent safety.* The substantial work on safety in multi-agent contexts, on cooperation among AI systems, on the various game-theoretic safety concerns constitutes a distinct subdiscipline. The various papers on cooperative AI from Cooperative AI Foundation and similar organizations constitute the literature.

*AI safety from foundation labs.* The substantial publications from major AI safety organizations and teams within industrial AI labs constitute substantial canonical literature. The Anthropic safety publications, the OpenAI safety publications, the DeepMind safety publications all constitute substantial contemporary literature with substantial perspective on contemporary safety practice.

#### Reference works

The Anthropic Core Views on AI Safety and similar position documents from major AI safety organizations serve as substantial reference for understanding organizational perspectives on the field.

The various AI safety research agendas (the substantial Anthropic technical safety agenda, the various academic safety agenda documents, the substantial DeepMind safety agenda) constitute substantial reference for what specific organizations are working on and why.

The Alignment Forum and LessWrong serve as substantial reference for ongoing discussion of AI safety topics, with substantial diversity of perspectives represented.

The 80,000 Hours research on AI safety provides substantial reference for the broader landscape of AI safety as career and field.

The various industrial AI lab transparency reports and system cards constitute substantial reference for how specific systems have been evaluated and deployed.

The various government reports and policy documents (EU AI Act documentation, the various US documents, the UK AI Safety Institute publications) constitute substantial reference for the policy dimension.

#### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within AI safety and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective on AI safety.* Substantial work questions whether AI safety as currently practiced is appropriately focused, whether substantial concerns are warranted, whether the substantial resources directed at AI safety are appropriately allocated. The various critical writings from researchers with substantial concerns about the field's framing or practices constitute substantial heterodox literature. The arguments are sometimes overstated but contain substantial insight that engagement with only the mainstream AI safety literature does not provide. Engagement with these perspectives gives substantial perspective on what motivates current practice and what alternatives might be considered.

*The "AI safety should focus on near-term" perspective.* Various researchers argue that AI safety should focus substantially more on near-term concerns (algorithmic bias, content moderation, the various deployed-AI concerns) and substantially less on longer-term and speculative concerns. The various papers and writings making this case constitute substantial alternative literature. Engagement with this perspective gives substantial perspective on what concerns are currently substantively important.

*The "AI safety should focus on long-term" perspective.* The opposing view — that AI safety should focus substantially more on longer-term concerns about substantially more capable AI systems and substantially less on near-term concerns — also has substantial proponents. The various MIRI publications and similar material constitute the literature. Engagement with this perspective gives substantial perspective on what concerns may become substantively important as AI develops.

*The "AI safety is oversold" perspective.* Various researchers argue that AI safety concerns have been substantially overstated, that current AI systems are substantially less concerning than safety advocates suggest, that the substantial attention to safety reflects political and economic interests rather than substantive technical concerns. Engagement with this perspective is substantively valuable, though substantial portions of the AI safety literature engage substantively with these arguments rather than dismissing them.

*The classical AI / GOFAI perspective on alignment.* The substantial classical AI tradition (treated in Section 5.5) provides alternative perspective on alignment — emphasizing systems with explicit symbolic representations of values, with substantial reasoning about consequences of actions, with substantial integration of ethical principles directly into reasoning. The argument is that purely learning-based approaches to alignment may have substantial intrinsic limitations that classical approaches address. The position is contested but provides substantial perspective.

*The technical alignment versus governance prioritization.* Substantial discussion within AI safety concerns the relative priority of technical alignment work versus governance and policy work. Various perspectives advocate for different prioritization. Engagement with this discussion gives perspective on what the field is currently doing and what it might do differently.

*The diversity-of-AI-development perspective.* Various researchers argue that contemporary AI safety substantially focuses on substantial AI development at substantial industrial labs while neglecting substantial alternative AI development approaches. The argument is that substantial portions of AI development happen outside the substantial labs that dominate contemporary AI safety research, and that safety concerns may be substantially different in these alternative contexts. The position has substantial proponents.

#### What to skip and why

Most marketing-driven books on "AI ethics" or "responsible AI" that conflate AI safety with corporate compliance frameworks should be approached with substantial caution. The genre exists and is substantially uneven, and the substantial research literature is substantially more reliable.

Most books on AI safety that predate 2020 are substantially dated for contemporary practice, with the substantial exceptions of foundational conceptual works (Bostrom, the various others) that retain substantive content despite some specifics being dated.

Books that present AI safety as a settled field with established techniques should be approached with skepticism. The substantial research literature is substantively clear that substantial open problems remain and that contemporary techniques have substantial known limitations.

Books that dismiss AI safety entirely without substantively engaging with the technical and conceptual content should be approached with skepticism. Engagement with the substantial research literature is substantially more reliable than blanket dismissals.

Most online tutorials on AI safety topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained materials from major safety organizations, the substantial educational materials from researchers like Rohin Shah, Paul Christiano, and similar figures, the substantial talks from major AI safety conferences — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without substantial depth.

Books that anthropomorphize AI systems substantially (treating contemporary models as having substantial agency, substantial preferences, substantial intentions in the sense humans have these things) without substantially grounding these claims in research evidence should be approached with substantial caution. The substantial scientific literature is substantively cautious about these claims, and confident anthropomorphizing claims that go beyond research evidence are typically unreliable.

Books that present AI safety primarily through ideological lens (advocating for specific political positions, presenting safety concerns as serving particular agendas) should be supplemented with substantial engagement with the technical research literature. The technical and conceptual content of AI safety is substantively distinct from any particular political position, and engagement filtered through ideology distorts what is learned.

#### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary AI safety research include conferences (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR have substantial safety content; the substantial workshops on AI safety, on alignment, on the various subtopics provide focused venues). The various AI-safety-specific conferences and workshops (the AAAI safety workshop, the various others) provide additional venues.

The arXiv repository is the substantial contemporary publication venue. The cs.AI, cs.LG, and cs.CY categories cover the relevant subareas.

The substantial Anthropic safety research is published primarily through Anthropic's research blog and through Anthropic's publication archive. These constitute substantial ongoing contemporary content. The substantial OpenAI safety research is published through OpenAI's research and blog. The substantial DeepMind safety research is published through DeepMind's research and blog. The various academic AI safety group publications constitute additional substantial sources.

The Alignment Forum is the substantial venue for ongoing AI safety discussion among researchers. LessWrong has substantial AI safety content alongside its broader rationalist-tradition content. Both provide substantial ongoing engagement with the field, with substantial diversity of perspectives.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. Paul Christiano's writings (across multiple venues) are consistently substantial. Eliezer Yudkowsky's writings represent a particular substantial perspective. Holden Karnofsky's writings provide substantial broader perspective. The various other AI safety researcher blogs (Rohin Shah, Ajeya Cotra, Daniel Kokotajlo, the various others) provide ongoing perspective. Importantly, engagement with researchers having substantially different perspectives is integral to productive engagement with the field.

The various AI safety organization publications constitute substantial contemporary literature. The substantial publications from MIRI, from the Center for AI Safety, from the Cooperative AI Foundation, from Apollo Research, from METR, from the substantial number of contemporary AI safety organizations, all constitute substantial contemporary content.

The various government and policy publications constitute substantial contemporary literature. The UK AI Safety Institute publications, the various US AI safety policy documents, the EU AI Act documentation, the various other policy publications constitute substantial sources.

The 80,000 Hours podcast and similar venues provide substantial interview content with major AI safety researchers. Substantial portions of these interviews contain substantial technical and conceptual content.

The various AI safety newsletters (AI Alignment Newsletter, ChinAI Newsletter for the China-specific perspective, the various others) provide curated coverage.

Conference talk recordings from major AI conferences and from AI-safety-specific events provide substantial video content.
